For more on this whole series of conversations, go here:

https://math.ucr.edu/home/baez/conversations/

John Baez (Dec 19 2024 at 21:47):

I have about 17 more months of weekly conversations left to upload. :weary:

John Baez (Dec 20 2024 at 22:51):

I put this paper on the arXiv, but it's not approved yet. Their automated system urged me to change the classification from category theory to combinatorics, which I did not do.

  • Groupoid cardinality and random permutations.

Abstract. If we treat the symmetric group SnS_nSn​ as a probability measure space where each element has measure 1/n!1/n!1/n!, then the number of cycles in a permutation becomes a random variable. The Cycle Length Lemma describes the expected values of products of these random variables. Here we categorify the Cycle Length Lemma by showing that it follows from an equivalence between groupoids.

I'm delighted that this paper is only 6 pages long. Another fun feature is that I prove a formula

∫∣F∣=∣∫F∣ \int |F| = |\int F| ∫∣F∣=∣∫F∣

where the integral sign at left is an actual integral, while that at right is the Grothendieck construction!

John Baez (Dec 24 2024 at 01:15):

I'm putting up tons of videos on YouTube. Here's another one where I do a lot of talking (skipping over plenty of more interesting ones where James dominates the conversation):

2023-09-14

https://youtu.be/kgzXzdnwlsY

Kähler differentials generalize 1-forms to an arbitrary commutative algebra A over a general commutative ring k:

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/K%C3%A4hler_differential

But there are even more general differentials that work for an arbitrary algebra A over k, used in noncommutative geometry. They are a universal object for derivations from A to (A,A)-bimodules. This universal derivation is obtained from D: A → A⊗A where D(a) = a⊗1 - 1⊗a, but the range of this D is really just I = ker(m) where m: A⊗A → A, and the universal derivation is D: A → I.

When A is commutative I is an ideal in A, and the Kähler differentials are d: I → Ω¹(A) where Ω¹(A) = I/I² and d comes from D. This is the universal derivation to a symmetric bimodule of A, i.e. one where the left and right actions of A agree.

The following are the same when k is a field: 1) finite separable extensions K of k, 2) finite extensions K of k with Ω¹(K) = 0, 3) finite-dimensional commutative algebras K over k that admit these structure of a special commutative Frobenius algebra. Algebras that admit the structure of a special Frobenius algebra are called 'separable algebras'. This is a different meaning of 'separable' than in 1), but related. All this is explained here more detail with some proofs here:

https://golem.ph.utexas.edu/category/2023/08/grothendieckgaloisbrauer_theor_2.html

https://golem.ph.utexas.edu/category/2023/09/grothendieckgaloisbrauer_theor_3.html

https://golem.ph.utexas.edu/category/2023/10/grothendieckgaloisbrauer_theor_4.html

Classification of ℤ/3 torsors over ℚ, as an example of Artin reciprocity.

For more on this whole series of conversations, go here:

https://math.ucr.edu/home/baez/conversations/

John Baez (Dec 25 2024 at 03:54):

Here's a video on Frobenius algebras:

2023-10-09

https://youtu.be/UQb8VW0ZoHk

Chat about tuning systems, especially just intonation.

The category of commutative separable algebras has special commutative Frobenius algebras as objects but algebra homomorphisms (not necessarily preserving the coalgebra structure) as morphisms, so 'separability' is being treated as a mere property of an algebra, namely the property that their exists a special Frobenius structure. We set things up this way because the only algebra homomorphisms between Frobenius algebras also preserving the coalgebra structure are isomorphisms.

The opposite of the category of separable commutative monoids in any symmetric monoidal category is cartesian - in fact a Boolean pretopos, by Carboni's work:

Aurelio Carboni, Matrices, relations, and group representations, https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/002186939190057F

John Baez, Grothendieck-Galois-Brauer Theory (Part 1), https://golem.ph.utexas.edu/category/2023/06/grothendieckgaloisbrauer_theor.html

The only tensor-invertible object in a cartesian monoidal category is the terminal object. A special commutative Frobenius monoid is 'extraspecial' if the inverse of the unit is the counit. Since the unit is a monoid homomorphism, this shows that the only extraspecial commutative Frobenius monoid is the terminal object. Nonetheless the free symmetric monoidal category on an extraspecial commutative Frobenius monoid, i.e. the prop for extraspecial commutative Frobenius monoids, is interesting: it's the category of corelations between finite sets:

Brandon Coya and Brendan Fong, Corelations are the prop for extraspecial commutative Frobenius monoids, https://arxiv.org/abs/1601.02307

The free symmetric monoidal category on a special commutative Frobenius monoid, i.e. the prop for special commutative Frobenius monoids, is the category of cospans between finite sets:

Steve Lack, Composing PROPs, http://www.tac.mta.ca/tac/volumes/13/9/13-09abs.html.

The free symmetric monoidal category on a commutative Frobenius monoid, i.e. the prop for commutative Frobenius monoids, is the category of oriented 2-dimensional cobordisms between oriented 1-dimensional compact manifolds:

Joachim Kock, Frobenius algebras and 2D topological quantum field theories (short version), https://mat.uab.cat/~kock/TQFT/FS.pdf

For more on this whole series of conversations, go here: https://math.ucr.edu/home/baez/conversations/

John Baez (Dec 28 2024 at 00:47):

I read a post on Mathstodon by Terry Tao saying he gets 1 or 2 papers rejected per year. That's a lot more than me, and my papers can't be better than his, so I decided either he writes a lot more papers and/or he submits to more prestigious journals.

I suspect both of those explanations are true. But I decided to try to get over my fear of rejection and submit some papers that I have sitting around in near-finished states.

Today I submitted this one:

  • Groupoid cardinality and random permutations.

I also want to quickly submit this one, since it's just been sitting on the arXiv since 2016:

  • Topological crystals.

Sunada's work on topological crystallography emphasizes the role of the 'maximal abelian cover' of a graph XX X. This is a covering space of XX X for which the group of deck transformations is the first homology group H1(X,Z)H_1(X,\mathbb{Z}) H1​(X,Z). An embedding of the maximal abelian cover in a vector space can serve as the pattern for a crystal: atoms are located at the vertices, while bonds lie on the edges. We prove that for any connected graph XX X without bridges, there is a canonical way to embed the maximal abelian cover of XX X into the vector space H1(X,R)H_1(X,\mathbb{R}) H1​(X,R). We call this a 'topological crystal'. Crystals of graphene and diamond are examples of this construction. We prove that any symmetry of a graph lifts to a symmetry of its topological crystal. We also compute the density of atoms in this topological crystal. We give special attention to the topological crystals coming from Platonic solids. The key technical tools are a way of decomposing the 1-chain coming from a path in XX X into manageable pieces, and the work of Bacher, de la Harpe and Nagnibeda on integral cycles and integral cuts.

But I don't know where to submit it! One option is Acta Crystallographica A, but I think my paper is too mathematical for that, even though it's the most mathematical of the various Acta Crystallographica journals, and some papers on topological crystals have been published there.

I guess I should submit to some general-purpose math journal. But now my fear of rejection kicks in: I don't want to submit to a journal that's 'too good'.

By the way, I once had a referee say that because a paper of mine had been on the arXiv for a long time, it must have gotten rejected by a bunch of previous journals. That's a really lazy form of refereeing (and it wasn't true).

John Baez (Dec 30 2024 at 06:32):

I've gotten the go-ahead to submit this paper, too:

  • Extensions of 2-rigs and the splitting principle, with Joe Moeller and Todd Trimble.

John Baez (Dec 30 2024 at 06:41):

I wrote a blog article that got me interested in plasma physics, a topic I'd always avoided:

  • The Parker Solar Probe.

It turns out that on December 24th this probe penetrated the 'Alfvén surface' of the Sun. Above this surface, the solar wind becomes supersonic, so no disturbances in its flow can affect the Sun below. So I had to learn about this and explain a bit of it.

For starters, the 'sound' here is really Alfvén waves: coupled vibrations in the plasma and the magnetic field. To compute the speed of these waves you need to understand a bit of the equations of 'magnetohydrodynamics' or 'MHD', which govern electrically conductive fluids interacting with magnetic fields. So I broke down and started studying the MHD equations.

This quickly led to another interesting subject I'd never thought about: Galilean electrodynamics! Maxwell's equations are relativistic hence invariant under the [[Lorentz group]], while the usual equations of fluid flow are non-relativistic hence invariant under the [[Galilei group]], which is the c→∞c \to \inftyc→∞ limit of the Lorentz group. If you stupidly combine these two sets of equations you get theory that's invariant under neither of these two groups! But the MHD equations are not stupid: they're invariant under the Galilei group.

And it turns out that it's because the MHD equations really use a certain c→∞c \to \inftyc→∞ limit of the Maxwell equations, called the 'magnetic limit' of Maxwell's equations, which is invariant under the Galilei group.

There's also an 'electric limit'.

All this is stuff I should have thought about a long time ago, since I like electromagnetism and the use of symmetry in physics. But I had avoided MHD.

I'm going to blog about this stuff a bit....

Todd Trimble (Dec 30 2024 at 06:44):

This sounds amazing! Galileo, you are still relevant.

John Baez (Dec 30 2024 at 06:51):

It is amazing. As someone pointed out, if Maxwell had been committed to Galilean invariance he would have been forced into a Galilean-invariant version of Maxwell's equations. But he introduced a term whereby a changing electric field creates a curl in the magnetic field, and this gives rise to light, and he computed an absolute value for the speed of light and got the right answer... thus destroying Galilean invariance and laying the groundwork for special relativity!

Maxwell was very much into thinking about these things. 3 years before Einstein was born, he wrote:

“Our whole progress up to this point may be described as a gradual development of the doctrine of relativity of all physical phenomena. Position we must evidently acknowledge to be relative, for we cannot describe the position of a body in any terms which do not express relation… There are no landmarks in space; one portion of space is exactly like every other portion, so that we cannot tell where we are. We are, as it were, on an unruffled sea, without stars, compass, sounding, wind or tide, and we cannot tell in which direction we are going. We have no log which we can case out to take a dead reckoning by; we may compute our rate of motion with respect to the neighboring bodies, but we do not know how these bodies may be moving in space.”

But he must have been talking about Galilean relativity!

Todd Trimble (Dec 30 2024 at 06:52):

I love the seafaring imagery...

John Baez (Dec 30 2024 at 06:53):

Yes! I believe Galileo too has a passage about what it would be like on a ship, below deck, on a quiet sea, and how you couldn't tell if you were moving. But it wasn't so poetic.

Cole Comfort (Dec 30 2024 at 07:40):

John Baez said:

I put this paper on the arXiv, but it's not approved yet. Their automated system urged me to change the classification from category theory to combinatorics, which I did not do.

  • Groupoid cardinality and random permutations.

Abstract. If we treat the symmetric group SnS_nSn​ as a probability measure space where each element has measure 1/n!1/n!1/n!, then the number of cycles in a permutation becomes a random variable. The Cycle Length Lemma describes the expected values of products of these random variables. Here we categorify the Cycle Length Lemma by showing that it follows from an equivalence between groupoids.

I'm delighted that this paper is only 6 pages long. Another fun feature is that I prove a formula

∫∣F∣=∣∫F∣ \int |F| = |\int F| ∫∣F∣=∣∫F∣

where the integral sign at left is an actual integral, while that at right is the Grothendieck construction!

I am enjoying reading. Reading combinatorics usually cause me more confusion. Perhaps this work is combinatorics in the sense that it is concerned with the category FinSet \sf FinSet FinSet.

This makes we wonder how much of FinSet\sf FinSet FinSet is actually needed. If you take your groups to be internal to locally compact Hausdorff spaces, then you have a more interesting Haar measures, and thus another notion of expected value. For example, consider the locally compact group Rn \mathbb{R}^n Rn with the usual topology.

If F F F is valued in locally compact Hausdorff spaces, do you think that the equation ∫∣F∣=∣∫F∣ \int | F| = |\int F| ∫∣F∣=∣∫F∣ would still hold? Or is there something about finite sets that is essential?

I ask this because I am curious if there is any connection to Bosonic Fock space in quantum mechanics, although maybe this is misguided.

David Corfield (Dec 30 2024 at 08:26):

John Baez said:

I believe Galileo too has a passage about what it would be like on a ship, below deck, on a quiet sea, and how you couldn't tell if you were moving. But it wasn't so poetic.

Yes,

Shut yourself up with some friend in the main cabin below decks on some large ship, and have with you there some flies, butterflies, and other small flying animals. Have a large bowl of water with some fish in it; hang up a bottle that empties drop by drop into a wide vessel beneath it. With the ship standing still, observe carefully how the little animals fly with equal speed to all sides of the cabin. The fish swim indifferently in all directions; the drops fall into the vessel beneath; and, in throwing something to your friend, you need to throw it no more strongly in one direction than another, the distances being equal; jumping with your feet together, you pass equal spaces in every direction. When you have observed all these things carefully (though doubtless when the ship is standing still everything must happen in this way), have the ship proceed with any speed you like, so long as the motion is uniform and not fluctuating this way and that. You will discover not the least change in all the effects named, nor could you tell from any of them whether the ship was moving or standing still. In jumping, you will pass on the floor the same spaces as before, nor will you make larger jumps toward the stern than toward the prow even though the ship is moving quite rapidly, despite the fact that during the time that you are in the air the floor under you will be going in a direction opposite to your jump. In throwing something to your companion, you will need no more force to get it to him whether he is in the direction of the bow or the stern, with yourself situated opposite. The droplets will fall as before into the vessel beneath without dropping toward the stern, although while the drops are in the air the ship runs many spans. The fish in their water will swim toward the front of their bowl with no more effort than toward the back, and will go with equal ease to bait placed anywhere around the edges of the bowl. Finally the butterflies and flies will continue their flights indifferently toward every side, nor will it ever happen that they are concentrated toward the stern, as if tired out from keeping up with the course of the ship, from which they will have been separated during long intervals by keeping themselves in the air. And if smoke is made by burning some incense, it will be seen going up in the form of a little cloud, remaining still and moving no more toward one side than the other. The cause of all these correspondences of effects is the fact that the ship's motion is common to all the things contained in it, and to the air also. That is why I said you should be below decks; for if this took place above in the open air, which would not follow the course of the ship, more or less noticeable differences would be seen in some of the effects noted.

Dialogue Concerning the Two Chief World Systems, translated by Stillman Drake, University of California Press, 1953, pp. 186 - 187 (Second Day).

John Baez (Dec 30 2024 at 18:32):

John Baez said:

I put this paper on the arXiv, but it's not approved yet. Their automated system urged me to change the classification from category theory to combinatorics, which I did not do.

It's been approved now, in the category theory classification:

https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.16386

Cole Comfort said:

I am enjoying reading. Reading combinatorics usually cause me more confusion.

I'm glad you're liking it! If you get bogged down, skip ahead to the Conclusions, which are largely self-contained once you know the definition of groupoid cardinality and action groupoid (given earlier.)

Perhaps this work is combinatorics in the sense that it is concerned with the category FinSet \sf FinSet FinSet.

It's 'enumerative combinatorics' because it's proving explicit formulas counting structures of some sort that you can put on finite sets. But it's category theory because it's deriving these from equivalences between groupoids.

If F F F is valued in locally compact Hausdorff spaces, do you think that the equation ∫∣F∣=∣∫F∣ \int | F| = |\int F| ∫∣F∣=∣∫F∣ would still hold? Or is there something about finite sets that is essential?

I know how to take the cardinality of a finite groupoid, or more generally a 'tame' groupoid, which is a very well-behaved countable coproduct of finite groupoids. What sort of thing might you be trying to define the cardinality of? A 'locally compact Hausdorff groupoid'?

Even with a compact Hausdorff group there's the issue of normalizing the Haar measure. We can normalize it so the integral over the whole group is 1, but the number 1 is not very exciting. With finite groups we can normalize it so the integral over the whole group is the cardinality of the group, and that's what I do in the theory of groupoid cardinality.

John Baez (Dec 30 2024 at 18:37):

Thanks for the Galileo quote, @David Corfield! I'll put that somewhere I can get to it. It's very clear.

John Baez (Dec 31 2024 at 05:42):

More conversations with James Dolan:

2023-11-06

https://youtu.be/07YB-ENbys8

More digressions on the mathematics of tuning systems. Possible relations between just intonation and Coxeter groups - see the series starting here:

https://johncarlosbaez.wordpress.com/2023/10/07/pythagorean-tuning/

How the PLR group acts on the Tonnetz (or 'tone net'):

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tonnetz https://alpof.wordpress.com/2014/01/26/an-introduction-to-neo-riemannian-theory-9/

Triads as a torsor of the dihedral group D₁₂:

https://math.ucr.edu/home/baez/week234.html
https://arxiv.org/abs/0711.1873

2023-11-13

https://youtu.be/eNZW_REeKY8

More on possible relations between tuning systems and Coxeter groups. Just intonation involves a group homomorphism from ℤ² to GL(1,ℝ), sending the first generator to 5/4 (a just major third) and the second to 6/5 (a just minor third). Similarly equal temperament involves a group homomorphism ℤ² to GL(1,ℝ) sending the first generator to 2^(1/3) (an equal-tempered major third) and the second to 2^(1/4) (an equal-tempered minor third).

Similar concepts led to the 'Fokker periodicity blocks':

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fokker_periodicity_block

which are related to the Tonnetz:

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tonnetz

A hexagon of triads containing a single note. How the PLR group is a quotient of the Coxeter group {∞,∞,∞} with three generators which acts as isometries on the hyperbolic plane preserving the infinite-order triangular tiling:

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Infinite-order_triangular_tiling

This group maps onto the Coxeter group {3,∞} which is the symmetry group of the infinite-order triangular tiling.

For more on this whole series of conversations, go here:

https://math.ucr.edu/home/baez/conversations/

John Baez (Jan 02 2025 at 01:54):

I wrote a bit about the Great Stagnation in fundamental physics, on Mastodon.

John Baez (Jan 02 2025 at 20:08):

I submitted 2-rig extensions and the splitting principle for publication.

Also, I wrote an explanation of the equations describing how a conductive fluid (like plasma) interacts with electromagnetic fields:

  • Magnetohydrodynamics.

The interesting part was proving some mathematical consequences of these equations:

  • magnetic diffusion (how the magnetic field tends to spread out according to an equation analogous to the heat equation)
  • magnetic pressure (how the magnetic field exerts a force on the fluid pointing in the −∇(B⋅B)-\nabla (\mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{B})−∇(B⋅B) direction).
  • magnetic tension (how curving magnetic field lines exert a force in the −(B⋅∇)B-(\mathbf{B} \cdot \nabla) \mathbf{B}−(B⋅∇)B direction).

John Baez (Jan 02 2025 at 21:18):

While trying to dig deeper and prove Alfven's theorem about how magnetic field lines get 'frozen in' to the plasma, I suddenly realized I've never seen a lowbrow vector calculus description of the Lie bracket of vector fields on R3\mathbb{R}^3R3. I think it's just

[v,w]=(v⋅∇)w−(w⋅∇)v [\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{w} ] = (\mathbf{v} \cdot \nabla) \mathbf{w} - (\mathbf{w} \cdot \nabla) \mathbf{v} [v,w]=(v⋅∇)w−(w⋅∇)v

but why have I never seen anyone mention the right-hand side?

John Baez (Jan 02 2025 at 21:20):

For example, it should obey the Jacobi identity, and you could torture college students of vector calculus by making them prove that.

John Baez (Jan 03 2025 at 03:23):

2023-11-27

https://youtu.be/AGqjsDBUzUw

Zeta functions and structure types. There is a kind of structure RLR_LRL​ we can put on finite sets, i.e. a species in Joyal's sense, such that an RLR_LRL​-structure on a finite set is a way of making it into a finite field. We can extract from this a Dirichlet series:

https://ncatlab.org/johnbaez/show/Dirichlet+species+and+the+Hasse-Weil+zeta+function

The exponential of this is the Riemann zeta function. The slice category of species over RLR_LRL​ is a Grothedieck topos: an object here is a way of making a finite set into a field and putting some further structure on it. Similarly, there's a species RL,pR_{L,p}RL,p​ such that an RL,pR_{L,p}RL,p​-structure on a finite set is a way of making it into a finite field of characteristic p. Again the slice category of species over RL,pR_{L,p}RL,p​ is a Grothendieck topos. Putting the double negation topology on this and forming the category of sheaves, we get a Boolean topos, which is the geometric theory of algebraic closures of Fp\mathbb{F}_pFp​. This topos is also the category of GGG-sets where GGG is the absolute Galois group of Fp\mathbb{F}_pFp​, namely the profinite completion of Z\mathbb{Z}Z:

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Profinite_integer

If you take a commutative ring AAA and hom it into the the algebraic closure of Fp\mathbb{F}_pFp​, you get an object in this topos. From this you can perhaps get one Euler factor of the L-series of this commutative ring (or its corresponding affine scheme).

For more on this whole series of conversations, go here:

https://math.ucr.edu/home/baez/conversations.html

[v,w]=(v⋅∇)w−(w⋅∇)v [\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{w} ] = (\mathbf{v} \cdot \nabla) \mathbf{w} - (\mathbf{w} \cdot \nabla) \mathbf{v} [v,w]=(v⋅∇)w−(w⋅∇)v

John Baez (Jan 04 2025 at 00:37):

Yes, Kelvin's circulation theorem says the vorticity (i.e. curl) of a fluid flow can also get 'frozen in' to the flow under certain conditions, but people seem to state this result by introducing an imaginary surface (or infinitesimal surface element) that flows along with the fluid, and showing the integral of the vorticity over this moving surface is constant with time. I would like to see this result stated using a Lie derivative, since the Lie derivative of w\mathbf{w}w with respect to v\mathbf{v}v says essentially how much w\mathbf{w}w changes as we let it move 'frozen in' to the flow of v\mathbf{v}v. I could be mixed up, but I'll try to figure this out.

John Baez (Jan 04 2025 at 05:42):

Excellent! The 3d Euler equation in 'Helmholtz' or 'vortex' form is what I want - but it's especially nice to see it written in terms of Lie brackets of vector fields. Where did you get this?

I realize now that when people think of the Euler equation as describing geodesic motion on the group of volume-preserving difeeomorphisms (whose Lie algebra is the Lie algebra of divergence-free vector fields), they must write down some equations like this. Yes, here:

  • D. G. Ebin and J. E. Marsden, Groups of diffeomorphisms and the solution of the classical Euler equations for a perfect fluid.

Way back in 1969!

John Baez (Jan 05 2025 at 02:16):

Another conversation with James Dolan:

2023-11-27

https://youtu.be/YLIE0wXEgZk

It would be interesting to study the double negation topology on the topos of ℕ-sets. Apparently sheaves with respect to this topology are ℤ-sets. It is also interesting to study the double negation topology on the category of Set-valued functors on the category of finite fields, or finite fields of characteristic p:

Olivia Caramello, Topological Galois theory, https://arxiv.org/abs/1301.0300

Investigating zeta functions arising from 2-rigs. Say a 2-rig is a cocomplete symmetric monoidal category enriched over abelian groups. Then any 2-rig R comes from an algebraic stack, and the groupoid of 'points' of this stack over a commutative ring k is the groupoid of 2-rig maps from R to kMod. If our stack is a mere scheme, we get a mere set of points for each commutative ring k, and then we can use this technique to define the Hasse-Weil zeta function of the scheme:

https://ncatlab.org/johnbaez/show/Dirichlet+species+and+the+Hasse-Weil+zeta+function#hasseweil_species

The idea is to define the 'Hasse-Weil species' of the scheme, a certain functor from the groupoid of finite sets to Set, and then decategorify it to get a Dirichlet series.

For a full-fledged algebraic stack we get a groupoid of points for each commutative ring k, and then instead of a Hasse-Weil species we get a Hasse-Weil 'stuff type', an analogously defined pseudofunctor from the groupoid of finite sets to Gpd. We should be able to still define a Dirichlet series from this, but this has not yet been worked out.

Every 2-rig comes from an algebraic stack, but here we consider 2-rigs coming from algebraic stacks of two different kinds: affine schemes (which are not stacky at all), and affine algebraic groups (which can be seen as very stacky algebraic stacks with just one point).

Any affine scheme gives a 2-rig as follows: the affine scheme corresponding to a commutative ring R gives the 2-rig of R-modules. We consider the cases R = ℤ[i] and R = ℤ[x].

Any affine algebraic group gives a 2-rig as follows: the affine algebraic group corresponding to a commutative Hopf algebra H gives the 2-rig of H-comodules. We consider the case where H is the commutative Hopf algebra of ℤ^G of ℤ-valued functions on a finite group G. We focus on the case G = ℤ/3.

For more on this whole series of conversations, go here:

https://math.ucr.edu/home/baez/conversations/

John Baez (Jan 08 2025 at 23:08):

A conversation with James Dolan and Chris Grossack:

2023-12-15

https://youtu.be/TbRrYwMgkEY

Categorifying various attitudes to rings, or rigs, to get corresponding attitudes to 2-rigs. A commutative algebraist studies commutative rings while an algebraic geometer might work in the opposite category and think of them as affine schemes. The algebraic side is more 'syntactical' while the geometric side is more 'semantic'.

You might think the geometric interpretation of a 2-rig is typically some sort of 'categorified affine scheme', but that's not always true! For example, if you take the 2-rig of modules of a commutative ring R, its spectrum is the same as that of R.

However, most 2-rigs aren't module categories of rings. Take a quasiprojective variety X and look at the 2-rig of quasicoherent sheaves on it, QCoh(X). When X is an affine variety QCoh(X) is equivalent to the 2-rig of modules of a ring, namely the ring R with Spec(R) ≅ X. But when X is a projective variety this is not true.

The free commutative ring on one generator is ℤ[x]. If we think of this as a space it's the line, which is an affine scheme. Similarly, the 2-rig of modules of ℤ[x] is the 2-rig of quasicoherent sheaves on the line, which is an affine scheme.

On the other hand, the free 2-rig on a line object is the 2-rig of ℤ-graded vector spaces, which is equivalent to the 2-rig of algebraic representations of the affine group scheme GL(1), or comodules of ℤ[x]. If we think of this 2-rig as a kind of 'space' it's the algebraic stack BGL(1).

Next consider the free 2-rig on a line object equipped with a line object equipped with a monomorphism to I⊕I, where I is the tensor unit. The corresponding space is the projective line P¹. For details see:

Martin Brandenburg, Tensor categorical foundations of algebraic geometry, https://arxiv.org/abs/1410.1716.

Also see Deligne's work on tensor categories, discussed here:

https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/Deligne%27s+theorem+on+tensor+categories

which shows that a large class of 2-rigs are categories of representations of supergroups, i.e. supercommutative Hopf algebras. A more general attitude toward the 'Tannakian philosophy' also considers supergroupoids (coming from 'non-neutral' Tannakian categories, as defined in the above nLab article).

For more on this whole series of conversations, go here: https://math.ucr.edu/home/baez/conversations/

John Baez (Jan 10 2025 at 01:15):

The above conversation got me interested in Jim's approach where he defines 'algebraic stacks' to be 2-rigs, by which he means symmetric monoidally cocomplete kkk-linear categories. I asked him some questions and I think the answers are pretty interesting.

I said:

You sometimes say a 2-rig is an algebraic stack.  Have you tried to make that into a theorem using some standard standard of algebraic stack, sticking on some conditions on one side or another to make it all work?  Of course another fine attitude is that 2-rigs are a better approach to algebraic stacks than the usual one, so screw the standard definition.  I can certainly live with that!

John Baez (Jan 10 2025 at 01:17):

He said:

there's published papers where stuff like this is discussed; i think there's some stuff about this by lurie and also in martin brandenburg's thesis, i think, and i've probably actually looked at both of those in particular but not necessarily worked through all of the details.  i think that the brandenburg stuff is probably very directly related to what we're discussing now whereas for the lurie stuff it might require some more care in how to translate the ideas between lurie's setting and a setting more like what i have in mind.  there's probably actually a whole bunch of published papers that are semi-relevant here, i'm just mentioning the brandenburg and lurie ones because i've had more direct contact with them.

the general theme of "galois connection" is a theme with variations that's useful in situations like this where you have two (or more....) related "concepts" x and y and you're wondering about the extent to which they're really "equivalent"; you set up "opposing" processes f for turning x-instances into y-instances and g for the opposite direction, you declare the "nice" x-instances to be the "fixed" points of fg and the "nice" y-instances to be the "fixed" points of gf, and if you do it right then you automatically/ish get an "equivalence" between the concepts of "nice x-instance" and "nice y-instance".

(there's more to say about this general theme-with-variations but for now i'll just mention how "opposing" sometimes means "suitably adjoint" and "fixed point" sometimes means "co/algebra of the idempotent co/monad of the suitable adjunction".)

roughly speaking in the context you're asking about, x specializes to the bicategory of 2-rigs, y specializes to the bicategory of pre-stacks on the site category of affine schemes, and z specializes to the bicategory of affine-algebraic groupoids (what i think you call something like "affine groupoid schemes").  in this context we're somewhat likely to regard the "nice" algebraic stacks as the ones that are coherent under all of the sensible processes for navigating within the concept-network {x,y,z}; that is, strongly fixed under those sensible processes that loop back to the same concept.  we can say more about what the sensible processes are in this context ....

(there are some parallel contexts useful for comparison purposes here, for example one where x is grothendieck toposes and z is localic groupoids, and another where y is pre-stacks on the site category of affine toric varieties ....)

that's a preliminary answer to your question ....

John Baez (Jan 10 2025 at 01:18):

I said:

there's published papers where stuff like this is discussed; i think there's some stuff about this by lurie and also in martin brandenburg's thesis, i think, and i've probably actually looked at both of those in particular but not necessarily worked through all of the details. 

Thanks.  I've looked at his thesis, but now I want to know exactly how precisely he related some more standard concept of 'algebraic stack' to some concept of 2-rig.

the general theme of "galois connection" is a theme with variations that's useful in situations like this where you have two (or more....) related "concepts" x and y and you're wondering about the extent to which they're really "equivalent"

Yes, you taught me this, though I've never thought about it more than two concepts at a time.

roughly speaking in the context you're asking about, x specializes to the bicategory of 2-rigs, y specializes to the bicategory of pre-stacks on the site category of affine schemes, and z specializes to the bicategory of affine-algebraic groupoids (what i think you call something like "affine groupoid schemes"). 

Huh!  How well, or badly, do affine groupoid schemes manage to subsume projective algebraic varieties?

John Baez (Jan 10 2025 at 01:20):

He said:

"Huh!  How well, or badly, do affine groupoid schemes manage to subsume projective algebraic varieties?"

they do it very beautifully.  at some point i developed an aesthetic that "glueing" of affine varieties into more general varieties is an ugly idea, and i still sort of feel that way.  (i can rant about that at length if necessary ,,,, this was my "theories good, glueing bad" stage ....)  however affine groupoid schemes do a beautiful job of making glueing less ugly, and in particular blending together the case where there's just a discrete family of coordinate patches with perhaps more interesting cases where the set of coordinate patches is itself an affine scheme (as well as possibly cases a bit more involved than that ....).  it's an excellent conceptual exercise to take some familiar important non-affine scheme x (for example x = p^1, p^2, flag varieties, etc) and to examine contrasting affine groupoid schemes which are all "morita-equivalent" to each other and to x, each of the contrasting but morita-equivalent affine groupoid schemes reflecting some contrasting intuitive philosophy (more glueing-oriented or more doctrinaire ....) of how to "geometrically construct" x.  there's also a nice unification of contrasting flavors of "morita-equivalence" here, i think, and in general the affine groupoid schemes act as an illuminating intermediary between the doctrinaire theories on the one hand and the glued-together pre-stacks on the other hand.

(i remember attending a seminar on algebraic stacks that ziv ran gave that probably covered this stuff, sort-of, but i didn't really get it at the time, nor until much later when i re-invented it for myself from a much more explicitly category-theoretic viewpoint ....)

i'm guessing we've probably briefly talked about this a bit at times, possibly even with chris there .... or maybe with both you and chris but separately.

so here's an example-puzzle to think about (possibly somewhat unfair as a puzzle because of possibly not explicitly supplying you with all the "necessary" definitions and so forth, but maybe you already know all that or can figure it out):

consider the usual "glueing" construction of p^1 as the union of two affine coordinate patches, one of them omitting the point at infinity and the other omitting the point at zero.  how might you construe this as an affine groupoid scheme?

(i also just realized that if you're not sure how to proceed then i have a potentially useful hint i can give you .... in fact i'm wondering now whether i should have started out with this hint at the beginning of this response ....)

John Baez (Jan 10 2025 at 01:21):

I said:

so here's an example-puzzle to think about (possibly somewhat unfair as a puzzle because of possibly not explicitly supplying you with all the "necessary" definitions and so forth, but maybe you already know all that or can figure it out):

I don't consider it an unfair puzzle.  

consider the usual "glueing" construction of p^1 as the union of two affine coordinate patches, one of them omitting the point at infinity and the other omitting the point at zero.  how might you construe this as an affine groupoid scheme?

It is surprising to me that you can do this - that's why I asked the question in the first place!  - but now that you're saying it's possible, I can imagine a way to try.

Can we define an affine groupoid scheme whose scheme of objects is the disjoint union of two affine lines, and whose scheme of morphisms has a unique isomorphism between x in the first line and y = 1/x in the second line when x and y are both nonzero... and no other isomorphisms except identities?

In this approach the gluing is done via isomorphisms rather than equations, and the idea is that "uniquely isomorphic is as good as equal".

John Baez (Jan 10 2025 at 01:24):

He said:

yes, that's the basic idea ....

the hint i was going to give is that the limits theory of a groupoid-object has a quotient theory given by the limits theory of an equivalence-relation-object, and your example (and more generally lots of examples that yield schemes rather than non-trivial stacks) is an example of an affine groupoid scheme that's really just an affine equivalence-relation scheme; the tip-off is probably where you say "and no other isomorphisms except identities".  that is, a groupoid satisfying "every automorphism is an identity morphism" is one of the ways to characterize the groupoid-objects that are essentially just equivalence-relation-objects.

thus one answer to "how do affine groupoid schemes manage to subsume projective algebraic varieties?" is via affine equivalence-relation schemes.  however there's very interesting ways to construct for example p^1 as an affine groupoid scheme via constructions with intermediate stages where some of the affine groupoid schemes are not just affine equivalence-relation schemes, and those constructions reflect different, non-glueing philosophies of geometric construction ....

It is surprising to me that you can do this - that's why I asked the question in the first place!  - but now that you're saying it's possible, I can imagine a way to try."

i learned about this rather late in my education and it came as somewhat of a minor revelation to me .... it helped to clarify for me lots of things about the way mainstream algebraic geometers talk about stacks and toposes and coverings and covers and morita-equivalence and so forth .... and again (or at least i think i mentioned this somewhere somewhat recently), it tied in with ideas i'd vaguely heard about joyal's work on grothendieck toposes and localic groupoids (and i think i remember discussing this tie-in with chris a bit) ....

another key point is that it's interesting to think conceptually about how to get from an affine groupoid scheme to a 2-rig, and vice versa ....

David Egolf (Jan 10 2025 at 02:25):

Most of the above conversation is sailing way over my head. But I just wanted to say that this part sounds powerful and beautiful to me:

the general theme of "galois connection" is a theme with variations that's useful in situations like this where you have two (or more....) related "concepts" x and y and you're wondering about the extent to which they're really "equivalent"; you set up "opposing" processes f for turning x-instances into y-instances and g for the opposite direction, you declare the "nice" x-instances to be the "fixed" points of fg and the "nice" y-instances to be the "fixed" points of gf, and if you do it right then you automatically/ish get an "equivalence" between the concepts of "nice x-instance" and "nice y-instance".

(Of course what immediately comes to mind for me is imaging reconstruction - where we can turn observations into pictures and pictures into (predicted) observations - but I'm sure the idea is also helpful in various mathematical settings).

David Egolf (Jan 10 2025 at 02:35):

I think we saw this idea in action in #learning: reading & references > reading through Baez's topos theory blog posts, where we had an adjunction between a category of presheaves and a category of bundles. The sheaves are the "nice" presheaves in the sense that if you sheafify them you get back what you started! And the etale spaces are the "nice" bundles in a similar sense, I guess. (And indeed there is an equivalence between the category of sheaves and the category of etale spaces for a given space).

I really like idea that "nice" structures can be discovered by studying the invariant objects of an adjunction (in the sense that if you take them on a round trip, you end up where you started, up to isomorphism). From that perspective, every adjunction is possibly a source of fresh ideas for "nice" objects!

Todd Trimble (Jan 10 2025 at 02:50):

David Egolf said:

I think we saw this idea in action in #learning: reading & references > reading through Baez's topos theory blog posts, where we had an adjunction between a category of presheaves and a category of bundles. The sheaves are the "nice" presheaves in the sense that if you sheafify them you get back what you started! And the etale spaces are the "nice" bundles in a similar sense, I guess. (And indeed there is an equivalence between the category of sheaves and the category of etale spaces for a given space).

I really like idea that "nice" structures can be discovered by studying the invariant objects of an adjunction (in the sense that if you take them on a round trip, you end up where you started, up to isomorphism). From that perspective, every adjunction is possibly a source of fresh ideas for "nice" objects!

Yes! Your suspicion about the Galois connection (really, adjunction) whose "fixed points" on both sides gives you the equivalence between sheaves and etale spaces is correct, and is nicely explained in this n-Category Cafe post by Tom Leinster.

John Baez (Jan 10 2025 at 02:58):

(deleted)

Todd Trimble (Jan 10 2025 at 03:07):

As a point of terminology, I would normally reserve "Galois connection" for a contravariant adjunction between posets (as opposed to general categories), but Jim has been known to bend ordinary meanings. By "contravariant adjunction", I mean a situation where you have two functors f:Pop→Qf: P^{op} \to Qf:Pop→Q and g:Qop→Pg: Q^{op} \to Pg:Qop→P, where you can say fop⊣gf^{op} \dashv gfop⊣g or equivalently gop⊣fg^{op} \dashv fgop⊣f. Usually I prefer to commit to neither choice (i.e., attach "op" to neither fff nor ggg), and just say instead that we have a natural bijection between morphisms

q→f(p)p→g(q).\frac{q \to f(p)}{p \to g(q)}.p→g(q)q→f(p)​.

A really typical situation where Galois connections arise is where you have a relation RRR between sets XXX and YYY -- it can be any relation whatsoever, and I'm going to write R(x,y)R(x, y)R(x,y) to mean (x,y)∈R(x, y) \in R(x,y)∈R -- and then you define poset maps between power sets as follows: f:P(X)op→P(Y)f: P(X)^{op} \to P(Y)f:P(X)op→P(Y) takes a subset SSS of XXX to

f(S)={y∈Y:(∀x∈S)  R(x,y)}f(S) = \{y \in Y: (\forall x \in S)\; R(x, y)\}f(S)={y∈Y:(∀x∈S)R(x,y)}

and g:P(Y)op→P(X)g: P(Y)^{op} \to P(X)g:P(Y)op→P(X) takes a subset TTT of YYY to

g(T)={x∈X:(∀y∈T)  R(x,y)}g(T) = \{x \in X: (\forall y \in T)\; R(x, y)\}g(T)={x∈X:(∀y∈T)R(x,y)}.

For example, if XXX is a polynomial algebra k[x1,…,xn]k[x_1, \ldots, x_n]k[x1​,…,xn​] and Y=knY = k^nY=kn, then you can define a Galois connection where the (f,a)∈X×Y(f, a) \in X \times Y(f,a)∈X×Y belongs to RRR iff f(a1,…,an)=0f(a_1, \ldots, a_n) = 0f(a1​,…,an​)=0. This example is important in algebraic geometry.

But there are a million examples!

John Baez (Jan 10 2025 at 03:11):

Btw, @David Egolf, Galois connections as Todd just explained them are the kind of adjunction used in "formal concept analysis". There we start with the relation RRR that means "thing xxx has property yyy".

David Michael Roberts (Jan 10 2025 at 03:55):

Whoops. Might need to move this

Notification Bot (Jan 10 2025 at 04:17):

2 messages were moved here from #community: our work > John Baez) by Madeleine Birchfield.

John Baez (Jan 10 2025 at 07:01):

Hey @David Egolf - I wrote a long post in reply to your comment here and put it in the wrong place! Here it is, somewhat improved:

David Egolf said:

I just wanted to say that this part sounds powerful and beautiful to me:

the general theme of "galois connection" is a theme with variations that's useful in situations like this where you have two (or more....) related "concepts" x and y and you're wondering about the extent to which they're really "equivalent"; you set up "opposing" processes f for turning x-instances into y-instances and g for the opposite direction, you declare the "nice" x-instances to be the "fixed" points of fg and the "nice" y-instances to be the "fixed" points of gf, and if you do it right then you automatically/ish get an "equivalence" between the concepts of "nice x-instance" and "nice y-instance".

I think we saw this idea in action in #learning: reading & references > reading through Baez's topos theory blog posts, where we had an adjunction between a category of presheaves and a category of bundles. The sheaves are the "nice" presheaves in the sense that if you sheafify them you get back what you started! And the etale spaces are the "nice" bundles in a similar sense, I guess.

Yes, exactly! That's a perfect example of what Jim is talking about. We have an adjunction between two categories, say

L:C→DL: \mathsf{C} \to \mathsf{D}L:C→D and R:D→CR: \mathsf{D} \to \mathsf{C}R:D→C,

so we get two round-trip functors

RL:C→C R L : \mathsf{C} \to \mathsf{C}RL:C→C and LR:D→DL R : \mathsf{D} \to \mathsf{D}LR:D→D

Then the fixed points of these two round-trip functors form "improved" versions of C\mathsf{C}C and D\mathsf{D}D that are equivalent.

I forget if we proved that the category of sheaves on a space is equivalent to the category of etale spaces over that space, but it's true and this is why.

I should add that the two "round-trip functors" have nice names, quite generally: RLR LRL is a monad and LRL RLR is a comonad.

I really like idea that "nice" structures can be discovered by studying the invariant objects of an adjunction (in the sense that if you take them on a round trip, you end up where you started, up to isomorphism). From that perspective, every adjunction is possibly a source of fresh ideas for "nice" objects!

Yes, it's great. We can even use this method to define the concept of 'concept'.

In "formal concept analysis" we start with a relation RRR between a set XXX of "things" and a set YYY of properties", where R(x,y)R(x,y)R(x,y) means "thing xxx has property yyy".

Then, following the procedure @Todd Trimble explained, we get a Galois connection between two posets: the power set P(X)P(X)P(X) and the power set P(Y)P(Y)P(Y). This consists of two maps:

  • One sends any set of things to the set of properties they all have.
  • The other sends any bset of properties to the set of things that all have all those properties.

The fixed points of the round-trip process are called "concepts". The n-Cafe host @Simon Willerton gives some nice examples here:

  • Formal concept analysis.

David Egolf (Jan 10 2025 at 19:44):

Thanks for your replies, @Todd Trimble and @John Baez. It will take me some time to work through them, but they look quite interesting!

John Baez (Jan 10 2025 at 23:09):

2024-01-22

https://youtu.be/V8gOqDDnPN4

Galois representations and motives: trying to understand the Langlands program, or at least the modularity theorem. Galois representations from torsion points on elliptic curves.

Consider the 5-torsion points of the Gaussian elliptic curve. These 25 points form a discrete subvariety of the elliptic curve, which is a projective variety, but we can treat this subvariety as an affine variety, and since it's discrete it is the spectrum of a special commutative Frobenius algebra. Since the 5-torsion points form a group, this algebra also becomes a bicommutative Hopf algebra. But these 25 points are also naturally a 2-dimensional vector space over the finite field 𝔽₅, and because the Gaussian elliptic curve is defined over ℚ, this vector space gives a representation of the absolute Galois group of ℚ.

Working with other elliptic curves and other primes ℓ, we get a map from the moduli stack of elliptic curves to the moduli stack of representations of the absolute Galois group of ℚ on 2d vector spaces over Fℓ\mathbb{F}_\ellFℓ​. This map of moduli stacks really arises, in a contravariant way, from a map of theories in the doctrine of 2-rigs: namely, a map from the theory of 2d vector spaces over Fℓ\mathbb{F}_\ellFℓ​ to the theory of elliptic curves. (A "theory in the doctrine of 2-rigs" is just a 2-rig.)

We would like to describe the theory of elliptic curves as the theory of a formal group together with extra relations which force our formal group to be an elliptic curve.

For more details see:

Nigel Boston, The proof of Fermat's last theorem, https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Thong-Nguyen-Quang-Do/post/Are-there-other-pieces-of-information-about-Victory-Road-to-FLT/attachment/5b0ab419b53d2f63c3ce5da8/AS%3A630893421006850%401527428121602/download/FLT+Boston.pdf

We would like to understand Chapter 4, and especially Theorem 4.28.

David Egolf (Jan 12 2025 at 02:08):

Todd Trimble said:

Yes! Your suspicion about the Galois connection (really, adjunction) whose "fixed points" on both sides gives you the equivalence between sheaves and etale spaces is correct, and is nicely explained in this n-Category Cafe post by Tom Leinster.

Very interesting! I don't think I had learned about the nerve/realization adjunction before! It's very cool that we can create an adjunction given a functor to a category with colimits! It's somewhat tempting to consider a few example functors to see what adjunctions (and corresponding "fixed points") we get.

David Egolf (Jan 13 2025 at 02:24):

I just read through @Todd Trimble's explanation of how to get a Galois connection from a relation! Thanks for sharing that - that sounds like a pretty powerful way to get Galois connections. I find myself wondering what the "fixed points" would be in the algebraic geometry example.

David Egolf (Jan 13 2025 at 02:26):

Let's see. We can start with some collection of polynomials with coefficients in kkk and in nnn variables. So this is some subset SSS of k[x1,…,xn]k[x_1, \dots, x_n]k[x1​,…,xn​]. What would f(S)f(S)f(S) be? It should be the collection of points f(S)f(S)f(S) in knk^nkn that make each of the polynomials in SSS vanish when evaluated at any point in f(S)f(S)f(S).

David Egolf (Jan 13 2025 at 02:27):

Then we apply ggg to get g(f(S))g(f(S))g(f(S)). This is the set of polynomials that vanish at each of the points in f(S)f(S)f(S). In general this could be a larger collection of polynomials than what we started with.

David Egolf (Jan 13 2025 at 02:28):

I seem to recall that the radical ideals (of k[x1,…,xn]k[x_1, \dots, x_n]k[x1​,…,xn​]) are the special collections of polynomials that don't get enlarged by this round trip process.

Consulting Eisenbud's book "Commutative Algebra with a View Towards Algebraic Geometry", this appears to be closely related to the Nullstellensastz. Based on this, I'm guessing the "fixed points" on the geometry side of things are the algebraic subsets of knk^nkn.

David Egolf (Jan 13 2025 at 03:01):

John Baez said:

I forget if we proved that the category of sheaves on a space is equivalent to the category of etale spaces over that space, but it's true and this is why.

I don't think we proved it, but it was mentioned in this blog post.

I should add that the two "round-trip functors" have nice names, quite generally: RLR LRL is a monad and LRL RLR is a comonad.

Good to know!

In "formal concept analysis" we start with a relation RRR between a set XXX of "things" and a set YYY of properties", where R(x,y)R(x,y)R(x,y) means "thing xxx has property yyy".

Then, following the procedure Todd Trimble explained...

The fixed points of the round-trip process are called "concepts".

Wow, that's cool!

The n-Cafe host Simon Willerton gives some nice examples here:

  • Formal concept analysis.

This blog article mentions a "closure operation". I'm imagining taking an object and sending it on repeated round trips until it stops changing. Then the "closure operation" I am guessing maps each object to the object it "stabilizes" at under repeated round trips. It looks like in this case we only need one round trip to stabilize, but maybe in general (for adjunctions other than galois connections?) we could require more round trips?

David Egolf (Jan 13 2025 at 03:03):

This reminds me a lot, by the way, of an image reconstruction algorithm I used in my MSc thesis. In that algorithm, we repeatedly go back and forth between observations and pictures, and we obtain our final picture when this process stabilizes. (Hopefully I'm remembering that correctly... it's been a while since I thought about the stuff in my thesis in detail!)

Todd Trimble (Jan 13 2025 at 04:56):

David Egolf said:

I seem to recall that the radical ideals (of k[x1,…,xn]k[x_1, \dots, x_n]k[x1​,…,xn​]) are the special collections of polynomials that don't get enlarged by this round trip process.

Consulting Eisenbud's book "Commutative Algebra with a View Towards Algebraic Geometry", this appears to be closely related to the Nullstellensastz. Based on this, I'm guessing the "fixed points" on the geometry side of things are the algebraic subsets of knk^nkn.

Yes, indeed! I sketched how the Galois connection goes back here, but it won't hurt to say it again at more length, now with a view toward fixed points of the Galois connection.

Fix a ground field kkk. We had introduced a relation between polynomials f∈k[x1,…,xn]f \in k[x_1, \ldots, x_n]f∈k[x1​,…,xn​] and points (a1,…,an)∈kn(a_1, \ldots, a_n) \in k^n(a1​,…,an​)∈kn, where a pair (f,a)(f, a)(f,a) belongs to the relation iff f(a1,…,an)=0f(a_1, \ldots, a_n) = 0f(a1​,…,an​)=0, i.e., 0∈k0 \in k0∈k is the value we get when we evaluate the polynomial fff by substituting a1a_1a1​ for x1x_1x1​, a2a_2a2​ for x2x_2x2​, etc. What contravariant adjunction do we get in this case?

In one direction, we have a map V:P(k[x1,…,xn])op→P(kn)V: P(k[x_1, \ldots, x_n])^{op} \to P(k^n)V:P(k[x1​,…,xn​])op→P(kn) where

$$V(S) = \{(a_1, \ldots, a_n) \in k^n|\; f(a_1, \ldots, a_n) = 0 \mathrm{ for all f∈Sf \in Sf∈S }\}$$

so in other words the common locus of all the polynomials in SSS. A subset in knk^nkn of this form is (by definition) an affine variety in knk^nkn, hence the letter VVV.

In the other direction, we have a map I:P(kn)op→P(k[x1,…,xn])I: P(k^n)^{op} \to P(k[x_1, \ldots, x_n])I:P(kn)op→P(k[x1​,…,xn​]) where

$$I(T) = \{f \in k[x_1, \ldots, x_n]|\; f(a_1, \ldots, a_n) = 0 \mathrm{ for all a∈Ta \in Ta∈T }\}$$.

Note that this is an ideal in k[x1,…,xn]k[x_1, \ldots, x_n]k[x1​,…,xn​] (hence the letter III), i.e., you can easily check that

  • I(T)I(T)I(T) is closed under addition and subtraction;
  • If f∈I(T)f \in I(T)f∈I(T) and g∈k[x1,…,xn]g \in k[x_1, \ldots, x_n]g∈k[x1​,…,xn​] is any polynomial, then fg∈I(T)fg \in I(T)fg∈I(T).

Actually, it's what is called a radical ideal, meaning it also has this property:

  • If fn∈I(T)f^n \in I(T)fn∈I(T), then also f∈I(T)f \in I(T)f∈I(T).

By the general magic of Galois connections, we have V=V∘I∘VV = V \circ I \circ VV=V∘I∘V and I=I∘V∘II = I \circ V \circ II=I∘V∘I. This means that the fixed points on one side of the Galois correspondence are just the varieties (subsets of the form V(S)V(S)V(S), and the fixed points on the other side are radical ideals (subsets of the form I(T)I(T)I(T)). There's a one-to-one correspondence between these.

Both VVV and III are right adjoints. In the case where the categories involved are power sets, right adjoints are the same thing as limit-preserving maps between posets, or in other words maps preserving arbitrary meets. So for example,

V:P(k[x1,…,xn])op→P(kn)V: P(k[x_1, \ldots, x_n])^{op} \to P(k^n)V:P(k[x1​,…,xn​])op→P(kn)

takes meets in P(k[x1,…,xn])opP(k[x_1, \ldots, x_n])^{op}P(k[x1​,…,xn​])op, which are joins (unions) in P(k[x1,…,xn])P(k[x_1, \ldots, x_n])P(k[x1​,…,xn​]), to meets in P(kn)P(k^n)P(kn), in other words to intersections of varieties. And similarly, III takes unions in P(kn)P(k^n)P(kn) to meets in P(k[x1,…,xn])P(k[x_1, \ldots, x_n])P(k[x1​,…,xn​]). Usually these observations appear as exercises in algebraic geometry texts, but there's nothing particularly "geometric" about it -- it's a phenomenon that happens at the more general level of Galois connections.

V(S)={(a1,…,an)∈kn∣  f(a1,…,an)=0  for  all  f∈S}V(S) = \{(a_1, \ldots, a_n) \in k^n|\; f(a_1, \ldots, a_n) = 0 \; \mathrm{for} \; \mathrm{all} \; f \in S\}V(S)={(a1​,…,an​)∈kn∣f(a1​,…,an​)=0forallf∈S}

and

I(T)={f∈k[x1,…,xn]∣  f(a1,…,an)=0  for  all  a∈T}I(T) = \{f \in k[x_1, \ldots, x_n]|\; f(a_1, \ldots, a_n) = 0 \; \mathrm{for} \; \mathrm{all} \; a \in T \}I(T)={f∈k[x1​,…,xn​]∣f(a1​,…,an​)=0foralla∈T}

John Baez (Jan 13 2025 at 18:14):

David Egolf said:

I seem to recall that the radical ideals (of k[x1,…,xn]k[x_1, \dots, x_n]k[x1​,…,xn​]) are the special collections of polynomials that don't get enlarged by this round trip process.

I think that's right! For example the ideal I⊂k[x]I \subset k[x]I⊂k[x] generated by the element (x−2)3(x-2)^3(x−2)3 is not radical. It consists of functions that vanish along with their first two derivatives at the point 222. If we take the round trip you describe, we get a bigger ideal: the ideal generated by x−2x-2x−2. This consists of all functions that vanish at the point 222. This is a radical ideal, and it's the smallest radical ideal containing III.

The smallest radical ideal containing an ideal III is called I\sqrt{I}I​, which is a bit of a joke. I suppose this process should be called 'radicalization', but I don't think anyone calls it that.

Consulting Eisenbud's book "Commutative Algebra with a View Towards Algebraic Geometry", this appears to be closely related to the Nullstellensastz. Based on this, I'm guessing the "fixed points" on the geometry side of things are the algebraic subsets of knk^nkn.

Yeah! This Galois connection you are discussing is indeed one of the foundations of algebraic geometry: it's a nice connection (pun intended) between algebra and geometry. But it's also a great introduction to how adjunctions let us 'refine' concepts.

David Egolf (Jan 13 2025 at 19:25):

That all makes sense! Thanks to both of you for elaborating on that topic!

John Baez (Jan 15 2025 at 01:47):

In February 2024, @Chris Grossack (they/them) joined my conversations with James Dolan (though there's one earlier one). I feel it took us a while to learn to talk without stepping on each other's toes - looking back at these I wish I'd shut up more. Later the conversations became a bit more efficient, sometimes.

2024-02-05

https://youtu.be/s1pYaO9gkho

Concrete examples of modular curves.

First case: the Coxeter group

o--3--o--4--o

is the symmetry group of a cube, which can be seen as a modular curve of genus 0.

Second case: the Coxeter group

o--3--o--∞--o

is PGL(2,Z), the symmetry group of a certain triangulation of the hyperbolic plane. The hyperbolic plane is not itself a modular curve, but it has many quotients that are modular curves, for example X₀(2), X₀(4), X₀(8), etc., each being is a branched cover of the previous one. The modular curve X₀(n) is the hyperbolic plane modulo the action of the group Γ₀(n), the so-called 'Hecke congruence subgroup of level n', which consists of 2×2 integer matrices where the lower left entry equals 0 mod n. X₀(n) is the moduli space of elliptic curves with a chosen nontrivial n-torsion point.

For congruence subgroups, see:

https://math.berkeley.edu/~sander/speaking/24June2015%20UMS%20Talk.pdf

John Baez (Jan 15 2025 at 01:53):

2024-02-19

https://youtu.be/KL9VgPaGNOw

A dictionary relating group theory and Kleinian geometry. Subgroups of a group G correspond to types of geometrical figures, cosets of a subgroup correspond to geometrical figures of a given type, and double cosets correspond to incidence relations between figures of two different types.

An example: take G = SL(2,ℝ) and the two subgroups SL(2,ℤ) and U(1). SL(2,ℝ)/U(1) is the hyperbolic plane. SL(2,ℤ) acts on the hyperbolic plane preserving a certain triangulation, and SL(2,ℤ)\SL(2,ℝ)/U(1) is the moduli space of elliptic curves (at least up to some fudge factors involving ℤ/2).

How double cosets give Hecke operators.

The modular curves

X₀(2) ↞ X₀(4) ↞ X₀(8) ↞ ...

where X₀(n) is the moduli space of elliptic curves with a chosen nontrivial n-torsion point.

John Baez (Jan 16 2025 at 02:40):

2024-02-26

https://youtu.be/s1pYaO9gkho

The clock arithmetic of slopes: fixing ℤ/n by introducing formal reciprocals for all elements that don't have them, or better yet: introducing formal reciprocals for all elements, but identifying them with the actual reciprocals when those exist. We don't get a ring, just a set with 2n - ϕ(n) elements, where where ϕ(n) is the number of elements of ℤ/n that don't have reciprocals, called 'Euler's totient function':

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Euler%27s_totient_function

The resulting set acts like a projective line for ℤ/n: indeed it's the set of ℤ/n-points for the scheme P¹. Apparently this set can also be seen as SL(2,ℤ)/Γ₀(n) where Γ₀(n), the so-called 'Hecke congruence subgroup of level n', consists of 2×2 integer matrices where the lower left entry equals 0 mod n. For more on congruence subgroups, see:

https://math.berkeley.edu/~sander/speaking/24June2015%20UMS%20Talk.pdf

Next, compare SL(2,ℤ)/Γ₀(n) to the modular curve X₀(n) = H/Γ₀(n) where H is the hyperbolic plane. This sets up connections to the dessin d'enfant for X₀(n):

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dessin_d%27enfant

and also the Coxeter group

o--3--o--∞--o

namely PGL(2,Z). Drawing a picture of X₀(11). Kostant on Galois' last letter and PSL(2,11):

Bertram Kostant, The graph of the truncated icosahedron and the last letter of Galois, http://www.ams.org/notices/199509/kostant.pdf

Erratum: 24 is not the largest number such that every number smaller than it and relatively prime to it is prime; that honor goes to 30:

https://mathstodon.xyz/@johncarlosbaez/112017268404654926

John Baez (Jan 27 2025 at 18:03):

Fernando Zalamea

Grothendieck and Differential Equations

Pages 45-55

The Enduring Legacy of Grothendieck’s Duality Theorem

Pages 151-169
Tôhoku 65 Years After

Pages 171-228
About Grothendieck Fibrations

Grothendieck Did Not Believe in Universes, He Believed in Topos and Schemes

Colin McLarty

Pages 265-276
The “Unifying Notion” of Topos

Olivia Caramello

Pages 277-307
Motivating Motives

John C. Baez

Pages 309-323
My View on and Experience with Grothendieck’s Anabelian Geometry

Mohamed Saïdi

Pages 325-335
Grothendieck’s Use of Equality

Kevin Buzzard

Pages 337-354
Boolean Valued Models, Sheafifications, and Boolean Ultrapowers of Tychonoff Spaces

Moreno Pierobon, Matteo Viale

Pages 355-390
Toward a Geometry for Syntax

Jonathan Sterling

Pages 391-432
Investigating Definability in Propositional Logic via Sheaves on Grothendieck Topologies

Silvio Ghilardi

Pages 433-452
Grothendieck and Model Theory: Five Characters in Search of a Theme

Andrés Villaveces Niño

Pages 453-478
Context-Dependence and Descent Theory

Brice Halimi

Pages 479-501
Grothendieck and Teichmüller Modular Spaces

Christian Houzel
