John Baez (Feb 02 2025 at 20:04):

As the Trump gang takes down or modifies federal government webpages, people are working to back them up. I've made a list of suggestions for how to help:

  • Backing up US federal databases, Azimuth.

Just to increase their spread, I'll put them here too:

• Save existing websites to the Wayback Machine. The easiest way to do this is by installing the Wayback Machine extension for your browser. The add-ons and extensions are listed on the left-hand panel of the website’s homepage.

• To find missing websites, go to Wayback Machine and type in the website’s URL in the search bar.

• If you’re concerned that certain websites or web pages may be removed, you can suggest federal websites and content that end in .gov, .mil and .com to the End of Term Web Archive.

• You can suggest federal climate and environmental databases to Environmental Data and Governance Initiative.

• You can suggest databases to The Data Liberation Project.

• You can suggest databases and also report databases you’ve backed up to the Safeguarding Research Discourse Group.

• For CDC data: tell science journalist Maggie Koerth which CDC data you’ve downloaded and whether you’ve made them publicly available.

David Michael Roberts (Feb 03 2025 at 00:57):

@John Baez regarding your zeta-function draft, there is (or was) a typo on the first page:

A ZXZ_XZX​-structure on a finite set is a way of making it into a semisimple commutative ring, say kkk, and then picking an element of S(k)S(k)S(k).

David Michael Roberts (Feb 03 2025 at 00:58):

X(k), not S(k) there, I think.

John Baez (Feb 03 2025 at 01:09):

Whoops! SSS was for "scheme", but I decided against that. I fixed it, thanks. There have been some other changes too, and you can always get the latest version here.

Jean-Baptiste Vienney (Feb 03 2025 at 01:48):

You wrote “A ZZZ-structure on a finite set is a way of making it into a semisimple commutative ring—or equivalently, a product of finite fields.” But a semisimple commutative ring is equivalently a finite product of fields. I guess an infinite product of fields (finite or not) is never a semisimple ring. Is it a mistake?

John Baez (Feb 03 2025 at 02:05):

I'm describing "A ZZZ-structure on a finite set". There's no way to make a finite set into an infinite product of fields, so my two characterizations of a ZZZ-structure on a finite set are equivalent.

John Baez (Feb 03 2025 at 02:08):

Maybe I'll change what I wrote to

"or equivalently, making it into a product of finite fields".

Jean-Baptiste Vienney (Feb 03 2025 at 02:10):

Oh, ok. Thank you.

Jean-Baptiste Vienney (Feb 03 2025 at 02:11):

It is obvious that the product will be finite.

Jean-Baptiste Vienney (Feb 03 2025 at 02:17):

John Baez said:

Maybe I'll change what I wrote to

"or equivalently, making it into a product of finite fields".

This new formulation sounds a bit clearer to me. I was confused when I thought I read that a commutative semisimple ring is the same as an arbitrary product of fields.

John Baez (Feb 03 2025 at 02:18):

Done.

John Baez (Feb 03 2025 at 05:28):

I might as well say what paper we're talking about! I started this as an nLab article in 2010, based on conversations with James Dolan. In January 2025 I finally decided to polish it up into a paper. It took some work to make all the proofs rigorous, but I think it was worthwhile:

  • Dirichlet species and arithmetic zeta functions.

Abstract. Though Joyal's species are known to categorify generating functions in enumerative combinatorics, they also categorify zeta functions in algebraic geometry. The reason is that any scheme XXX of finite type over the integers gives a 'zeta species' ZXZ_XZX​, and any species FFF gives a Dirichlet series F^\widehat{F}F, in such a way that Z^X\widehat{Z}_XZX​ is the arithmetic zeta function of XXX, a well-known Dirichlet series that encodes the number of points of XXX over each finite field. Specifically, a ZXZ_XZX​-structure on a finite set is a way of making that set into a semisimple commutative ring, say kkk, and then choosing a kkk-point of the scheme XXX. This is joint work with James Dolan.

By the way, the actual results don't rely on schemes - so you don't even need to understand those to read this paper. I wrote it in a way that's supposed to be readable without any knowledge of algebraic geometry.

David Michael Roberts (Feb 03 2025 at 07:01):

I'm not sure if it's worth saying, but your multiplicative functors are sheaves for the extensive topology on the category of affine schemes, which is to say they are really almost, but not quite, entirely unlike schemes, which are sheaves for the finer Zariski topology.

I do wonder if the tameness requirement is related to something like how a functor that is a scheme has a(n open) cover by affine schemes, and moreover an affine scheme has a corresponding tame functor purely for cardinality reasons. That is, starting from a multiplicative+tame functor, what can one say about how close it might be to being a scheme?

David Michael Roberts (Feb 03 2025 at 07:03):

Or might such a functor be a quotient of a scheme, or an algebraic space, or whatnot. I guess the big obstruction would be how one connects something that is a sheaf for the Zariski topology, to one that is merely a sheaf for the extensive topology.

But this is getting too far afield, I'm just think out loud, now.

John Baez (Feb 03 2025 at 16:17):

This is very interesting. Personally I'm quite happy with multiplicative (= product-preserving) functors X:CommRing→SetX: \mathsf{CommRing} \to \mathsf{Set}X:CommRing→Set as objects of study in their own right, especially since they open up this field of study to people who don't know about schemes, sheaves, Grothendieck topologies, and so on. James Dolan likes to undercut traditional subjects by doing them, or at least parts of them, in simpler ways. I like it too - it's a kind of do-it-yourself approach to math: learning by redoing.

However, if the experts get interested in this subject they'll definitely want to do what you're starting to do: connect these ideas to traditional algebraic geometry.

David Michael Roberts (Feb 04 2025 at 02:02):

A last point, in case anyone else feels like thinking about this: I've asked over on MathOverflow if it's possible to define [[algebraic space]] s just as certain sheaves on affine schemes, that is, as tame functors CommRing→Set\mathbf{CommRing}\to\mathbf{Set}CommRing→Set.

The nLab claims the Stacks Project definition of algebraic space is as I am asking about, but I just looked at the definitions and they do not agree with the nLab ones, rather taking certain sheaves on sites of "all" schemes. Questions about representability of the diagonal change, if the site changes, and I don't know if the nLab definition really agrees with the Stacks Project one.

David Michael Roberts (Feb 04 2025 at 02:03):

Oh, this was already asked! https://mathoverflow.net/questions/11226/commutative-rings-to-algebraic-spaces-in-one-jump and yes, it can be done, thanks to a nice answer by Jim Borger.

David Michael Roberts (Feb 04 2025 at 02:11):

I have deleted my question, but people with high enough rep can still see it at that link. I think that 'finite type' probably makes sense for algebraic spaces, and this should imply that the corresponding functor on commutative rings is tame. But oh well, I'd best be off doing something else, I was hoping pointing out that John's result applies to zeta functions of certain algebraic spaces as well, but I'd have to think a bit more to get a decent question together about those.

John Baez (Feb 04 2025 at 02:19):

Sorry my initial reaction wasn't more welcoming... it's just not the sort of thing I feel I can make progress on myself. But I've heard a bit about algebraic spaces, and if one can define a zeta function of a 'finite type' algebraic space that could be interesting. It makes me want to know a candidate example of a finite type algebraic space that's not a scheme - what sort of flavor does it have?

David Michael Roberts (Feb 04 2025 at 02:31):

if you take a scheme XXX and take its quotient by an equivalence relation R⊆X×XR \subseteq X\times XR⊆X×X where R→XR\to XR→X is étale, then the result may not be a scheme, but it is an algebraic space. At least, that's the standard "example", but I don't know any concrete examples of this phenomenon! I guess it's a bit like taking the quotient of a manifold by a badly-behaved relation, but where it's still meaningful to talk geometrically about what's going on.

(No worries about your reaction, I understand what you are doing with this paper, I was just following my own line of thought)

David Michael Roberts (Feb 04 2025 at 02:34):

Oh, here's examples: https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/02Z0 In particular, you can take the quotient of a scheme by the action of a group (not a group scheme, just a group GGG, acting externally via G→Aut(X)G\to Aut(X)G→Aut(X)), and the result becomes an algebraic space.

David Michael Roberts (Feb 04 2025 at 02:35):

A more prosaic example is this: take the affine line over a field of characteristic not = 2. Then quotient by the action of Z/2 by x↦−xx \mapsto -xx↦−x.

John Baez (Feb 04 2025 at 02:35):

What's the map R→XR \to XR→X above? R→X×XR \to X \times XR→X×X followed by one of the projections X×X→XX \times X \to XX×X→X?

John Baez (Feb 04 2025 at 02:37):

David Michael Roberts said:

A more prosaic example is this: take the affine line over a field of characteristic not = 2. Then quotient by the action of Z/2 by x↦−xx \mapsto -xx↦−x.

Okay, now we're talking!

David Michael Roberts (Feb 04 2025 at 02:39):

The whole notion of 'R-valued point' is more subtle for algebraic spaces, since one should really be thinking of them as special sorts of algebraic stacks presented by equivalence relations in schemes, not the more general internal groupoids in schemes. I don't understand much of this stuff, it's more like a weird frontier that I use to try to test my very small intuition about fancy algebraic geometry and/or general ideas about stacks in other contexts.

John Baez (Feb 04 2025 at 02:43):

You mean sort of like how orbifolds can be thought of as singular spaces but are probably better thought of as stacks?

David Michael Roberts (Feb 04 2025 at 02:45):

Yes, except in this case no points have nontrivial automorphisms, it's just that the gluing together is bad.

John Baez (Feb 04 2025 at 02:49):

I'm very puzzled. Do you know a simple example over the integers? Is the affine line over Z\mathbb{Z}Z mod the action x↦−xx \mapsto -xx↦−x an algebraic space over the integers? If so I'd like to calculate its zeta function, just for kicks.

John Baez (Feb 04 2025 at 02:50):

(Why doesn't the origin has a nontrivial automorphism here?)

David Michael Roberts (Feb 04 2025 at 03:16):

Ah, sorry, I was brushing details under the rug. You take the equivalence relation coming from the group action. And the affine line apparently needs to be over a field, according to https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/02Z1

And the quotient-by-a-group-action example has an extra condition: https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/02Z2 namely that if a group element fixes a point, then some induced action on the residue field at that point is nontrivial. This condition (which I didn't notice before, and I don't really grasp) apparently makes the group action an equivalence relation in schemes.

David Michael Roberts (Feb 04 2025 at 03:19):

I also would like to know some pedestrian examples, but that would require some expertise, or else sustained attention that I cannot spare.

John Baez (Feb 05 2025 at 00:32):

Hmm, I'm hoping that the second example, https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/02Z2, doesn't require working over a field. I need algebraic spaces over the integers to define their arithmetic zeta functions.

John Baez (Feb 05 2025 at 00:34):

But I don't see why the (obvious?) action on the residue field should be nontrivial, if the original group action is more 'geometric' than 'algebraic' (if you know what I mean).

John Baez (Feb 05 2025 at 00:34):

Anyway, interesting!

John Baez (Feb 05 2025 at 00:40):

On a completely different note, I wrote a little article based on a remark I heard here:

  • A characterization of standard Borel spaces.

My coauthors and I used standard Borel spaces in our book Infinite-Dimensional Representations of 2-Groups. I think the combination of a little 2-category theory and Lie theory with some nontrivial measure theory made this book too scary for most mathematicians! It's nice to see that standard Borel spaces have a characterization even category theorists could love.

David Michael Roberts (Feb 05 2025 at 01:46):

FWIW I asked here: https://math.stackexchange.com/questions/5031605/whats-a-simple-explicit-example-of-a-finite-type-algebraic-space-over-specz-t

Chris Grossack (they/them) (Feb 05 2025 at 03:03):

Oh! I've been meaning to read that paper! Ronnie Chen (the author) is doing REALLY cool work in the intersection of descriptive set theory and category theory, and these are two of my favorite subjects!

John Baez (Feb 05 2025 at 03:20):

I wish I could figure out the key steps in this paper, modulo "Loomis–Sikorski duality", which I'm willing to take for granted. That duality thereorem says that the category of standard Borel spaces is the opposite of the category of countably presented countably complete Boolean algebras. I don't know the proof, but it sounds so much like all the other duality theorems I know that I'm not worried about it. I guess Chen uses it to reduce the universal property of the category of standard Borel spaces to a universal property of the category of these boolean algebras.

Graham Manuell (Feb 06 2025 at 14:19):

@Simon Henry apparently has a similar result characterising prodiscrete (i.e. ultraparacompact) locales as the initial complete infinitely-extensive category.

So I'd wager that in the countable case without the Boolean assumption you'd get the category of zero-dimensional Polish spaces.

John Baez (Feb 08 2025 at 01:03):

Cool!

John Baez (Feb 08 2025 at 01:06):

Shalma Wegsman is a `writing fellow' at Quanta magazine. I told her some really cool stuff about Noether's theorem, which goes beyond the stuff you always hear. She understood it, was excited about it, and seemed to want to explain it. She wrote this article:

  • How Noether's theorem revolutionized physics

Alas, her article doesn't mention any of that stuff. At the end it says

“There’s a lot we have left to learn by thinking hard about Noether’s theorem,” the mathematical physicist John Baez said. “It has layers and layers of depth to it.”

But none of these layers of depth are actually discussed. :crying_cat_face:

Perhaps they would be too much for readers to digest. But it's a pity that every pop science explanation of Noether's theorem stops at roughly the same point... just where things are starting to get really cool.

Please don't get me wrong - I'm not knocking Wegsman. There's just a lot of cool stuff which science journalists don't discuss. This is why I do my own science popularization. This is why, most times when I read a pop science article, I need to go the paper they're discussing, to get the really juicy stuff.

The good news is that Wegsman explains time translation symmetry, and she says Noether's theorem only works for theories described using a Lagrangian. I guess this counts as going deeper than usual.

(I gave her an example of what goes wrong when your theory isn't described using a Lagrangian.)

For my take on Noether's theorem, go here:

  • Getting to the bottom of Noether's theorem

This is pretty technical, but Wegner helped me figure out a simpler 'pop' way to explain some of the ideas, so sometime I may try that.

Fernando Yamauti (Feb 08 2025 at 01:52):

John Baez said:

The good news is that Wegsman explains time translation symmetry, and she says Noether's theorem only works for theories described using a Lagrangian. I guess this counts as going deeper than usual.

Are there non artificial examples of field theories that cannot be described by a Lagrangian? What I've heard in the past is that it's possible to obtain some field theories from Lagrangian ones such that people don't know whether those can or cannot be presented by (local) Lagrangians.

Perhaps the mentioned articles already explain that...

John Baez (Feb 08 2025 at 01:58):

I think it's a lot easier to study classical mechanics than field theory, and a simple classical mechanics problem not described by a Lagrangian is a particle that experiences friction, a force proportional to its velocity but pointing in the opposite direction. In 1-dimensional space this equation is an example

d2qdt2=−dqdt \displaystyle{ \frac{d^2 q}{d t^2} = - \frac{d q}{d t} }dt2d2q​=−dtdq​

It has time translation symmetry so if Noether's theorem applied there would be a conserved quantity (energy), but there is no conserved quantity of the form G(q,q˙)G(q,\dot{q})G(q,q˙​) (except constants).

John Baez (Feb 08 2025 at 02:04):

Interestingly if you allow for a Lagrangian with explicit time-dependence, namely

L(q,q˙,t)=12q˙2et L(q,\dot{q},t) = \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^2 e^{t} L(q,q˙​,t)=21​q˙​2et

you can get the above equation from a Lagrangian, and you do get a conserved energy, but this energy has explicit time-dependence, meaning the formula for energy includes the variable ttt, so the fact that it's conserved is sort of a joke.

John Baez (Feb 08 2025 at 02:15):

I'm pretty sure all of these ideas could be carried out in field theory, e.g. using the wave equation with a 'damping' term analogous to friction. But I think it's best to start off by keeping things simple.

Fernando Yamauti (Feb 08 2025 at 02:46):

John Baez said:

Interestingly if you allow for a Lagrangian with explicit time-dependence, namely

L(q,q˙,t)=12q˙2e−t L(q,\dot{q},t) = \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^2 e^{-t} L(q,q˙​,t)=21​q˙​2e−t

you can get the above equation from a Lagrangian, and you do get a conserved energy, but this energy has explicit time-dependence, meaning the formula for energy includes the variable ttt, so the fact that it's conserved is sort of a joke.

Ah. Ok. That clarifies it then. So there's a Lagrangian! I didn't know you were referring to time independent ones only. But I guess my initial question remains: do we have natural examples that definitely cannot be presented by a (possibly time dependent) Lagrangian?

John Baez (Feb 08 2025 at 04:38):

By the way, I fixed a typo: I should have said

L(q,q˙,t)=12q˙2et L(q,\dot{q}, t) = \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}^2 e^t L(q,q˙​,t)=21​q˙​2et

John Baez (Feb 08 2025 at 04:39):

Time-dependent Lagrangians are sort of silly, at least in physics, because they don't give a Hamiltonian that's a function on phase space.

John Baez (Feb 08 2025 at 04:50):

But if you want a theory that has no Lagrangian, not even a time-dependent one, we may need a particle in 2d space. Every smooth vector field on R1\mathbb{R}^1R1 is the gradient of a function, but not every smooth vector field on R2\mathbb{R}^2R2. So let's take a vector field that's not a gradient, like

F⃗=(−y,x) \vec{F} = (-y, x) F=(−y,x)

and write down Newton's F⃗=ma⃗\vec{F} = m \vec{a}F=ma for that vector field. I bet these equations don't come from a Lagrangian.

John Baez (Feb 08 2025 at 04:55):

I think you can just take the Euler-Lagrange equations, assume they give F⃗=ma⃗\vec{F} = m \vec{a}F=ma for this vector field F⃗\vec{F}F, and see they give a set of differential equations for LLL that have no solution because no function has F⃗\vec{F}F as its gradient.

John Baez (Feb 09 2025 at 00:22):

I wrote some stuff on Matstodon about my attempts to understand the Langlands program. It's very sketchy, both because most of my audience there doesn't know much math, and because I don't understand things well enough to give a really good simplified explanation.

Here's what I said:

John Baez (Feb 09 2025 at 00:23):

My friend James Dolan wants to understand the Langlands program and - as usual for him - refashion it in his own language. It's going slowly, because first he had to redo all the preliminary concepts, like schemes and algebraic stacks and elliptic curves and modular curves and Artin reciprocity. But I really enjoy this process of "learning by redoing": it's a lot more fun than just reading books and absorbing the standard viewpoint on everything. So I've been talking to him about this stuff every week on Zoom, and putting videos of the conversations on YouTube. Alas, I'm way behind on uploading them - about a year behind - because I want to write a summary of each conversation.

I'm more willing than Jim to just absorb the standard viewpoint by reading stuff, but it's fairly hard to get a simple explanation of the Langlands program. One reason is that it's a hydra-headed program: while popularizations calling it a "grand unified theory of mathematics" are hopelessly exaggerated, it does cover a lot of ground. Another is that math always seems confusing before it's actually done; later people come and clean it up.

One piece of the Langlands program says something like "every motivic L-function is also an automorphic L-function, and vice versa".

Huh? :thinking:

An L-function is a function like

L(s)  = 1 -2⁻ˢ - 3⁻ˢ + 6⁻ˢ + 8⁻ˢ - 13⁻ˢ - 16⁻ˢ + 23⁻ˢ - 24⁻ˢ + ....

where the list of numbers here is not random: it encodes interesting information about a mathematical object. For a fun and elementrary introduction to some examples, aimed at people who were good at math in high school, watch the video here!

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4bzSFNCiKrk&list=PL300nJOfVNKZH3H-2J_g1bZRYOx-dQUp3&index=2

John Baez (Feb 09 2025 at 00:24):

So the big questions are: what's a 'motivic L-function' and what's an 'automorphic L-function'?

I said an L-function amounts to a list of numbers that contains information about a mathematical object. But not just any old object: it has to be a special sort of thing coming from number theory or algebraic geometry. A motivic L-function comes from something called a 'motive', while an automorphic L-function comes from an 'automorphic representation'.

So, to make progress on understanding the Langlands program, it helps to figure out what the hell 'motives' and 'automorphic representations' are.

Near the end of that video I showed you, the guy talking, Andreas Holmstrom, recommends this paper by Matthew Emerton:

• Langlands reciprocity: L-functions, automorphic forms, and Diophantine equations, https://math.uchicago.edu/~emerton/pdffiles/reciprocity.pdf

.

Emerton really helped me when I was trying to understand motivic L-functions, so I find it hilarious that Holmstrom says roughly "If you were going to be stuck on an deserted island, and you could only bring one person with you, and you didn't care about love or friendship or survival, only learning about the Langlands program, who should you bring? Matthew Emerton!"

This paper takes a historical approach. That motivates the Langlands program very well. But the complex winding course of history makes it hard to pick out a clean modern statement of (some tiny chunk of) what the Langlands program is trying to accomplish. In particular, while I understand motives and their L-functions pretty well from a couple years of study, I'm still quite fuzzy about automorphic representations and their L-functions. So I will need to put extra work into those.

(2/3)

John Baez (Feb 09 2025 at 00:26):

One last little thing. People often say something like "every motivic L-function is an automorphic L-function, and vice versa", which is an equation between two functions. But how would you prove this?

Both L-functions are just summaries of information - one about a motive, another about an automorphic representation. So you have to find a way to get from a motive to an automorphic representation, and vice versa. And this is what people are trying to do.

So it's a bit like what people in combinatorics call a 'bijective proof'. You're trying to prove that two sequences of numbers are equal. You show that the first sequence counts the number of gadgets with n elements. You show the second counts the number of widgets with n elements. And then you construct a one-to-one correspondence between gadgets with n elements and widgets with n elements! That gets the job done - and it's also a deeper fact than the mere equation between sequences of numbers.

Category theorists call this 'categorification': instead of proving an equation, you set up an isomorphism, or an equivalence of categories.

This is the attitude I instinctively take when people say "every motivic L-function is an automorphic L-function, and vice versa". But I don't understand the details yet! I can explain motives in a simple way:

• Motivating motives, https://math.ucr.edu/home/baez/motives/

but not automorphic representations.

My friend Bruce Bartlett explained the correspondence in a special case called the Modularity Theorem:

• The Modularity Theorem as a bijection of sets, https://golem.ph.utexas.edu/category/2024/04/the_modularity_theorem_as_a_bi.html

So that's a place to start, though you'll instantly be tested on your knowledge of elliptic curves and modular forms!

John Baez (Feb 10 2025 at 23:42):

I submitted my paper Dirichlet species and arithmetic zeta functions for publication.

John Baez (Feb 18 2025 at 21:13):

Now I'm working on three papers:

1) @Todd Trimble and I are writing a paper tentatively called "2-rigs of representations", but that's not a very descriptive name. The main goal is to prove a conjecture from our last paper with Joe Moeller.

At least since the work of Armand Borel, people have written a lot about algebraic representations of groups of matrices, but more recently they've generalized this to monoids. The category of representations of a monoid forms a '2-rig' using ⊕\oplus⊕ and ⊗\otimes⊗, and we have a plan to characterize some famous monoids in terms of their 2-rigs of representations. This paper handles one conjecture about how this works.

I'll simplify the language a bit:

Conjecture. If kkk is a field of characteristic zero, the category of algebraic representations of the monoid M(n,k)M(n,k)M(n,k) of n×nn \times nn×n matrices with entries in kkk is the free 2-rig on a generator of dimension nnn.

In simple rough terms, this says that the monoid of n×nn \times nn×n matrices is the walking monoid with an nnn-dimensional representation.

But we need to be a bit more precise to make this true: we need algebraic representations. An algebraic representation of this monoid amounts to a monoid homomorphism ρ:M(n,k)→M(m,k)\rho: M(n,k) \to M(m,k)ρ:M(n,k)→M(m,k) where the matrix entries of ρ(T)\rho(T)ρ(T) are polynomials in the matrix entries of TTT. There's an obvious tautological representation 1:M(n,k)→M(n,k)1: M(n,k) \to M(n,k)1:M(n,k)→M(n,k). This is algebraic.

The conjecture says that the 2-rig of all algebraic representations of M(n,k)M(n,k)M(n,k) is generated by the tautological representation. It also says we need just one relation, too, saying that the tautological representation is nnn-dimensional. (More precisely, this relation says the (n+1)st exterior power of the tautological representation is 000.)

To prove this conjecture we use [[Tannaka duality]].

John Baez (Feb 18 2025 at 21:21):

2) @Adittya Chaudhuri and I are working on a paper on categories generated by labeled graphs. I got some ideas started in my blog posts on polarities, but so far he's done most of the writing on the paper.

3) I'm working with @Nathaniel Osgood and @Evan Patterson and @Xiaoyan Li on a paper tentatively called "System dynamics for applied category theorists". This is supposed to present software for working with various diagrams widely used to model systems - stock-flow diagrams, system structure diagrams and causal loop diagrams - in a way that category theorists can understand.

Mike Shulman (Feb 18 2025 at 23:06):

John Baez said:

If kkk is a field of characteristic zero, the category of algebraic representations of the monoid M(n,k)M(n,k)M(n,k) of n×nn \times nn×n matrices with entries in kkk is the free 2-rig on a generator of dimension nnn.

Am I reading that right as implying that those categories of representations are independent of kkk, up to equivalence, as long as kkk has characteristic zero?

Martin Brandenburg (Feb 18 2025 at 23:55):

I assume we implicitly talk about k-linear rig categories here. The category of algebraic representations over k has this property.
The "independence" from k then would mean that all these categories are base changes of the special case of Q\mathbb{Q}Q.

John Baez (Feb 19 2025 at 00:07):

Yeah, sorry - I didn't say what I meant by "2-rig". In my papers with Joe and Todd a 2-rig is a Cauchy complete kkk-linear symmetric monoidal category. So the concept depends on kkk: let's call it a "2-rig over kkk" to be more honest.

However, as Martin notes, for any field kkk of characteristic zero the homomorphism

Q→k \mathbb{Q} \to kQ→k

gives by base change a 2-functor

2RigQ→2Rigk \mathbf{2Rig}_{\mathbb{Q}} \to \mathbf{2Rig}_{k} 2RigQ​→2Rigk​

and this 2-functor maps "the free 2-rig over Q\mathbb{Q}Q on an object of dimension nnn" to "the free 2-rig over kkk on an object of dimension nnn".

So the dependence on kkk is very mild: when we switch from Q\mathbb{Q}Q to some larger field kkk of characteristic zero, we just fatten up the hom-vector-spaces in our 2-rig, doing k⊗Q− k \otimes_{\mathbb{Q}} - k⊗Q​− to change them from vector spaces over Q\mathbb{Q}Q to vector spaces over kkk.

John Baez (Feb 19 2025 at 00:12):

There are plenty of groups whose representation theory is quite sensitive to the choice of field, even in the realm of fields with characteristic zero. But Todd and I are focused on gadgets where the representation theory works very uniformly for fields of characteristic zero.

It's fun to see how the representations of the group Z/3\mathbb{Z}/3Z/3 are very different depending on whether you're working over Q\mathbb{Q}Q or C\mathbb{C}C, while for S3S_3S3​ they work the same way over all fields of characteristic zero.

Kevin Carlson (Feb 19 2025 at 00:21):

The homomorphism k→Qk\to \mathbb{Q}k→Q? Surely you wanted the other way around?

John Baez (Feb 19 2025 at 00:21):

Fixed!

Mike Shulman (Feb 19 2025 at 01:47):

Ah, I see. I don't suppose we could call that something like a "kkk-2-algebra"?

John Baez (Feb 19 2025 at 02:42):

Sure, but this is our third paper on 2-rigs so we're going to use that term. By the way, I think @Todd Trimble and @fosco have been developing a kind of adjustable theory of 2-rigs where you get to say the base of enrichment, and the kinds of colimits they need to have, and maybe also whether the tensor product is monoidal or symmetric monoidal or cartesian. Working out the best terminology for all these things is not for me!

David Corfield (Feb 19 2025 at 06:00):

I like how in his book Representation Theory of Finite Monoids, Benjamin Steinberg writes:

The direct catalyst for writing this treatise was the following question, asked on MathOverflow on August 30, 2010 [Mik10]: Why aren’t representations of monoids studied so much? This book may very well be the longest answer ever given to a MathOverflow question!

MO question.

I was looking in that direction recently because people looking to get some understanding of neural networks are deploying quiver representation theory. We even have Quiver neural networks now.

That got me wondering whatever happened to 'category representation theory', and hence to monoid representations.

John Baez (Feb 19 2025 at 06:11):

From what I've seen so far, I really like Lex E. Renner's book Linear Algebraic Monoids. He shows that a lot of the representation theory of linear algebraic groups (subgroups of invertible linear transformations of a finite-dimensional vector space that are picked out by polynomial equations) generalize elegantly to linear algebraic monoids. The theory of roots and weights, etc.

For formal aspects of the theory I prefer to work a bit more generally with affine monoids, i.e. monoids in the category of affine schemes. But for detailed results people prefer monoids in the category of affine algebraic varieties, which are a bit less general. I believe these turn out to all be equivalent to linear algebraic monoids.

John Baez (Feb 19 2025 at 06:19):

Our paper 2-rig extensions and the splitting principle sets up the very basics of the representation theory of affine categories, i.e. categories enriched in affine schemes. A great example is the category FinVect, which can be given such an enrichment. Representations of FinVect should play a really fundamental role in the whole theory! But after fiddling around with these, we turned out not to need them for our main results, and our paper was getting too long, so we shelved them. Now Todd has some new ideas on them.

John Baez (Feb 19 2025 at 06:20):

By the way, all this 'affine scheme' baloney is a way of making things easy to control, much as Lie groups are easier to handle than other infinite groups.

John Baez (Feb 19 2025 at 07:29):

On another note....

The asteroid posing the greatest threat of collision is called 2024 YR₄. Today the probability that it hits the Earth on December 22nd, 2032 went up from 2.6% to 3.1%.

It's 55 meters across, and the impact would release energy equal to an 7.8-megaton bomb.

Its threat level is 3 on the Palermo scale, which means:

"A close encounter, meriting attention by astronomers. Current calculations give a 1% or greater chance of collision capable of 𝙡𝙤𝙘𝙖𝙡𝙞𝙯𝙚𝙙 𝙙𝙚𝙨𝙩𝙧𝙪𝙘𝙩𝙞𝙤𝙣. Most likely, new telescopic observations will lead to reassignment to Level 0. Attention by public and by public officials is merited if the encounter is less than 10 years away."

For more information go here:

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2024_YR4

or the Center for Near Earth Object Studies page on this particular asteroid:

https://cneos.jpl.nasa.gov/sentry/details.html#?des=2024%20YR4

John Baez (Feb 26 2025 at 01:16):

Hurrah!

Dear Sir or Madam,

The processed visa application for GWF reference number GWF081091491 is dispatched via courier on 02/25/2025 19:24:21. Please note this is an auto generated e-mail. Please do NOT reply this email.

That should be my UK visa. So I'll get it well in time for starting work in Edinburgh on May 1st.

Even better, Lisa got the same sort of message, so she'll get her passport back in time to attend a conference in Beijing for a week in late March. This in turn will free me to go up to the Topos Institute in Berkeley. I'm thinking of something roughly like March 23-29. I'd like to take a train but it seems rather difficult, albeit beautiful.

(The difficult part is making the connection between the few Riverside-Los Angeles trains and the few Los Angeles-Oakland trains.)

John Baez (Feb 26 2025 at 05:49):

I realize now I may not have said much about my job in Edinburgh because I didn't want to publicly count my chickens before the eggs were hatched.

My position is called the Maxwell Fellowship for Public Engagement, and I'll be working half for the School of Mathematics and half for the School of Physics and Astronomy. I have a 5-year contract, and by the end of that Lisa and I should get the right to remain in the UK.

Morgan Rogers (he/him) (Feb 26 2025 at 09:29):

I don't know if you should be publicly sharing your visa reference number @John Baez ? (While I don't know how this code could be used to nefarious ends, it still seems like something you should treat as sensitive personal info!)

John Baez (Feb 26 2025 at 16:50):

Thanks. I think my passport has been sent back to me, but now if someone does something nefarious I'll know it was you. :wink:

John Baez (Feb 26 2025 at 20:33):

I wrote three posts on politics:

  • The Dark Enlightenment.

Lots of good comments.

Kevin Carlson (Feb 26 2025 at 22:01):

Yarvin's whole theory of an archipelago of unlimitedly sovereign presumably-authoritarian states with an absolute right to exit is very rapidly self-defeating--who enforces the right to exit? At some point he was raving about having a space UN with big lasers to enforce that the terrestrial states couldn't break those foundational rules, or something; it's a deeply silly defense mechanism against admitting that illiberal regimes, by definition, can control what their citizens do, and there's no way to have a clean escape valve from that.

John Baez (Feb 26 2025 at 23:02):

His thoughts on politics looked like childish ravings to me the moment I saw them, but I hadn't seen his idea of an (obviously incorruptible) "space UN" enforcing laws with lasers from on high.

Kevin Carlson (Feb 26 2025 at 23:03):

I think they’re inherently incorruptible because they’re immeasurably more powerful than the earthlings :upside_down:

David Michael Roberts (Feb 26 2025 at 23:14):

The idea that there would be collusion between the powerful gov-corps so they they support each other's oppression was immediate to me. I live in a place where you can't go to another country without a nontrivial amount of money (I never went to another country until I was in my 20s), all of which are overseas and the easiest way to go is to fly. The land is generally so sparsely populated and/or inhospitable that if you lived in, say, the Perth gov-corp, and it was a despotic outfit, you could very easily be held there without much recourse to leave.

David Michael Roberts (Feb 26 2025 at 23:15):

Also, didn't he just re-invent the concept of the Italian city-states system?

John Baez (Feb 26 2025 at 23:52):

Indeed, my reaction was "been there, done that!" The idea of authoritarian city-states was extensively tested from the middle ages on to the Renaissance and beyond, so we know a lot about what kind of things happen. That's why I said

Remember, Bach was jailed by the Duke of Saxe-Weimar for trying to leave town for a different job.

In his day you often couldn't quit your job, leave a town and work somewhere else unless you got your lord's permission!

John Baez (Feb 26 2025 at 23:59):

Bach eventually did persuade the duke to let him take a job somewhere else. But many rights we take for granted, like the right to quit our job and move somewhere else, were presumably hard-fought. (I don't know the history of how that happened.)

Amar Hadzihasanovic (Feb 27 2025 at 07:18):

I used to read Yarvin's blog when I was in undergrad, must have been 2011 or so, so hopefully my thoughts on it were not tainted with hindsight about what the alt-right would be up to in the following years. I thought there were interesting things in it, some of which have been "mainstreamed" since and do not look particularly original now, but at the time were not so commonly discussed; this is to say that its appeal doesn't seem unreasonable to me. Of course it was mixed with some inane stuff and some downright morally despicable stuff.

Amar Hadzihasanovic (Feb 27 2025 at 07:26):

I thought the "formalist" international order was one of the interesting things, if anything as a thought experiment.
I don't recall the "space UN with lasers"; my recollection is that the proposal was a system not unlike the one we have now, simply founded on a different set of "common values".
Essentially, whereas the current international order is based on the idea of "universal human rights" that must be enforced—which Yarvin sees as a dangerous crypto-Christian ideology, mutated from certain American branches of protestantism—the formalist international order would pretty much only hold the "right to exit" as universal. So, in the same way that currently the UN can (in principle) use sanctions, economic pressure, and eventually the threat of an international military intervention to "police" violations of human rights, the same instruments could be used to police the violations of the right to exit; but no sanction should exist for any "internal matters".
This doesn't guarantee efficacy but the point is, it's not less effective in principle than the system we have (or at least used to have).

Amar Hadzihasanovic (Feb 27 2025 at 07:33):

The other point on which Yarvin was somewhat "ahead of his time" is the one I just briefly mentioned, that is, the claim that "liberal values" (including the stuff which now gets put under the name of "woke") are essentially a memetic mutation of Christian values. He used this in polemic with the "new atheists" such as Dawkins: as in, you cannot see that the "rationalist" values that you hold are actually a minor rearrangement of the particular religious mindset that you claim to be against.
It's interesting that, in fact, since then, Dawkins and a bunch of "new atheists" have made the same extra step, and are now militant against "wokeism" on the same side as Yarvin and the new right-wingers.

Amar Hadzihasanovic (Feb 27 2025 at 07:41):

I think the inane and despicable stuff was, in fact, precisely in what puts the "Enlightenment" into "dark Enlightenment" (and which is why I think the word is quite tainted and would discourage John from using it), that is, a particular reading of "rationalism" which translates into stuff like scientific racism or gender determinism.
(I note that scientific racism was basically invented by the original-generation Enlightenment thinkers, so this is indeed a development in the spirit of Enlightenment)

Amar Hadzihasanovic (Feb 27 2025 at 07:43):

Basically, the idea that "reason", "science" and "facts" put us in front of certain "hard truths", which may be at odds with [choose whether "woke" or "religious"] moralism, and that in order to progress as a society we need to take a "scientific" approach which makes these part of the calculation.

Amar Hadzihasanovic (Feb 27 2025 at 07:47):

Usually (surprise!) these "facts" are things like "black people are simply incapable of making it on their own, the data says it:cry:"

Amar Hadzihasanovic (Feb 27 2025 at 07:47):

Or "women just cannot reach the heights of genius that men can:sob:"

Amar Hadzihasanovic (Feb 27 2025 at 07:54):

By the way, this stuff, and its "lite" version like the obsession with IQ and intelligence metrics, is pretty transversal, which is what I think makes it particularly dangerous. In fact I would say it is the one thing I have noticed unites most self-proclaimed "pro-Enlightenment" people both on the right and the (centre-)left [in my experience actual leftists are usually free of this particular sin, although you never know].

Amar Hadzihasanovic (Feb 27 2025 at 08:00):

I'm thinking of how e.g. Scott Aaronson in his blog often claims to hold "normal Enlightenment values", which he obviously considers to include forms of sexism and scientific racism, despite being a "hardcore anti-Trump rationalist liberal".

Amar Hadzihasanovic (Feb 27 2025 at 08:30):

Also @John Baez , returning to "O.G." Enlightenment, it should not be underappreciated how much it loved autocrats! Voltaire thought that Peter and Catherine the Great were the best thing since sliced bread. I think one could make the argument that a "free-rein" autocrat like Trump reshaping society with the "enlightened" counsel of the "aristocrat of thought" Elon Musk is quite close to Voltaire's ideal model of government.

John Baez (Mar 12 2025 at 17:39):

Next week Tuesday someone from the UCR radio station, KUCR, plans to interview me... about my music! Later he plans to play some of my music and this interview. I'm really excited, because nobody has ever shown signs of giving a damn about my music. (I have not tried to impose it on people, and there really is too much music out there now....)

Morgan Rogers (he/him) (Mar 12 2025 at 20:01):

I am amused by the last parenthesis, as if there was ever a time in the past fifty years when there wasn't more music in the world than anyone could hope to listen to :laughing:

John Baez (Mar 12 2025 at 20:31):

I'm the sort who would easily use "now" to cover the last 50 years. But there's much more recorded music since the rise of digital audio workstations: in 2024, more music was released in a day than in the whole year of 1989.

Basically, we're approaching the point where everyone who wants to record music can. Since creating one's own music can be more fun than listening to other people's music, this is okay... as long as most people creating it don't expect many others to listen to it. It's almost like the high-tech version of sitting around a campfire singing songs.

Morgan Rogers (he/him) (Mar 12 2025 at 20:41):

Right, that statistic is certainly very inflated by the fact that there was no formal trace of the people making music in 1989 without a digital platform to release it onto.

David Michael Roberts (Mar 12 2025 at 21:18):

When John is used to discussing medieval modes in music and tuning theory through the centuries, "now" has a different meaning :-)

John Baez (Mar 14 2025 at 18:11):

In the talk I gave today I explained the McGee graph, the unique graph where each vertex has 3 neighbors and the shortest cycles have length 7, animated here by Mamuka Jibladze:

If you click, you'll see it move. Each vertex comes back to its original position after two turns, so this illustrates an 8-fold symmetry of the McGee graph.

The symmetry group of the McGee graph has order 32, and Greg Egan and I figured out what's going on with this group, though I'd still like a simpler construction of the McGee graph based on this group.

This group, let's call it the McGee group, is the semidirect product of Z/8\mathbb{Z}/8Z/8 with its automorphism group. Alternatively, it's the group of affine transformations of Z/8\mathbb{Z}/8Z/8, i.e. transformations

x→ax+b x \to a x + b x→ax+b

where a,b∈Z/8a,b \in \mathbb{Z}/8a,b∈Z/8 and aaa is invertible, so a=1,3,5,7a = 1, 3, 5, 7a=1,3,5,7. That gives 32 elements.

When I mentioned this stuff, someone said that this group is the smallest group GGG with an outer automorphism f:G→Gf: G \to Gf:G→G that maps each element of GGG to an element in the same conjugacy class! So for each g∈Gg \in Gg∈G we have

f(g)=hgh−1 f(g) = h g h^{-1} f(g)=hgh−1

for some h∈Gh \in Gh∈G, but we can't use the same hhh for all ggg! Let's call this a pseudo-inner automorphism.

Puzzle. Explicitly describe the pseudo-inner automorphism of the McGee group.

There should be a nice description in terms of Z/8\mathbb{Z}/8Z/8, and there should also be a visually nice description of how to turn any symmetry ggg of the McGee graph into a new symmetry that is conjugate to ggg, but not via an inner automorphism.

John Baez (Mar 15 2025 at 00:25):

After a longish hiatus I seem to be giving some talks:

  • Tuesday March 2th - at 11 am I'll be giving the Berkeley Seminar at the Topos Institute. Not sure what I'll talk about yet! I'm tempted to talk about "Doing Science When Science is Under Threat".

  • Monday June 28th - Friday July 4th, 2025 - I'll be giving a mini-course in a Summer School on Algebra at the Zografou campus of the National Technical University of Athens (NTUA). My host is Χριστίνα Βασιλακοπούλου, who once was a postdoc at U.C. Riverside providing key insights to develop the theory of structured cospans. I'm not sure what I'll talk about yet! I get the feeling the students will be first-year grad students who like algebra, so maybe a bit about representations of finite groups would be good for them. I could use this opportunity to talk about decategorification, and how C⊗K(Rep(G))≅C[G]G\mathbb{C} \otimes K(\text{Rep}(G))\cong \mathbb{C}[G]^G C⊗K(Rep(G))≅C[G]G (the basic fact about characters, where the exponent means the subspace invariant under conjugation, which provides an action of GGG on its group algebra C[G]\mathbb{C}[G]C[G].

Alex Kreitzberg (Mar 15 2025 at 04:51):

I hang out in the Illustrating Math Discord, and I was excited to see your talk there.

I'll ask a question here since I didn't find time to squeeze it there.

You introduced a metaphor that was something like "there are dots with many lines through them, and lines with many dots, you can study math from either the dots or the lines"

Where dots were concrete math, and lines were abstract math.

Do you have a concrete example that consistently surprises you? Where no matter how often you come back to look at it, you learn something new about it?

What "point of mathematics" has the most, or at least a surprising number of, lines going through it?

Thanks again for the talk, I like how you contextualized symmetry as broadly useful because it nicely fits in category theory.

John Baez (Mar 16 2025 at 00:08):

I'm glad you liked my talk! For those who didn't attend, in my talk I started by saying that either Cayley or Sylvester said something roughly like

Mathematics is not only the study of general theorems: it is also the study of interesting individuals.

I can't find the quote, even though it's on my web pages somewhere.

Theorems are like lines, individual mathematical entities are like points, and a theorem applying to some entity is like a line going through a point.

John Baez (Mar 16 2025 at 00:11):

Anyway, yes, there are a bunch of entities that keep surprising me as I keep finding new theorems that go through them. One of my favorites is the number 24.

John Baez (Mar 17 2025 at 22:27):

I posted about my dream of a "new Enlightenment" and the counter-dream that seems to be gaining ground temporarily:

  • The dark enlightenment.

I disabled comments on that blog article, because people get so angry about politics, but someone emailed me a thoughtful reply which I posted today:

  • Fred Mott, Critique of Yarvin's system.

It triggered a discussion which is quite reasonable and friendly so far.

John Baez (Mar 17 2025 at 22:31):

Folks at the Topos Institute liked two ideas for talks I might given when I visit next week: a talk about my work with @Adittya Chaudhuri on graphs with polarities, and a talk on "science in an era of increasing authoritarianism". Unfortunately I haven't yet prepared either of these talks, so I don't think I can do a good job on two. Since the work with Adittya is still underway, and the second topic is so urgent, I think I'll talk about the second one.

John Baez (Mar 19 2025 at 06:07):

Okay, I spent the day preparing my talk for the 'Berkeley Seminar' at the Topos Institute (Tuesday March 25th, 2025):

Science Under Authoritarian Rule

While the Topos Institute seeks to realize a world where "the systems that surround us benefit us all", others with a very different vision now rule the United States. What is happening, why is it happening, and what can we do about it? These are hard questions. I will try to present some thoughts, and look forward to hearing yours.

John Baez (Mar 19 2025 at 06:07):

It will not be televised....

Kevin Carlson (Mar 19 2025 at 07:17):

Is that hinting at a converse to the saying about the revolution?

John Baez (Mar 19 2025 at 18:11):

Yes.

John Baez (Mar 20 2025 at 16:38):

For several years I ran a blog called Visual Insight, which was a place to share striking images that help explain topics in mathematics.  Last week I gave this talk about it at the Illustrating Math Seminar:

Visual insights

John Baez (Mar 20 2025 at 16:39):

It was fun showing people the great images created by Refurio Anachro, Greg Egan, Roice Nelson, Gerard Westendorp and other folks. For more info on the images I talked about, go here:

• 2015 – 01 – 15 — Hammersley Sofa
• 2015 – 12 – 01 — Golay Code
• 2014 – 07 – 15 — {7,3} Tiling
• 2014 – 03 – 15 — {6,3,3} Honeycomb
• 2013 – 09 – 15 — {6,3,3} Honeycomb in Upper Half Space
• 2015 – 12 – 15 — Kaleidocycle
• 2016 – 02 – 15 — 27 Lines on a Cubic Surface
• 2015 – 04 – 15 — Sphere in Mirrored Spheroid
• 2015 – 10 – 01 — Balaban 10-Cage
• 2015 – 09 – 15 — McGee Graph

David Michael Roberts (Mar 24 2025 at 23:58):

I was thinking I might try to track down the quote you made, John atthe start of your talk, about general patterns and interesting individuals - and found that Cayley's collected papers are 967 papers published in 13 volumes (!) They are findable on the Internet Archive, and searchable, but one has to just open everything titled "The Collected Mathematical Papers of Arthur Cayley", as the volume numbers aren't given. I haven't looked in them all, yet!

Sylvester's mathematical papers are in four volumes, each a bit bigger, but are easier to access. I can't find the quote looking for key words ('general', 'patterns', 'individuals') in his books.

John Baez (Mar 25 2025 at 04:11):

Thanks, @David Michael Roberts! It's possible all the keywords are wrong, even if the overall meaning is as I remember it. I too have failed to find the quote - which is especially infuriating because I think I put it at the bottom of some issue of This Week's Finds.

John Baez (Mar 25 2025 at 04:13):

John Baez (Apr 04 2025 at 00:32):

A couple of things:

  • I had a great visit to the Topos Institute. @Evan Patterson, @Kevin Carlson and @Sophie Libkind explained the basics of @David Jaz Myers's double categorical systems theories to me. Evan and Kevin also explained double theories, which are fundamental to their new CatColab software. Sophie also explained the operad of directed wiring diagrams. I'd like to blog about all this stuff sometime.

  • I copied all 82 posts from my AMS blog Visual Insight to my own website, because I don't trust the stability of any platform anymore. It took a fair amount of work to reformat them. I can imagine paying someone to turn them into LaTeX so I could make a book of them and put it on the arXiv. Right now they are in an unpleasantly complicated HTML format, much more gnarly than This Week's Finds.

John Baez (Apr 07 2025 at 18:36):

  • @Todd Trimble and I have come out with a new paper on categorified rigs in representation theory! We prove a conjecture from an earlier paper characterizing the monoid of n×nn \times nn×n matrices in terms of its category of representations, which is a '2-rig'. This is the first and easiest of a series of similar conjectures.

  • John Baez and Todd Trimble, Tannaka reconstruction and the monoid of matrices.

Settling a conjecture from an earlier paper, we prove that the monoid M(n,k)\text{M}(n,k)M(n,k) of n×nn \times nn×n matrices in a field kkk of characteristic zero is the 'walking monoid with an nnn-dimensional representation'. More precisely, if we treat M(n,k)\text{M}(n,k)M(n,k) as a monoid in affine schemes, the 2-rig Rep(M(n,k))\mathsf{Rep}(\text{M}(n,k))Rep(M(n,k)) of algebraic representations of M(n,k)\text{M}(n,k)M(n,k) is the free 2-rig on an object x with Λn+1(x)≅0\Lambda^{n+1}(x) \cong 0Λn+1(x)≅0. Here a 2-rig is a symmetric monoidal kkk-linear category that is Cauchy complete. Our proof uses Tannaka reconstruction and a general theory of quotient 2-rigs and 2-ideals. We conclude with a series of conjectures about the universal properties of representation 2-rigs of classical groups.

John Baez (Apr 15 2025 at 20:40):

My paper Groupoid cardinality and random permutations got accepted by Theory and Applications of Categories.

John Baez (Apr 21 2025 at 20:37):

The proposal for @Nathaniel Osgood, @Xiaoyan Li @Evan Patterson, @Kris Brown, @Owen Haaga, @ww and me to meet in Edinburgh for 6 weeks and use category theory to develop new modeling software has gotten accepted by the International Centre for Mathematical Sciences' "Mathematics for Humanity" program! We'll do it either May 15 - June 26 or June 9 - July 21 this year.

Here's the short description:

Many of the most urgent social, economic and public health problems facing humanity require dynamic simulation modeling for a well-informed response. Sadly, most current models are labour intensive to build, difficult to reuse or adapt, and reliant on proprietary software. Our team has used modern mathematics to develop flexible open-source frameworks for "stock and flow models", which treat populations en masse, and also "agent-based models" or "ABMs" which simulate each individual separately. In our meeting we will develop new mathematics and create new software to design and work with "hybrid" models which combine stock and flow models and ABMs. To illustrate the power of this new software, we will create demonstration models of 1) gestational and type II diabetes, 2) labour shocks as the economy decarbonizes. Our team includes mathematicians, software engineers, computer scientists who specialize in epidemiology and public health, and an economist.

Here's the whole proposal in case anyone cares:

  • Toward hybrid modeling.

John Baez (Apr 27 2025 at 19:29):

I'm giving a few talks after I go to Edinburgh:

  • Monday May 12, 2025 - I'll be giving a talk at 2 pm Pacific Time, or 10 pm UK time. The talk is for physics students at the Universidade de São Paulo in Brazil, and my contact is Artur Renato Baptista Boyago. I don't know if a zoom link will be made widely available, or whether the talk will be put on YouTube. I should find out.

Visions for the Future of Physics

The 20th century was the century of fundamental physics. What about the 21st? Progress on fundamental physics has been slow since about 1980, but there is exciting progress in other fields, such as condensed matter physics. Most of all, physicists in the 21st century should embrace the challenges of the Anthropocene — and face up to the challenges of authoritarian rule.

John Baez (Apr 27 2025 at 19:33):

  • Wednesday May 14, 2025 - Hassaan Saleem will interview me for his physics podcast called Phymaths at 4 pm UK time. He wrote: "I want to invite you to be a guest on one of our podcast episodes. In this episode, I want to discuss your work on GUTs, LQG (and its relation to Black Hole Entropy), higher gauge theory, category theory and TQFTs. I also want to discuss your journey as a science writer (including your series "This Week's Finds in Mathematical Physics" and your blog). In this aspect, I would like to discuss your thoughts about the features that make a piece of scientific writing more engaging and readable for the community."

John Baez (Apr 27 2025 at 19:35):

  • June 2, 2025 - There will be a workshop called Transactions in Category Theory from 10 am to 6 pm UK time at the University of Strathclyde, Royal College 512 in Glasgow, Scotland. I'm giving a talk at 2 pm UK times, for one hour including questions:

Graphs with Polarities

In fields ranging from business to systems biology, directed graphs with edges labeled by signs are used to model systems in a very simple way: the nodes represent entities of some sort, and an edge indicates that one entity directly affects another either positively or negatively. Multiplying the signs along a directed path of edges lets us determine indirect positive or negative effects, and if the path is a loop we call this a positive or negative feedback loop. Here we generalize this to graphs with edges labeled by a commutative monoid, whose elements represent "polarities" possibly more general than simply "positive" or "negative". We define a "1st homology monoid" of a directed graph, different from the usual 1st homology group in that it only detects directed loops. Finally, we describe the emergence of new feedback loops when we compose open graphs using a variant of the Mayer-Vietoris exact sequence for homology. This is joint work with Adittya Chaudhuri.

John Baez (May 02 2025 at 18:39):

Yesterday I started my new job as Maxwell Fellow here in Edinburgh. I want to stop being such a hermit and go to some talks. Here's one that looks interesting:

Wednesday, May 7, 13:00-14:00

Location: 40 George Square 11.18
Description: Speaker: Luke K. Davis (University of Edinburgh)
Title: Towards the efficient control of active matter
Abstract: In this talk I will outline my latest and current work on the statistical physics of active matter, mainly on how to control it. I will show the essential “under the hood” machinery of a versatile framework for the control of nonequilibrium systems with internal driving, that exhibit discrete and continuous states. The framework combines recent developments in stochastic thermodynamics and response theory. I will then briefly present proofs of concepts of this framework, through the solution of a semi-analytical one-body control problem, involving a trapped active particle, and a many-body control problem involving active collectives that phase separate.

John Baez (May 02 2025 at 18:40):

Active matter is a fashionable new topic in physics.

John Baez (May 07 2025 at 21:02):

Good news - after lots of delay the ICMS (= International Centre for Mathematical Sciences) has given our categorical epidemiological group a specific time and location to meet!

We'll be meeting from May 26th to June 26th on the first floor of Maxwell's old house in Edinburgh, now the location of the James Clerk Maxwell Foundation.

Some other good news: Owen Haaga, the economist in our groups, says that J. Doyne Farmer, the director of the Complexity Economics programme at the Institute for New Economic Thinking at Oxford, wants me to come down and give a talk sometime. I should combine this with a visit to Topos Research UK, where David Jaz Myers, Owen Lynch, and others are currently working on "double categorical systems theory for safeguarded AI". Also Jeremy Gibbons has invited me down there.

John Baez (May 10 2025 at 15:26):

Here are the slides for the talk I'm giving on Monday. I still haven't heard if this talk will show up on YouTube, but I pressed the organizer to make that happen.

Visions for the Future of Physics

The 20th century was the century of fundamental physics. What about the 21st? Progress on fundamental physics has been slow since about 1980, but there is exciting progress in other fields, such as condensed matter physics. Most of all, physicists in the 21st century should embrace the challenges of the Anthropocene — and face up to the challenges of authoritarian rule.

I found it so depressing to talk about the combined challenge of authoritarian rule and climate change that I decided to say just a bit about that near the start, and focus on physics.

I will ask the Brazilian students to help teach USians how to get rid of an authoritarian leader.

John Baez (May 13 2025 at 22:25):

On Wednesday May 14, 2025 I’ll be giving my talk "Visions for the Future of Physics" (see above) at 2 pm Pacific Time, or 10 pm UK time. The talk is for physics students at the Universidade de São Paulo in Brazil, organized by Artur Renato Baptista Boyago.

You can join at this Zoom link:

https://usp-br.zoom.us/j/85955083127?pwd=zXDwrPoMZXnbcffaqCOEb5aAWokBix.1

David Corfield (May 14 2025 at 06:44):

image.png

And beyond K-theory to some extraordinary nonabelian cohomology theory, such as 2-cohomotopy:

image.png

if our old co-blogger is to be believed (paper).

John Baez (May 14 2025 at 09:24):

That's a fun-looking paper which I'd never dared examine before. At some point it starts discussing denotational semantics and replacing "sets of worlds" with "groupoids of worlds". This will elude most existing condensed matter physicists. But it does testable predictions, so if Urs' ideas ever catch on I imagine it could be here, in the fractional quantum Hall effect. Unlike string theory or M-theory in high-energy physics, here we have a real-world system where people can do experiments.

My own talk is mainly aimed at the less mathematical side of physics, since I don't know who will be attending except that they're physicists. And I don't think the future of physics is higher categories, except in part. I think it's the move beyond 'fundamental' laws to the study of designed systems that are approximately described by laws we get to choose to a large extent - even laws that violate basic principles like conservation of energy. This opens the field immensely. It's almost terrifying.

Adittya Chaudhuri (May 14 2025 at 13:04):

Sometimes I wonder, "whether every single law/phenomena is a trivial case of some higher law". I am not only talking about physics, but biology, sociology, ..etc in general. I developed this intuition from the kinds of "categorification" I had been exposed to. It makes me wonder, whether, that `higher law' can be considered as a model for the purpose of the lower law. I think (although not sure) "the notion of purpose" doesn't makes much sense in physics, but I think it does makes sense in social systems, political systems, economic systems, or, most systems created by humans.

I may be completely wrong, as "what I described" is at the moment just a feeling.

John Baez (May 14 2025 at 13:21):

I would have to see an example of what you're saying to understand what you mean by "higher law" and also "purpose of the lower law".

I'm reminded of a story I heard last night. A computer scientist was explaining computer proofs of the 4-color theorem. A mathematician in the audience asked whether these computer proofs give us an understanding of the theorem. The computer scientist replied, "What do you mean by 'understanding'?"

At first I thought this was a trick to point out how little we know about "understanding". But the person telling the story seemed to believe that the computer scientist didn't even grasp the mathematician's question: for them, the point of a proof was simply to be sure that that theorem is true.

This isn't very relevant, but I feel like the computer scientist when I'm asking your comment means. Do laws have a "purpose"?

Adittya Chaudhuri (May 14 2025 at 13:57):

Thanks!! I understood the point and the moral of the story. Unfortunately, I also do not know much about "laws with purpose", but I can try explaining what I am thinking in this direction:

Consider the diagram attached.
Purpose.PNG

Here, I have drawn a Heafliger path in the Lie groupoid [X1⇉X0][X_1 \rightrightarrows X_0][X1​⇉X0​], where the blue portion represents smooth paths in in the manifold X0X_0X0​ and the the red portions represent elements of X1X_1X1​ which are compatible with source/target and initial/final points.

Now, say, a particle is moving across this Haefliger paths in the Lie groupoid, however, we can only see the "blue portion", and we are not aware that the oveeall trajectory of the particle is guided by a "higher law" i.e a path in Lie groupoid [X1⇉X0][X_1 \rightrightarrows X_0][X1​⇉X0​]. In a way, we are making our interpretations and assesments on only the basis of the "visible" blue portions.

In the above context, I am interpreting, the "visible portion of the particle's movement has a purpose, and that purpose is to satisfy the definition of a Haefliger path in the Lie groupoid [X1⇉X0][X_1 \rightrightarrows X_0][X1​⇉X0​].

Now, from this perspective, I am thinking, social laws in a community are creataed to maintain the stability/ some other properties of a bigger community, but when we are only following the laws of subcommunities we do not feel the existence of "this purpose-maintining the stability/other properties of a bigger community"

David Corfield (May 14 2025 at 16:15):

John Baez said:

At some point it starts discussing denotational semantics and replacing "sets of worlds" with "groupoids of worlds".

I think you're looking at a paper that's referenced, rather than the one directly concerning FQH here. This latter seems more clearly straight mathematical physics.

John Baez said:

But it does testable predictions, so if Urs' ideas ever catch on I imagine it could be here, in the fractional quantum Hall effect.

One advantage here is that although the work is downstream of their M-theory work, the lack of dynamics we discussed recently is not an issue, it is claimed,

because contrary to what TQFT theorists tend to wish for, quantum materials in the lab tend to just sit there and not change their topology over time.

John Baez (May 14 2025 at 17:17):

That doesn't mean dynamics is not an issue! The topology of the chunk of matter isn't changing, but the electrons and flux quanta are still moving around, and a theory of the fractional quantum Hall effect is supposed to say how.

John Baez (May 14 2025 at 17:19):

There are some predictions you can make in absence of any theory of dynamics, but the big fat book Quantum Hall Effects (the one in my talk slides) is full of dynamics: equations saying how things change with time.

John Baez (May 14 2025 at 17:47):

Mind you, I don't think Schreiber and Sati are denying the importance of dynamics in the fractional quantum Hall effect. But they're focused on flux quantization, which is something else. I believe they're treating it as a kinematical issue, i.e., part of the description of what the system's state can be at any moment in time.
