Morgan Rogers (he/him) (May 15 2025 at 11:08):

John Baez said:

I'm reminded of a story I heard last night. A computer scientist was explaining computer proofs of the 4-color theorem. A mathematician in the audience asked whether these computer proofs give us an understanding of the theorem. The computer scientist replied, "What do you mean by 'understanding'?"

At first I thought this was a trick to point out how little we know about "understanding". But the person telling the story seemed to believe that the computer scientist didn't even grasp the mathematician's question: for them, the point of a proof was simply to be sure that that theorem is true.

Google released some results derived by "Alpha Evolve" of packing and matrix multiplication problems in the past day or so. Hearing of this brought to mind a very similar contrast between different perspectives on what proofs or solutions are "for"...

ww (May 15 2025 at 11:15):

A similar thing arises in the very applied area of (machine assisted) risk assessment. The value of assessing risk when you are trying to do a thing is to think that thing through properly. A risk assessment document is mainly to have some evidence that you've done the thinking. The standard of "proof" that you can do the thing safely is very different from proof in mathematics. But increasingly, the focus is on producing the documents... "Ideally" without having to do too much actual thinking, get the computer to do that...

David Michael Roberts (May 15 2025 at 11:26):

@Morgan Rogers (he/him) embarassingly, the big headline claim by DeepMind about improving matrix multiplication for the 4x4 (characteristic 0) case by removing one field multiplication wasn't actually new. The paper claims they made things better from 49 to 48 field multiplications, but such an algorithm was posted on math.SE as an answer in 2014..... And someone tells me results from the late 60s from before the famous Strassen approach was enough for the 4x4 case to give 48 field multiplications, even though the Strassen result would scale to larger matrices by iteration, being possible with non-commutative ring entries (and this is how the 49 mults were arrived at).

Maybe, to be charitable, the DeepMind paper should have said it betters Strassen's technique to arrive at 48 mults using tensor decompositions, rather than just saying it beat this old count and that it "was an open problem" for n decades (50ish years, from memory, is what the paper claims), merely to beat this number at all, by any means.

David Michael Roberts (May 15 2025 at 20:16):

Terry Tao let me know that he spoke to an author on the paper, and they are going to try to get the wording updated to reflect that the result is better than the known method in that class of tensor decomposition methods (the 4x4 multiplication algorithm found by the software is a tensor decomposition, not an arbitrary technique). This will unfortunately have to go through internal review which will be not quick.

David Michael Roberts (May 17 2025 at 04:04):

And now (within 24 hours!) DeepMind has updated the paper so their claim is not false: they specify they are making the first improvement to a decomposition of the multiplication tensor in (M4×4(C)⊗M4×4(C))∗⊗M4×4(C)(M_{4\times 4}(\mathbb{C})\otimes M_{4\times 4}(\mathbb{C}))^*\otimes M_{4\times 4}(\mathbb{C})(M4×4​(C)⊗M4×4​(C))∗⊗M4×4​(C) since Strassen, in a way that gives the 48 mults, not that they are first to get 48 multiplications (and a footnote to the effect that other algorithms exist with that number of operations, just that they aren't tensor decompositions)

John Baez (May 17 2025 at 15:47):

Speaking of correcting errors....

I completed a mildly amusing, mildly depressing job: pointing the world to a 51-year-old paper that rigorously disproves a shocking new claim based on hand-wavy approximate calculations, which has been getting a lot of media attention:

  • Dead stars don't radiate, Azimuth.

In fact I supervised a student whose thesis did an infinitely more skillful job of handling this issue.

Tobias Fritz (May 17 2025 at 16:50):

I don't have a competent opinion on this (let alone skin in the game), but I've always found it counterintuitive that a massive object without a horizon wouldn't emit Hawking radiation. So perhaps this is a good opportunity to make it more intuitive!

To think about this in a setting that is as highly idealized as possible, is it meaningful to work with QFT on a static spacetime without backreaction, and in particular to ignore the loss of mass of the object? This seems to be the setting in which the textbooks derive Hawking radiation, so I suppose that this should work. On the other hand, Schwarzschild spacetime is invariant under time reversal, so one can't really expect to prove that the radiation is outgoing rather than incoming, right?

Then what is the intuition behind why the presence or absence of a horizon afflicts things that happen at spatial infinity? Does it make sense to say that the part of the quantum field outside of the horizon is entangled with the field that's inside, and that such entanglement is responsible for the state in each region being mixed? And that this is analogous to the Unruh effect, where the Rindler horizon plays the same role? I'm a bit skeptical about this being the right intuition because the field should be entangled between any two spatially separated regions, regardless of a horizon.

John Baez (May 17 2025 at 16:58):

To think about this in a setting that is as highly idealized as possible, is it meaningful to work with QFT on a static spacetime without backreaction, and in particular to ignore the loss of mass of the object?

Yes, everyone thinks so, at least for a sun-sized dead star, since the loss of mass is so slow under these conditions.

John Baez (May 17 2025 at 16:59):

(In fact there's none at all, except for a black hole, but those guys claim there's a very slow loss of mass for a dead star that's not a black hole.)

John Baez (May 17 2025 at 17:01):

On the other hand, Schwarzschild spacetime is invariant under time reversal, so one can't really expect to prove that the radiation is outgoing rather than incoming, right?

Hawking did a calculation valid for an astrophysical black hole, which is not invariant under time reversal: it was formed from a star in the past.

But we can also do a calculation for a Schwarzschild black hole if we assume appropriate boundary conditions.

However, it's much easier to think about what these guys are talking about: a static spacetime without an event horizon.

Tobias Fritz (May 17 2025 at 17:01):

Ah, I think I get why my time symmetry argument fails: one considers only the black hole and not the white hole :sweat_smile: (which may be related to the boundary conditions you mentioned)

John Baez (May 17 2025 at 17:09):

Then what is the intuition behind why the presence or absence of a horizon afflicts things that happen at spatial infinity?

I'm not sure "intuition" is what I can provide here.

First, if you've got a static spacetime with a Killing field that's timelike everywhere, and it's orthogonal to a foliation by Cauchy surfaces that are geodesically complete, you can define a single-particle Hilbert space for a massive or massless scalar field, or various other kinds of particles, and you can define a self-adjoint Hamiltonian on the this Hilbert space. Then you can form the Fock space to describe collections of particles, and you automatically get a Hamiltonian on that. So, you can define energy, particles, antiparticles, the vacuum, and so on very much as you can in Minkowski spacetime. The vacuum will be stable.

John Baez (May 17 2025 at 17:11):

If you start dropping some of the hypotheses I listed things get more tricky. But all the hypotheses hold if you've got a spacetime that's asymptotically Minkowskian, with a static chunk of matter in the middle.

Tobias Fritz (May 17 2025 at 17:13):

Got it. I expect that you're going to say that this applies to the dead star, but not to the black hole because Schwarzschild doesn't have a Cauchy surface? Or rather that it only applies to the black hole exterior?

John Baez (May 17 2025 at 17:16):

Right. The Schwarzschild solution breaks the hypotheses I listed quite a lot. For one thing, has a Killing vector field that's timelike outside the event horizon but becomes spacelike inside. If we try to consider only the region outside the horizon that's not a Cauchy surface. Intuitively, if we consider only this region, our scalar field equations will have solutions that "fall into the black hole" or conversely "pop out of the white hole". So we don't get a single-particle Hilbert space with a nice one-parameter group of unitaries describing time evolution - at least not if we only consider the exterior region.

Tobias Fritz (May 17 2025 at 17:17):

Of course, makes sense!

John Baez (May 17 2025 at 17:18):

Someone more dedicated than me must have studied all the variations on this theme that one can consider. I can imagine arguing with myself, saying "but what about...."

But most of the subtleties show up when we consider black holes, not what the authors of the new work are considering, namely static spacetimes without event horizons.

James Deikun (May 17 2025 at 17:21):

Doesn't the Hawking radiation directly toward you disappear if you're in freefall? The way I see it all spacetimes, even flat ones, have (at least the Rindler) horizons in some coordinate systems, and if you assume those systems you get Unruh radiation. The way you get "a little Hawking radiation" from plain matter is actually that you have to accelerate to stay still and that produces Unruh radiation. The static horizon is only an extreme version of this, and the reason the object itself loses mass in that case is that the horizon encloses it instead of being off in the distance. But I'm not a specialist so I can't really back this up.

James Deikun (May 17 2025 at 17:23):

(Note: specifically I can see why from my perspective these guys are still wrong: without a true horizon the radiation doesn't seem to come from near the mass, and the mass won't shrink and break baryon conservation.)

John Baez (May 17 2025 at 17:24):

Doesn't the Hawking radiation directly toward you disappear if you're in freefall?

I believe that as you're falling through the horizon the Hawking radiation is not visible to you - but if you're far away from the horizon you can see it. That is, I could be far from a black hole, very slowly free-falling into it, and I could see the Hawking radiation. In the limit where I'm very far and very slowly falling, this is pretty much what we mean by saying there "is" Hawking radiation.

James Deikun (May 17 2025 at 17:25):

Hm, interesting, that does break my intuition a bit.

Tobias Fritz (May 17 2025 at 17:26):

That's very helpful for intuition -- because what James said is one of the reasons for why I found it intuitive that a dead star would emit Hawking radiation too!

James Deikun (May 17 2025 at 17:28):

It feels weird that the presence of Hawking radiation would depend (discontinuously!) on a global property of spacetime, but in a way that can be detected completely locally. I would have expected a global property like that could only be detected by looking at what happens at a number of disparate points.

John Baez (May 17 2025 at 17:29):

It's actually a lot harder to compute what various observers moving along various paths would "see" than to do other kinds of computations. To do the former we need a model of how the observer, or apparatus, interacts with the quantum field. People do make such models. Then you have to think about how much depends on the particular model!

John Baez (May 17 2025 at 17:31):

It's much easier to show that under all the favorable conditions I listed, there are time-translation-invariant "number operators" that describe the number of particles and antiparticles in space... so the vacuum won't turn into particles and antiparticles as time goes by.

John Baez (May 17 2025 at 17:33):

You could then have fun arguing about how these number operators are or are not connected to what various measuring apparati moving along various paths would detect. But then things get a lot more complicated, and most of the work I've seen becomes a bit less rigorous.

Tobias Fritz (May 17 2025 at 17:33):

James Deikun said:

It feels weird that the presence of Hawking radiation would depend (discontinuously!) on a global property of spacetime

Maybe it's not discontinuous after all, because spacetimes with and without a horizon live in disconnected parts of the moduli space of spacetimes (whatever that means exactly)? I'm not sure, but what's clear is that if you vary the mass of the star, then at the critical value already the classical physics changes discontinuously, namely all the matter in the star gets sucked into the singularity, while below the critical value you still have an extended ball of matter.

John Baez (May 17 2025 at 17:36):

It feels weird that the presence of Hawking radiation would depend (discontinuously!) on a global property of spacetime, but in a way that can be detected completely locally.

I don't really know what that means, especially the "discontinuously" part. If you start with a star that's not a black hole, and you start squeezing it, eventually it will become unstable and collapse into a black hole. By the time the black hole has settled down, the geometry of spacetime is quite different from any spacetime that had a squeezed star in it.

John Baez (May 17 2025 at 17:36):

Maybe I said almost the same thing @Tobias Fritz just said.

John Baez (May 17 2025 at 17:40):

Anyway, there's a big difference between a static spacetime where a particle moving slower than light can go wherever it wants and then go back to where it started, and one where it can't. Hawking radiation doesn't happen in the former case.

James Deikun (May 17 2025 at 17:40):

Hm, but classically in order to detect the difference between a slightly subcritical and slightly supercritical star locally you have to actually fall into the prospective black hole, the gravitational and radiation field outside the Schwarzschild radius varies continuously with slight perturbations of mass.

James Deikun (May 17 2025 at 17:41):

Whereas quantumly it seems like the Hawking radiation would just turn on like a switch as soon as you hit the critical mass.

Tobias Fritz (May 17 2025 at 17:45):

Maybe the Hawking/Unruh radiation that you'd witness at finite distance from the collapsing star is still a continuous function of its mass, while this is not the case at infinite distance? In other words, it might be an instance of the fact that a pointwise limit of continuous functions can fail to be continuous.

John Baez (May 17 2025 at 17:45):

If a dead star is too light it won't collapse and form a black hole, but if it's heavy enough it does. In the first case, however long we wait, we won't see Hawking radiation as we're freely falling into the star from an enormous distance. In the second case we will.

John Baez (May 17 2025 at 17:46):

All questions about what we see "close up" are more complicated - I would need to put pen to paper and suffer for days or weeks.

John Baez (May 17 2025 at 17:47):

I'm a big believer in solving easy problems and then deciding whether I really care enough before moving on to harder ones.

John Baez (May 17 2025 at 17:49):

I'll say this though: there's an extensive literature on this simple-sounding but frustrating puzzle:

Suppose you have a charge held at a fixed distance from the Earth. It's not in free fall so according to general relativity it's accelerating. Therefore it must radiate. But wait - does it?

John Baez (May 21 2025 at 12:38):

No.

Madeleine Birchfield (May 21 2025 at 13:11):

John Baez said:

Suppose you have a charge held at a fixed distance from the Earth. It's not in free fall so according to general relativity it's accelerating.

What if the charge is at a Lagrange point?

John Baez (May 21 2025 at 14:32):

Well, that's interesting. I was talking about a charged chunk of plastic sitting on my table.

A charge orbiting the Earth at an Earth-Moon Lagrange point, say L3 for example, should radiate with a frequency on the order of its period, i.e. 1/month. Since a month is about 2.6 million seconds, it'll radiate with a frequency of about 3.8×10−73.8 \times 10^{-7}3.8×10−7 hertz. That's extremely low, and the power output will also be very low - I'm too lazy to calculate it now - so the radiation will be completely indetectable and for practical purposes nonexistent.

John Baez (May 21 2025 at 14:35):

In fact, when I was considering an electric charge sitting on my table, and saying it doesn't radiate, I was discounting the rotation of the Earth!

Due to that, the charge will actually radiate a microscopic amount with a frequency of about 10−510^{-5}10−5 hertz. But I doubt anyone is able to detect this.

John Baez (May 21 2025 at 14:54):

This fall I need to supervise some undergraduate research projects in the School of Mathematics at the University of Edinburgh, which means I have to propose a couple of projects. Here is one. It's probably too ambitious, but I'm jointly hired by the maths and the School of Physics and Astronomy, so I figured some ambitious student might want to try some serious mathematical physics.

Toward a quantum theory of fluid flow

Project description. An “ideal fluid”, meaning an incompressible fluid with no viscosity, is described by a set of partial differential equations called the Euler equations. While this model of a fluid is so oversimplified that von Neumann jokingly called it the theory of “dry water”, it is mathematically rich, so it should be interesting to attempt to quantize the Euler equations. If this is possible, it would give a theory of a quantum ideal fluid. If impossible, there should be some mathematically interesting reason why it cannot be done.

V. I. Arnol’d showed that the Euler equations describe geodesic motion on a certain infinite-dimensional Lie group, the group of volume-preserving diffeomorphisms of space. Using this fact he showed that the Euler equations in 2-dimensional space are “chaotic”: small changes in the initial conditions lead to exponentially growing changes in what happens later on. This mathematical description may also be the key to an elegant quantization of the Euler equations.

A large part of this project will consist of studying the existing literature on the Euler equations and attempts to quantize them. A bare minimum goal would be to write a clear summary of the state of the art. However, it may well be possible to make further progress involving original research.

Reading material:

Solomon Endlich, Alberto Nicolis, Riccardo Rattazzi and Junpu Wang, The quantum mechanics of perfect fluids, https://arxiv.org/abs/1011.6396

L. D. Landau and E. M. Lifschitz, A Course in Theoretical Physics Part 9: Statistical Physics, Part 2: Theory of the Condensed State, Butterworth-Heinemann, 1980. Section 24: Phonons in a liquid.

P. J. Morrison, Hamiltonian Description of the Ideal Fluid, Reviews of Modern Physics. 70 (1998) 467–521.

Vladimir Igorevich Arnol'd, Mathematical Methods of Classical Mechanics, Springer, 2013. Appendix 2: Geodesics of left-invariant metrics on Lie groups and hydrodynamics of ideal fluids.

John Baez (May 21 2025 at 18:59):

I'm also offering this more open-ended project, for undergrads who have taken @Tom Leinster's category theory course:

Topics in category theory

Description. Category theory is becoming increasingly used in algebra and topology and even some corners of applied mathematics. After a first course in category theory there are many directions one can go: abelian categories, monads, sheaves, monoidal categories, enriched categories and 2-categories are some of the most important. In this project we will tackle a few of these.  A minimum goal in this project is to write an expository article on some topic in category theory; a more ambitious goal would be to do some original research.   Students should have taken a first course in category theory that covers categories, functors and natural transformations, adjunctions, limits and colimits, and have some exposure to abstract algebra and/or topology.

Reading material

Emily Riehl, Category Theory in Context, Dover, New York, 2016.

Francis Borceux, Handbook of Categorical Algebra, Cambridge U. Press, Cambridge, 1994.

John Baez (May 29 2025 at 14:32):

The book "System Archetype Basics" studies 7 troublesome patterns. Here's one:

As the symptom of some problem increases, we work harder at the "fundamental solution" but also the quicker and easier "symptomatic solution". Both of these decrease the symptom of the problem. But the fundamental solution takes longer - there's a delay. Also, the symptomatic solution tends to cause a side-effect that tends to work against the fundamental solution.

The picture here is called a 'causal loop diagram' because it helps you spot feedback loops. Just find a loop in the picture and multiply the plus and minus signs around it to tell if it's a positive or a negative feedback loop!

The math of these things runs deeper than you might think. I'm talking about work I did with @Adittya Chaudhuri at a category theory workshop in Glasgow on Monday June 2nd. It's at 2 pm UK time, and you can watch it on Zoom if you register here:

https://jademaster.xyz/TACT25.html

This workshop is being run by @Jade Master and it's at the University of Strathclyde, Royal College, room RC 512. My long-time n-Category Cafe co-host, the philosopher @David Corfield , will be giving a talk on how modalities in homotopy type theory can be thought of as 'logical', and my friend @Nathaniel Osgood will be talking about compositional modeling in public health. There are also lots of other great talks, so this should be lots of fun (for me at least). Abstracts are here:

https://jademaster.xyz/ListOfAbstracts

and a tentative schedule is here:

https://jademaster.xyz/TACTSchedule.html

I hope to see you there, at least virtually!

David Corfield (May 30 2025 at 10:59):

System Archetypes looks interesting and relevant in all kinds of areas! I wonder if you can see from a category-theoretic perspective what the basic troublesome patterns are - like some sort of classification result.

Do you end up close to the work on motifs in networks?

  • Rebekah Aduddell, James Fairbanks, Amit Kumar, Pablo S. Ocal, Evan Patterson Brandon T. Shapiro, A compositional account of motifs, mechanisms, and dynamics in biochemical regulatory networks (arXiv:2301.01445)

Maybe, I'll just wait until Monday to find out.

John Baez (May 30 2025 at 13:06):

Yes, I was planning to talk about that on Monday. My theory is that motifs in regulatory networks benefit the organisms containing them, while the 7 system archetypes listed in that book are harmful: they were chosen because they are patterns to beware of.

Adittya Chaudhuri (May 30 2025 at 15:35):

I also feel the same:

"motifs in regulatory networks benefit the organisms containing them, while the 7 system archetypes listed in that book are harmful"

However, I was thinking about possible correlations between cancer pathways and how we try to deal with them using targeted therapies.

I think in many cases cancer cells activate/inhbit certain genes (sometimes by mutations) responssible for cell death, cell proliferations and tumor suppressions. etc via specific pathways. Now, in targeted therapies we try to block/inhibit the activity of those mutated genes, but due to adaptative nature of cancer cells, often other alternative pathways may get activated to serve the "purpose of cancer ".

I was trying thinking whether our appraoch through targeted therapy for dealing with cancer is often failing because of the unintended activations of "some variants of those 7 system archetypes" in our body's signal's transduction networks, of which we may not be aware of. This is just a thought!!

David Corfield (May 31 2025 at 07:47):

Here's a different idea. Cancer and autoimmune diseases seem to be 'system failures' of some sort, so maybe they could be detected very abstractly by detecting more 'bad motifs' activated in the gene regulatory network.

You'd imagine there would be a need for a hierarchical approach to say when a system is behaving well or badly. From the perspective of the cancer, things are going well. From the perspective of the cell, in apoptosis things are going badly. I wonder if such a perspective are open to ACT treatment.

As a biologist thinking along these lines, Michael Levin,

Biological systems employ hierarchical regulatory networks to maintain structure and function at multiple scales, from molecules to whole organisms,

who is no stranger to ACT circles, as with his talk to the Topos Institute, has expressed such hopes.

Nathaniel Osgood (May 31 2025 at 18:01):

John Baez said:

Yes, I was planning to talk about that on Monday. My theory is that motifs in regulatory networks benefit the organisms containing them, while the 7 system archetypes listed in that book are harmful: they were chosen because they are patterns to beware of.

For whatever it is worth -- and recognizing the distinction from larger system archetypes featured in "System Archetype Basics" -- within the System Dynamics literature, both balancing/regulatory feedbacks and reinforcing/positive feedbacks are viewed including beneficial examples and harmful examples. To use a particularly marked example that you'll sometimes see featured in the System Dynamics community, a reinforcing feedback exhibiting instability in an unfavourable direction can be called a "vicious cycle", while that same feedback heading in a favourable direction could in some cases be termed a "virtuous cycle". In my introductory lecture on causal loop diagrams at an undergraduate or graduate level, I oft note examples of balancing feedbacks that are desirable (certain homeostatic feedbacks and psychodynamic feedbacks come to mind), but others that can be entrapping. I also note reinforcing feedbacks that are widely viewed unfavourably (e.g., spread of a pathogen within a population, a conspiracy theory), and others that (rightly or wrongly!) people actively pursue (growing a company, or one's content that "goes viral". I note these things fully recognizing that this is a different distinction that you are mentioning here.

John Baez (May 31 2025 at 20:14):

Thanks! Yes, in general whether a system behavior counts as 'good' or 'bad' depends on us and how we interact with that system. But right now I'd eager to explore the crazy-sounding idea that there are certain motifs, or system archetypes, that are 'inherently bad' for the system possessing these motifs. (It could be a gang of criminals.) I would also be perfectly happy if this depended not just on the qualitative information in the motifs but also quantitative information; the main thing is that it be something you can read off from the model without making external value judgements. And the main reason I like this crazy idea is that it generates a testable hypothesis, namely that such 'inherently bad motifs' should be rare in gene regulatory networks, which have to some extent been optimized through evolution.

Adittya Chaudhuri (May 31 2025 at 20:34):

John Baez said:

such 'inherently bad motifs' should be rare in gene regulatory networks, which have to some extent been optimized through evolution.

I find this idea really super interesting!!

John Baez (May 31 2025 at 23:56):

Thanks! I haven't looked hard, but the few papers I've read on experimental searches for motifs in regulatory networks have focused on quite small motifs. It would be nice to get a large dataset and search it for certain larger motifs like the 'inherently bad' ones I'm talking about. Unfortunately the 'delay' feature that seems important in the bad system archetypes doesn't seem to be explicitly notated in the biological literature, so one would have to think about reactions that cause a delay.

I said "deadly" because, in such case "our immune system" fails to save us through their counter attack mechanisms, and we need external medications/treatements to cure ourselves. Hence, I think "a deadly disease pathways" can be reasonably compared to a complete "biological system failure". I think may be a good place for us to start looking for a large collection of such deadly pathways could be KEGG Human disease pathways.

John Baez (Jun 11 2025 at 06:17):

On a different note: my International Centre for Mathematical Sciences research group "Towards Hybrid Modeling" is going full steam!

The main goal is to develop models that blend agent-based models and stock-flow models.

Yesterday we also started tackling the problem of "observation processes" in modeling. We're trying to get a modeling framework with a clear separation between the "model proper" and the code that reports data collected from the model. Often the model proper and the observation processes are mixed in a way that's difficult to disentangle, leading ultimately to a lot of dead code that nobody dares delete.

Here I'm pontificating about this issue:

This group consists of @Nathaniel Osgood, @Evan Patterson, @Kris Brown, @Xiaoyan Li, @Owen Haaga, and @ww and his colleagues @Sándor Bartha and @Akindele Onifade.

I've been talking to Owen trying to help him come up with hybrid models that are good for labor economics, starting with some very simple toy models. Economists tend to use models in different ways from epidemiologists, so this is broadening our scope of concerns in eye-opening ways.

Shortly after this project ends on June 26, 2025, I'm going to Athens from June 28 to July 4 to give some lectures. I don't really want to be flying around, but I'm so happy to be in Europe that when @Christina Vasilakopoulou invited me, I failed to resist the opportunity to give a minicourse in a Summer School on Algebra at the Zografou campus of the National Technical University of Athens (NTUA). The summer school will cover some topics from representation theory, model-theoretic universal algebra, and combinatorics.

Counting with Categories

Many questions in enumerative combinatorics ask us to count the number of structures of some sort on an n-element set, as a function of n. But what is a "structure of some sort"? This is made precise by Joyal's theory of species. This theory also clarifies a simple yet powerful technique for counting structures on finite sets, namely generating functions. We shall see that the category of species is the "free 2-rig on one generator", and that combinatorially interesting operations on generating functions arise from this fact. Thus, a large chunk of enumerative combinatorics can be seen as a branch of categorified ring theory. We hope to strike a balance between abstract theory and fun concrete examples of counting problems.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Jun 11 2025 at 06:31):

John Baez said:

Does that KEGG database include any causal loop diagrams with delays?

According to https://www.genome.jp/kegg/document/help_pathway.html, there is no provision for mentioning delay in KEGG diagrams. However, interestingly, SBGN-AF in general accomomdates delay operators along with other operators like logical operators. Hence, I think if one can convert the KEGG diagrams into SBGN-AF diagrams by some means (preferably in a categorical way), then we may be able to model the KEGG database via causal loop diagrams with delays.

John Baez (Jun 11 2025 at 06:36):

But this would require figuring out (or guessing) which processes cause delays, right?

ww (Jun 11 2025 at 23:39):

It turns out the data is a little messier than I thought, but I was able to find a cycle in a way that tells us something about how to read those pictures. See here:

https://www.kegg.jp/pathway/hsa04210+355

Which is the same as the above, but with some vertices highlighted in red. Actually, that's a single vertex, the "Fas cell surface death receptor".

A good deal of what makes the data messy is that it is designed for display. And the solution that KEGG seems to use to avoid a spaghetti and make the layout nice is to repeat vertices. If I am reading it correctly that is. The downside is that cycles are not at all obvious, you really need to pay attention to the labels.

ww (Jun 12 2025 at 08:22):

Adittya Chaudhuri said:

and P53 signalling pathway

the other reason besides the "display orientation" that it is harder to produce something that looks like a causal loop diagram than i at first thought is that this apoptosis pathway references several other pathways. i don't quite understand their representation and how exactly it works to reference or include a pathway from another pathway.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Jun 12 2025 at 08:30):

What I understand is that every pathway in KEGG is written in a "modular way". That is, the Apopstosis path way is an interconnection of "zoomed out vesions of various other pathways" like MAPK pathways , P53 pathways and so on. If you click on MAPK path way or P53 pathway, etc, you can see the "Zoomed-in" versions of them.

ww (Jun 13 2025 at 08:42):

Adittya Chaudhuri said:

What I understand is that every pathway in KEGG is written in a "modular way". That is, the Apopstosis path way is an interconnection of "zoomed out vesions of various other pathways" like MAPK pathways , P53 pathways and so on. If you click on MAPK path way or P53 pathway, etc, you can see the "Zoomed-in" versions of them.

Visually, yes, this makes sense. Near as I can tell in the data, there is no information about how the interface between the embedded pathway and the one you are looking at. There are three things in the data: an enclosing pathway (the whole picture), "entries" which are vertices and contain information about layout on the screen, and "relations" which are edges between entries.

Entries have a "type" which is typically "gene" or "compound" or "group". A "group" is just a shorthand for a bunch of genes, like the "pro-apoptotic genes" on the right. But another type of entry is a "map" which means an embedded pathway. These ones do not seem to figure in relations at all, they are just placed there on the screen with nothing to say how they are hooked in.

So, take the p53 signalling pathway. In the apoptosis diagram, the p53 gene is connected to DNA which lead to a bunch of pro-apoptotic genes. In the p53 signalling pathway diagram, the p53 gene also appears, and again is connected to DNA which leads to a bunch of genes that include some but not all of those mentioned in the apoptosis diagram. The apoptosis diagram is mentioned, but appears all the way to the right, several hops downstream.

Is taking the union of all these nearby diagrams and identifying the genes (which have identifiers so that's easy) the right thing to do? What about the dashed lines, the indirect links? These involve what looks like text in the diagram but is really pictures of text that doesn't appear in the data itself.

I would be inclined to filter out all of the indirect links and work only with identifiable things...

John Baez (Jun 13 2025 at 08:53):

What's the exact meaning of these dashed line "indirect links", @Adittya Chaudhuri?

ww (Jun 13 2025 at 09:00):

John Baez said:

What's the exact meaning of these dashed line "indirect links", Adittya Chaudhuri?

The docs say "indirect effect without molecular details". But if we look at, say, the p53 pathway we see the target of these links being things like (a picture of the text) "p53 negative feedback", which is intriguing but underspecified...

ww (Jun 13 2025 at 09:06):

The diagrams cross scales wildly. From specific chemical reactions (according to the docs, I haven't seen one yet) to protein-mediated gene regulation to high-level concepts like DNA repair and damage prevention.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Jun 13 2025 at 09:16):

John Baez said:

What's the exact meaning of these dashed line "indirect links", Adittya Chaudhuri?

I think according to the definition of arrows here we may think it as an "unknown effect", i.e we know there exists an influence, but the nature of the influence is unknown. I think it can be labeled as "indeterminate" or 000 acording to our notation. The "state change" can also be its meaning. I think this matches with our intuition of existence of unknown effect beacause "if we think that the arrows are describing transitions from one state to another" then, the dotted arrows means that we only know that state change occured but we do not know how.

ww (Jun 13 2025 at 09:23):

That doesn't quite line up with the picture of the text "p53 negative feedback" which suggests a known effect, but there's no way that I've found to get at that information so yeah, probably 0 is safe.

However the bigger issue is that the targets of those links (often but not always) do not seem to be things that exist in the database.

ww (Jun 13 2025 at 09:26):

In the apoptosis diagram, the same style of link is also used simply to join different appearances of the same gene at different locations in the picture. That's ok because it amounts to a self-loop which could be labelled with 0 (or probably better ignored).

Adittya Chaudhuri (Jun 13 2025 at 09:29):

I think "whenever they do not know the underlying nature of the influence but they know its existence/ they know some state change happened", they are using those "dotted arrows" or indirect links.

John Baez (Jun 13 2025 at 09:30):

In the apoptosis diagram, the same style of link is also used simply to join different appearances of the same gene at different locations in the picture. That's ok because it amounts to a self-loop which could be labelled with 0 (or probably better ignored).

That sounds like not a 0 (an unknown effect of something on something else) but just a hint that we should identity the two appearances of the same gene. It sounds like they are using these dashed lines in an undisciplined way.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Jun 13 2025 at 09:34):

John Baez said:

It sounds like they are using these dashed lines in an undisciplined way.

I agree.

ww (Jun 13 2025 at 09:35):

ww (Jun 13 2025 at 10:45):

The SBML version is useful -- it contains a ton of annotations but more importantly it is designed for machine-readable exchange of chemical reaction networks. So we have chemical species and their reactions.

I am familiar with SBML from work I used to do in systems/molecular biology, but there I was mostly concerned with doing "mechanistic" modelling with graph rewriting because doing this stuff with reactions can from combinatorial explosion issues.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Jun 13 2025 at 10:58):

Petri net description is more closer to SBGN-PD. I think we need SBGN-activation flow diagram (SBGN-AF) which are more closer to causal loop diagrams. These are mentioned here. There is a paper on converting SBGN-PD (Petri nets) to SBGN-AF (causal loop diagrams).

Adittya Chaudhuri (Jun 13 2025 at 11:02):

I think KEGG diagrams are more closer to SBGN-AF. Reactome diagrams are written in SBGN-PD format

ww (Jun 13 2025 at 11:06):

I agree. Though translating from SBGN-PD to SBGN-AF or something similar seems like a better strategy because it looks like all the information is available. The VANTED software from that paper can do that.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Jun 13 2025 at 14:14):

I am trying to write down what I am thinking. I may be completely wrong. I am just trying to arrange my thoughts.

  • (1) KEGG data base contains quite a large number of pathways (I think probably one of the biggest pathway data available for the systems biologists), and the diagrams are to some extent similar to causal loop diagrams.
  • (2) We can try to convert the KEGG diagrams into MMM-labeled graphs for appropriate collection of monoids MMM. We already have the almost correct monoid for SBGN-AF diagrams in our ongoing paper with @John Baez . We may need to find more general monoids which can capture the logic of pathways in KEGG diagrams. Or, may be something else!! "The point is to make KEGG diagrams mathematical objects where one can run ACT-based software or objects where one can run software to find motifs.
  • (3) We can use the Klesili morphism approach to find motifs in those mathematical objects which are equivalent to KEGG diagrams via the available or yet to be made ACT based softwares.

Another approach as you said:

Converting the diagrams present in reactome (which are drawn in the language of SBGN-PD) in to SBGN-AF format, and then do (3). However, personally I feel KEGG data base is more general that reactome. Also, KEGG diagrams are almost like "causal loop diagrams". Thus, at the moment, I am a little biased towards "above approach". However, I may be completely wrong.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Jun 14 2025 at 04:46):

Thanks. I got your point. As of now, I found the paper Applied Category Theory for Genomics – An Initiative along this direction, especially, if you look at section 3.3, where they talked about possible applications of ACT on geneomic data to tackle its heterogenosity and diverseness. However, I feel this paper is not exactly an ACT paper, and looks more like a biology/ biological philosophy paper which proposes a research direction about the application of ACT in genomics. I will search more if there exists any other papers in the direction of integrative bioinformatics. Also, may be David Spivak's works on categorical database theory may be helpful for the purpose of integrarive bioinformatics.

John Baez (Jun 14 2025 at 07:54):

His categorical database theory is fundamental to AlgebraicJulia, which underlies a lot of my team's work on stock-flow diagrams, system structure diagrams, causal loop diagrams and agent-based models. So yes, it should be useful.

ww (Jun 14 2025 at 08:49):

I wonder about the relative volume of SBML data vs SBGN(-AF) data that is not derived from SBML.

ww (Jun 14 2025 at 09:04):

More generally, about "integrative bioinformatics", the language is typically the language of the Semantic Web. In principle this is Description Logic (e.g. OWL) territory but actually using this language in anger never really caught on. Partly because the underlying RDF language is a bit weird and clunky and partly because doing DL inference on larger datasets is expensive. So, we have these big datasets, like Uniprot and the Gene Ontology and so forth that are written in RDF but don't actually use most of the available logic or, in some cases, even publish in the expected way.

So the community has heavily invested in the RDF substrate for publishing data but cherry-picked those bits of it that are convenient and left the rest aside. Each sub-community has cherry-picked a little differently. And there's historical artefacts of the development process that can be seen. For example, if you look at an SBML file, you will see XML stuff and then RDF/XML fragments stuffed into various places. This is because when SBML started, they weren't convinced by RDF, and then they decided it was necessary but since they already had an XML format, they thought to just stuff the least convenient RDF serialisation in places. It's not clean design, it's design that has evolved over a couple of decades through committees...

ww (Jun 14 2025 at 09:27):

We did some work, jesus, it's 10 years ago now, on annotation of rule-based models (by which I mean graph rewriting). The idea there was to have a way (probably a functor) of relating the schema of the rewriting system to a schema in RDF in order to facilitate model composition. If you want to compose models, you need to be able to identify objects at least, and you can do this if you can say that they correspond to some standard thing in Uniprot, GO, etc.

Furthermore, for some types of objects, we know, in principle, the general shape of the rules that they should obey. For example, ribosomes bind to particular codons and slide along, producing the corresponding RNA polymer from the coding sequences until they hit a stop codon, then a similar process operates on the RNA polymer to produce proteins. This is the basic machinery that we have regardless of the specific sequence. So, by knowing that we have a specific sequence, we can generate the rules to produce a model for it.

We didn't express this in categorical language mostly because I didn't know any categorical language at the time...

Here, we want to produce causal loop diagrams for gene regulatory networks rather than rule-based models, but a similar setup could work.

ww (Jun 14 2025 at 09:31):

ww said:

We didn't express this in categorical language mostly because I didn't know any categorical language at the time...

Also because we were, for several reasons, publishing in bio journals...

Adittya Chaudhuri (Jun 14 2025 at 11:18):

ww said:

I wonder about the relative volume of SBML data vs SBGN(-AF) data that is not derived from SBML.

Many times I found biologists use cell designer to draw their biochemical networks/regulatory networks, and I think many important databases store the biological information using the cell designer. I think this cell designer is well connected to SBML. I think most diagrams created via cell designer use a mix of SBGN-PD and SBGN-AF format for example the database Reactome, AIR, etc. However, the databases like KEGG is also linked to SBML as mentioned in cell designer , but I think KEGG diagrams are not drawn in SBGN style. I think quite a large amount of biological information is now a days stored via cell designer and mostly as combinations of SBGN-PD and SBGN-AF (i.e. in an hybrid way).

Adittya Chaudhuri (Jun 14 2025 at 15:47):

Attached is a screenshot of a portion from Figure 1 of this paper, which shows the negative feedback of p53. To me, the Figure 1 looks almost like a causal loop diagram. It would be great if it is possible to convert KEGG like diagrams into diagrams of these types in an automated way.
zoomcell.png

ww (Jun 16 2025 at 10:26):

It's not super obvious to me what the relevant differences between this diagram and the KEGG diagrams are, apart from KEGG having a concept of linking/including other pathways. What do you see as the important differences?

Adittya Chaudhuri (Jun 17 2025 at 04:20):

At the moment, I am able to see two differences, becaue of which I find the "screenshot diagram" a bit more closer to "causal loop diagrams":

  • (1) Unlike the KEGG diagrams, I am not able to see a repeated node in the screenshot diagram.
  • (2) In the KEGG diagram (for example in the KEGG diagram here), I see most arrows are drawn either from "left to right" or from "top to bottom" (representing a sequentiality). The arrows which go to the other directions are connecting mostly the same nodes representing only probably identifications about which @John Baez already mentioned earlier. I do not think such sequentiality is present in the screenshot diagram.

I feel it may be possible to obtain the screenshot diagram from the KEGG diagram/s by taking pushouts of various sequential pathways along the common nodes. I think due to this, to me, "the pushout diagram" (screenshot diagram) looks more like a "causal loop diagram", which is not the case for a usual KEGG diagram.

ww (Jun 20 2025 at 09:23):

I do see "DNA Damage" repeated in the figure from the paper, but maybe that is a kind of high level process that could be considered "ambient" somehow.

I agree that the KEGG diagrams do have an overall "upper left" to "lower right" flow, with some exceptions like the hypothesised "Granzyme B pathway" that leads to GZMB back to CASP8.

This feels a lot like the problem of how to draw a non-planar graph in such a way as to make it aesthetically pleasing and easy to read. Very similar to when I was a network engineer and did a lot of laying out telecommunications network diagrams as documentation to support maintenance and troubleshooting. It's a bit of an art to do well.

What I'm not totally sure about is how much are we concerned with ways of laying out these diagrams to make them as easy as possible for humans to read at vs how much we are concerned with the underlying biological networks?

My instinct is that if we want to detect motifs that might be helpful or pathological and possibly are unknown to the biologists, it is better to work with the underlying network and derive a causal loop representation from that. When it comes to presenting or communicating these, then we are in layout territory. Does that make sense?

John Baez (Jun 20 2025 at 09:27):

Personally I don't care about the layout of diagrams unless nobody helps me find motifs using software so I have to do it by hand, or more precisely "by eye".

ww (Jun 20 2025 at 09:57):

The reactome helpfully makes their database available for download (this will be the source from which SBML and SBGN are created). This is very helpful because otherwise it would mean making some software to extract information from those kinds of files and put it into a database of some sort. So I now have the whole of the reactome on my computer and will see about getting something out of it in due course...

ww said:

What I'm not totally sure about is how much are we concerned with ways of laying out these diagrams to make them as easy as possible for humans to read at vs how much we are concerned with the underlying biological networks?

Adittya Chaudhuri (Jun 20 2025 at 12:19):

A question in a little different direction: As we saw in our paper that feedback loops can be described using homology monoids. Now, there are various patterns in regulatory netoworks "which are not exactly like loops". Like the way, people differentiate/characterise various topological spaces using tools like homology, is it possible to differentiate/characterise "various patterns in regulatory networks" in a similar fashion?

Adittya Chaudhuri (Jun 20 2025 at 12:37):

More formally, if

ϕ ⁣:(X,lX ⁣:EX→L)→(G,lG ⁣:EG→L)\phi \colon (X, l_{X} \colon E_{X} \to L) \to (G, l_{G} \colon E_{G} \to L)ϕ:(X,lX​:EX​→L)→(G,lG​:EG​→L) and

ψ ⁣:(X,lX ⁣:EX→L)→(G,lG ⁣:EG→L)\psi \colon (X, l_{X} \colon E_{X} \to L) \to (G, l_{G} \colon E_{G} \to L)ψ:(X,lX​:EX​→L)→(G,lG​:EG​→L)

be two (monic)Kleisli morphisms, can we show

H1(ϕ(X,ℓX),N)≅H1(ψ(X,ℓX),N)H_1 \big(\phi(X, \ell_{X} \big), \mathbb{N}) \cong H_1 \big( \psi(X, \ell_{X}) , \mathbb{N} \big)H1​(ϕ(X,ℓX​),N)≅H1​(ψ(X,ℓX​),N)?

On the other hand, if (X,ℓX)(X, \ell_{X})(X,ℓX​) is not isomorphic to (Y,ℓY)(Y, \ell_{Y})(Y,ℓY​), and

if θ ⁣:(Y,ℓY ⁣:EY→L)→(G,lG ⁣:EG→L)\theta \colon (Y, \ell_{Y} \colon E_{Y} \to L) \to (G, l_{G} \colon E_{G} \to L)θ:(Y,ℓY​:EY​→L)→(G,lG​:EG​→L) be a monic Kleisli morphism,

then can we expect that H1(ϕ(X,ℓX),N)H_1 \big(\phi(X, \ell_{X} \big), \mathbb{N})H1​(ϕ(X,ℓX​),N) is not isomorphic to H1(ψ(Y,ℓY),N)) H_1 \big( \psi(Y, \ell_{Y}), \mathbb{N} )\big)H1​(ψ(Y,ℓY​),N)) ?

John Baez (Jun 20 2025 at 12:44):

I don't understand your first question here: "can we show H1(ϕ(X,ℓX),N)≅H1(ψ(X,ℓX),N)H_1 \big(\phi(X, \ell_{X} \big), \mathbb{N}) \cong H_1 \big( \psi(X, \ell_{X}) , \mathbb{N} \big)H1​(ϕ(X,ℓX​),N)≅H1​(ψ(X,ℓX​),N)?"

First of all, I guess ϕ(X,ℓX)\phi(X, \ell_{X} )ϕ(X,ℓX​) is some sort of LLL-labeled graph built from the LLL-labeled graph (X,ℓX)(X, \ell_X)(X,ℓX​) and the Kleisli morphism ϕ\phiϕ. You didn't define ϕ(X,ℓX)\phi(X, \ell_{X} )ϕ(X,ℓX​). Am I supposed to know what it means?

Second of all, if my first guess is right, what is H1H_1H1​ of an LLL-labeled graph?

John Baez (Jun 20 2025 at 12:47):

Now, there are various patterns in regulatory netoworks "which are not exactly like loops".

By the way, yesterday you mentioned feedforward loops as an example of this. And that make me realize something.

The set of undirected loops in a directed graph is N\mathbb{N}N-graded, where the loops of grade nnn change direction nnn times. So, the directed loops have grade 000, and feedforward loops have grade 111.

John Baez (Jun 20 2025 at 12:50):

In a similar way, I think H1(G,Z)H_1(G,\mathbb{Z})H1​(G,Z) is N\mathbb{N}N-filtered, where the nnn th filtration contains all Z\mathbb{Z}Z-linear combinations of homology classes of loops that change direction nnn times.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Jun 20 2025 at 12:50):

John Baez said:

I don't understand your first question here: "can we show H1(ϕ(X,ℓX),N)≅H1(ψ(X,ℓX),N)H_1 \big(\phi(X, \ell_{X} \big), \mathbb{N}) \cong H_1 \big( \psi(X, \ell_{X}) , \mathbb{N} \big)H1​(ϕ(X,ℓX​),N)≅H1​(ψ(X,ℓX​),N)?"

First of all, I guess ϕ(X,ℓX)\phi(X, \ell_{X} )ϕ(X,ℓX​) is some sort of LLL-labeled graph built from the LLL-labeled graph (X,ℓX)(X, \ell_X)(X,ℓX​) and the Kleisli morphism ϕ\phiϕ. You didn't define ϕ(X,ℓX)\phi(X, \ell_{X} )ϕ(X,ℓX​). Am I supposed to know what it means?

Second of all, if my first guess is right, what is H1H_1H1​ of an LLL-labeled graph?

Sorry!! I understand your question. We can assume L={1}L= \lbrace 1 \rbraceL={1}. We may think ϕ(X,ℓX)\phi(X, \ell_{X} )ϕ(X,ℓX​) as a subgraph of (G,ℓG)(G, \ell_{G})(G,ℓG​)(since I assumed monic). Thus for the case of L={1}L= \lbrace 1 \rbraceL={1}, I think it means XXX is a subgraph of GGG.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Jun 20 2025 at 12:55):

John Baez said:

Now, there are various patterns in regulatory netoworks "which are not exactly like loops".

By the way, yesterday you mentioned feedforward loops as an example of this. And that make me realize something.

The set of undirected loops in a directed graph is N\mathbb{N}N-graded, where the loops of grade nnn change direction nnn times. So, the directed loops have grade 000, and feedforward loops have grade 111.

Thanks very much !! I find this point of view super interesting!!

Adittya Chaudhuri (Jun 20 2025 at 13:02):

John Baez said:

In a similar way, I think H1(G,Z)H_1(G,\mathbb{Z})H1​(G,Z) is N\mathbb{N}N-filtered, where the nnn th filtration contains all Z\mathbb{Z}Z-linear combinations of homology classes of loops that change direction nnn times.

Interesting!! I may be comepletely wrong, but it feels like your N\mathbb{N}N-filtration of H1(G,Z)H_1(G,\mathbb{Z})H1​(G,Z) and your N\mathbb{N}N-grading of undirected loops can possibly be the right mathematical language to understand various patterns in regulatory networks?

John Baez (Jun 20 2025 at 13:27):

It might help us understand some patterns. From a pure math viewpoint, I think it's very interesting to see what information the directed graph GGG has that is not contained in its undirected version. We know that Z⊗H1(G,N)Z \otimes H_1(G,\mathbb{N})Z⊗H1​(G,N) is just a subgroup of H1(G,Z)H_1(G,\mathbb{Z})H1​(G,Z), and that subgroup contains some of this information. But this is just the 0th stage of the aforementioned filtration of H1(G,Z)H_1(G, \mathbb{Z})H1​(G,Z).

(By the way, this filtration may be a grading; I'm just trying to play it safe.)

Adittya Chaudhuri (Jun 20 2025 at 16:49):

Thanks very much!! Yes, I agree to your point.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Jun 20 2025 at 19:07):

Althogh a different context than characterising various patterns, here #community: our work > John Baez @ 💬 I wanted to say the following:

Let L={1}L= \lbrace 1 \rbraceL={1}. Thus, I am ignoring the labeling. Then, I think a monic Kleisli map ϕ ⁣:X→G \phi \colon X \to Gϕ:X→G idenitfies XXX as a subgraph ϕ(X)\phi(X)ϕ(X) of GGG. I claim the monic Kleisli map ϕ\phiϕ preserves our 1st homology. In other words, I claim the following :

H1(X,C)≅H1(ϕ(X),C)H_1(X, C) \cong H_1(\phi(X), C)H1​(X,C)≅H1​(ϕ(X),C) for any commutative monoid CCC.

It just says, similar patterns should have similar homology monoids irrespective of their "sizes". I agree, my statement sounds obvious/ rather trivial. But, in a way, it is possiobly giving a relationship between our homology monoids and monic Kleisli maps.

John Baez (Jun 20 2025 at 20:44):

Then, I think a monic Kleisli map ϕ ⁣:X→G\phi \colon X \to Gϕ:X→G idenitfies XXX as a subgraph ϕ(X)\phi(X)ϕ(X) of GGG.

That's true only for a rather subtle concept of "subgraph", not the usual concept "subobject in the category Gph\mathsf{Gph}Gph". For example, there's a monic Kleisli map from

1→2 1 \to 2 1→2

to

1→A→2 1 \to A \to 21→A→2

sending 111 to 111 and 222 to 222. But it's not a map of graphs in the usual sense, so it's not picking out a subgraph of 1→A→2 1 \to A \to 21→A→2 in the usual sense.

John Baez (Jun 20 2025 at 20:46):

I think graph theorists do study the "generalized subgraphs" of the kind you're talking about here, but I forget what they call them.

John Baez (Jun 20 2025 at 20:47):

Anyway, I think the first step is to check that any Kleisli map f:G→Hf: G \to Hf:G→H functorially induces a map on 1-chains

C1(f,C):C1(G,C)→C1(H,C)C_1(f,C): C_1(G,C) \to C_1(H,C)C1​(f,C):C1​(G,C)→C1​(H,C)

and on 0-chains

C0(f,C):C0(G,C)→C0(H,C)C_0(f,C): C_0(G,C) \to C_0(H,C)C0​(f,C):C0​(G,C)→C0​(H,C)

Then, check any Kleisli map f:G→Hf: G \to Hf:G→H functorially induces a map on homology H1(f,C):H1(G,C)→H1(H,C)H_1(f,C): H_1(G,C) \to H_1(H,C)H1​(f,C):H1​(G,C)→H1​(H,C). So in the end, you'll know first homology with coefficients in CCC is a functor from graphs and Kleisli morphisms to commutative monoids.

That seems plausible, and worth working out.

Adittya Chaudhuri (Jun 21 2025 at 05:12):

John Baez said:

Then, I think a monic Kleisli map ϕ ⁣:X→G\phi \colon X \to Gϕ:X→G idenitfies XXX as a subgraph ϕ(X)\phi(X)ϕ(X) of GGG.

That's true only for a rather subtle concept of "subgraph", not the usual concept "subobject in the category Gph\mathsf{Gph}Gph". For example, there's a monic Kleisli map from

1→2 1 \to 2 1→2

to

1→A→2 1 \to A \to 21→A→2

sending 111 to 111 and 222 to 222. But it's not a map of graphs in the usual sense, so it's not picking out a subgraph of 1→A→2 1 \to A \to 21→A→2 in the usual sense.

Thanks!! Yes, I agree!!

Adittya Chaudhuri (Jun 21 2025 at 05:38):

John Baez said:

Anyway, I think the first step is to check that any Kleisli map f:G→Hf: G \to Hf:G→H functorially induces a map on 1-chains

C1(f,C):C1(G,C)→C1(H,C)C_1(f,C): C_1(G,C) \to C_1(H,C)C1​(f,C):C1​(G,C)→C1​(H,C)

and on 0-chains

C0(f,C):C0(G,C)→C0(H,C)C_0(f,C): C_0(G,C) \to C_0(H,C)C0​(f,C):C0​(G,C)→C0​(H,C)

Then, check any Kleisli map f:G→Hf: G \to Hf:G→H functorially induces a map on homology H1(f,C):H1(G,C)→H1(H,C)H_1(f,C): H_1(G,C) \to H_1(H,C)H1​(f,C):H1​(G,C)→H1​(H,C). So in the end, you'll know first homology with coefficients in CCC is a functor from graphs and Kleisli morphisms to commutative monoids.

That seems plausible, and worth working out.

Thanks very much!!

I am trying to write down a part of the first step:

Let f:G→Hf: G \to Hf:G→H be a Kleisli map. Hence, we have a usual map of graphs

fˉ ⁣:G→Und∘Free(H)\bar{f} \colon G \to Und \circ \mathsf{Free}(H)fˉ​:G→Und∘Free(H). We know that this map induces the maps

C1(fˉ,C) ⁣:C1(G,C)→C1(Und∘Free(H),C)C_1(\bar{f}, C) \colon C_1(G, C) \to C_1(Und \circ \mathsf{Free}(H),C) C1​(fˉ​,C):C1​(G,C)→C1​(Und∘Free(H),C) and

C0(fˉ,C) ⁣:C0(G,C)→C0(Und∘Free(H),C)C_0(\bar{f}, C) \colon C_0(G, C) \to C_0(Und \circ \mathsf{Free}(H),C) C0​(fˉ​,C):C0​(G,C)→C0​(Und∘Free(H),C)

such that C1(fˉ,C)C_1(\bar{f}, C)C1​(fˉ​,C) and C0(fˉ,C)C_0(\bar{f}, C)C0​(fˉ​,C) commute with the maps C1(s)C_1(s)C1​(s) and C1(t)C_1(t)C1​(t).

Now, since H1(Und∘Free(H),C)H_1(Und \circ \mathsf{Free}(H),C)H1​(Und∘Free(H),C) is the equalizer of the corresponding C1(s)C_1(s)C1​(s) and C1(t)C_1(t)C1​(t), the map C1(fˉ,C)C_1(\bar{f}, C)C1​(fˉ​,C) canonically induces a map

H1(fˉ,C) ⁣:H1(G,C)→H1(Und∘Free(H),C)H_1(\bar{f}, C) \colon H_1(G, C) \to H_1(Und \circ \mathsf{Free}(H),C)H1​(fˉ​,C):H1​(G,C)→H1​(Und∘Free(H),C).

So, in a way, I think for proving your claim, we only need to verify functoriality. (I am working on it).

I am now trying to write down (in the above language) what I want to say here #community: our work > John Baez @ 💬 :

Let us define Gˉ\bar{G}Gˉ as the graph fˉ(G)\bar{f}(G)fˉ​(G). (I am not claiming that fˉ(G)\bar{f}(G)fˉ​(G) is a subgraph of Und∘Free(H)Und \circ \mathsf{Free}(H)Und∘Free(H), I am just considering it as an independent graph). Then, can we expect (from the point of topological intuition) that

H1(G,C)≅H1(Gˉ,C)H_1(G, C) \cong H_1(\bar{G}, C)H1​(G,C)≅H1​(Gˉ,C) when fˉ\bar{f}fˉ​ is a monomorphism ?

John Baez (Jun 21 2025 at 08:34):

That sounds believable. My own strategy would be to try to understand Kleisli morphisms geometrically. Maybe you can prove that a Kleisli morphism from GGG to HHH is the same thing as a map sending each vertex vvv of GGG to a vertex f(v)f(v)f(v) of HHH and each edge eee of GGG to a path f(e)f(e)f(e) in HHH, such that f(s(e))f(s(e))f(s(e)) is the starting point of the path f(e)f(e)f(e) and f(t(e))f(t(e))f(t(e)) is the end point of the path f(e)f(e)f(e).

This lays the groundwork for understanding how Kleisli morphisms act on C0C_0C0​ and C1C_1C1​. For example, each 1-chain in GGG is a linear combination of edges eee, and fff maps each of those edges to a path f(e)f(e)f(e), which in turn gives a 1-chain in HHH.

John Baez (Jun 21 2025 at 08:35):

I'm afraid I can't work on this stuff anymore, at least not for a while: I have to prepare some lectures in combinatorics.

This lays the groundwork for understanding how Kleisli morphisms act on C0C_0C0​ and C1C_1C1​. For example, each 1-chain in GGG is a linear combination of edges eee, and fff maps each of those edges to a path f(e)f(e)f(e), which in turn gives a 1-chain in HHH.

John Baez (Jun 22 2025 at 15:50):

I'm going to resume using this thread for what it's normally for: announcing my plans and recent work. We can keep talking about motifs in biology on

#theory: applied category theory > Graphs with polarities

So:

  • As mentioned, Additya Chaudhuri and I finished our paper Graphs with polarities.
  • You can also see slides of a talk I gave about that paper. A video may someday show up too, but I haven't gotten ahold of it yet.
  • I'm soon going to Athens to give a 4.5-hour minicourse on combinatorial species at a Summer School on Algebra at the Zografou campus of the National Technical University of Athens. It's run by @Christina Vasilakopoulou. I hope to put lecture notes online, and here's the first part: Counting with categories (Part 1).

John Baez (Jun 24 2025 at 23:12):

Okay, I have a preliminary version of all my lecture notes done:

  • Counting with categories (part 1) - species and their generating functions; the category of species.
  • Counting with categories (part 2) - the 2-rig of species; counting binary trees.
  • Counting with categories (part 3) - substitution of species; linear species.

John Baez (Jun 30 2025 at 15:33):

Here are 3 things I need to do.

  • Submit Graphs with polarities to the arXiv (done) and then to a journal (not yet done).
  • Make the changes requested by the referee to 2-Rig extensions and the splitting principle (with @Joe Moeller and @Todd Trimble) and resubmit.
  • Submit Tannaka reconstruction and the monoid of matrices (with @Todd Trimble) to a journal.

All these should be easy. Then I have various more substantial projects I can start, and it's fun but a bit challenging to decide what to do first!

  • Finish writing a paper on the mathematics of the periodic table in chemistry. I started this when I first retired and was feeling very relaxed. Then I got busy. I should finish it. It's not very important but it's a fun subject.
  • Write a short paper on applied double category theory for the volume in honor of Bob Paré. The deadline for this has been pushed back so it's possible for me now. (I forget the deadline.)
  • Finish writing a paper with Nathaniel Osgood, Xiaoyan Li and perhaps others on the category theory behind the subject called System Dynamics.

That's enough, though I can imagine more!

John Baez (Jul 03 2025 at 13:57):

I just learned that the strangest of the complex reflection groups is G31G_{31}G31​, called the rabbit because its diagram looks like a head with two ears.

John Baez (Jul 06 2025 at 10:25):

David Suzuki says something I believe: it's too late to save the civilization we're used to, but not too late to save some of your neighbors for a while:

  • David Suzuki on climate change, Azimuth.

In the above blog article I quote what feel like the most important parts of an interview with him. It's probably better to read the whole interview. I conclude:

It’s hard to know what to do with my life if these things are true, because my life was based on other assumptions. I also believe there’s a decent chance I can eke out a comfortable existence for a couple more decades without dramatically changing my behavior (apart from moving to Scotland). But it would be silly not to update my assumptions, state this publicly, and take it from there.

It sounds like Suzuki, too, was reluctant to say publicly what he’s now come to believe. But I’m glad he’s come out and said it. If the basic outlines are correct, there’s a lot to do, and no point in staying quiet about it, even if we don’t yet have the courage and energy to change our lives as the situation calls for.

Kevin Carlson (Jul 07 2025 at 02:47):

I’m always confused when I see a new maximally negative statement about climate like this, because while of course there’s lots and lots of bad stuff happening to the Earth, it’s seemed to me like the great majority of the news in the 21st century has been very good relative to the ‘90s baseline. US emissions are the lowest they’ve been in my lifetime, coal is basically economically dead, solar is the best power source in many situations, the terrifying 8.5 degree warming scenarios that filled my nightmares in 2005 are now out of the question, electric cars are fantastic, China is moving really rather vigorously toward electrification; all of this is great news that would have deeply surprised my 2000-era self! On the other hand, Trump is really bad. But again, “republicans hate the climate” has been true for decades, and that hasn’t stopped really rather substantial progress. Certainly, I expect the world to warm 2.5 degrees by my old age and for that to be really bad. Is it therefore “too late to save our civilization?” What, exactly does that even mean, and how did people discover such fine grained parameterization of the prospects of a whole civilization that they can distinguish between the safety of 1.5 degrees and the doom of 2.5?

What’s most frustrating about this discourse is (1) it plays into a profoundly pessimistic, even nihilistic tendency among many of today’s educated classes that is highly nonconducive to working to make things as good as possible, independently of how bad they actually are (2) the tendency to make broad, highly confident predictive claims on the basis of research that itself makes almost no predictive claims at all. I read the most recent work on the “nine planetary boundaries” that is all I see Suzuki citing, and it’s interesting and important and makes no concrete predictions whatsoever except temperature ones, which we already knew about! It’s not news that we are driving many terrestrial systems outside of their Holocene norms, is it? So what new information, other than “trump got reelected”, is really driving such despondent commentary? Honestly, I wish you wouldn’t post things about having no hope for the future. Those of us who are expecting to have to deal with most of this century, however it turns out, don’t need the extra burden.

John Baez (Jul 07 2025 at 06:39):

I didn't say I have "no hope for the future", and I don't even think Suzuki feels that way. I also don't think Suzuki was saying anything dramatically new has occurred recently: he says "it’s been coming all along." It's true that the rise of pro-carbon-burning, climate-change-denying authoritarian rule in the US makes it harder to imagine bright futures. But more fundamentally the problem is that the CO2 concentration in the atmosphere, deforestation, biodiversity loss, etc. keep increasing quite rapidly.

This is not new. I think what's new is that Suzuki got tired of not saying what he actually believes. That's what's happening to me, anyway - not a big change in my actual assessment of the situation.

I'm familiar with the argument that it's better to shut up because saying what I believe would be demoralizing. Suzuki must be even more familiar with that argument, being a bigshot "environmental activist".

Maybe it's better to say what we actually believe, so we can go from there, either taking action that's more realistic to the situation at hand, or getting into a productive argument with people who have more optimistic assessments.

As for me, my dark assessment of the future isn't making me "give up", but it does make me want to rethink where I'm putting my energy. Too much of my activity presumes a kind of future that seems unlikely to happen.

By the way, I think the most productive argument here would not be about "whether or not we're doomed", or even "how bad will things get", but what we should do.

John Baez (Jul 08 2025 at 07:39):

Having almost wrapped up work on submitting my last 3 papers, I'm getting back to a paper on the mathematics of the periodic table of elements, which exposes some surprising links to quantum field theory. I last worked on this in 2021, in a lull in the COVID pandemic when Lisa and I drove to the Center for Hellenic Studies in Washington DC and stayed there for a semester. I had just retired, and I spent a lot of time admiring the colorful autumn foliage in the center, very peacefully, thinking about how anthocyanins and carotenoids in the leaves interact with photons, and thinking about the periodic table. My work then looks surprisingly detailed to me now - I was really getting into it! We later drove back across the country, with the omicron variant hot on our heels.

It should be pretty quick to finish off this paper.

Amar Hadzihasanovic (Jul 08 2025 at 08:06):

Took me a minute to realise you are not talking about the periodic table of nnn-categories!

John Baez (Jul 08 2025 at 08:34):

Heh, no, I'm talking about the actual periodic table of elements, which has wonderful links to many branches of mathematics! I'll edit my comment to clarify that.

John Baez (Jul 08 2025 at 08:43):

In the 1990s I was really excited about higher category theory, but then a bunch of smart people started working on that, making it require a lot of work to do something really new in that area. These days I often prefer to take relatively "concrete" aspects of science and find connections that people haven't fully explored. I find it very pleasant to think about things like mathematical links between the 14 rare earth elements and the 14-exceptional dimensional Lie group G2, for example.... though annoyingly, I never quite figured out what the physicist Racah was doing there! In fact I never even finished off the closely project to describe the 7d cross product using the spin-3 representation of SO(3). But a lot of chemistry is based on group representation theory, so we should expect to find lots of interesting connections like this if we look hard enough.

John Baez (Jul 16 2025 at 16:08):

I finished that paper I mentioned earlier, and it should appear on the arXiv soon. I'm writing a series of blog articles about it, and the first is here:

  • The Kepler Problem (Part 2).

(Yes, there was an earlier part that's not so closely connected.) This part introduces the extra conserved quantity for the inverse square force law which does not exist for other central forces: the so-called 'Runge-Lenz vector', which I'd rather call the 'eccentricity vector' since it was discovered neither by Runge nor Lenz.

But this is just the beginning of a long story which becomes a lot more surprising. I've got a bunch more blog articles lined up.

Now I want to start work on a paper called something like "Applied double category theory", for the volume in honor of Bob Paré.

  • I'll probably start by describing decorated and structured cospan double categories, a bunch of concrete examples of these, and how they have been (to some extent) coded into AlgebraicJulia and applied to fields like epidemiology.

  • Then I'll try to explain David Jaz Myer's double categorical systems theory, and how it subsumes decorated and structured cospan double categories but also double categories of lenses.

  • Then I'll try to explain how Catcolab uses double categorical doctrines, and what Catcolab can do. Here I'll be aided by Brendan Fong's new talk Abstractions for real people, since it explains pretty well why we need multiple 'frames' in which to think.

John Baez (Jul 17 2025 at 08:02):

  • The Kepler Problem (Part 3).

Not only is the eccentricity vector of a planet orbiting the Sun conserved, so its orbit doesn't precess, its velocity vector moves around in a circle! This was discovered by Hamilton, and it sets the stage for deeper developments. I explain it here.

John Baez (Jul 18 2025 at 09:59):

My paper is out now:

  • Second quantization for the Kepler problem.

Abstract. The Kepler problem concerns a point particle in an attractive inverse square force. After a brief review of the classical and quantum versions of this problem, focused on their hidden SU(2)×SU(2)\text{SU}(2) \times \text{SU}(2)SU(2)×SU(2) symmetry, we discuss the quantum Kepler problem for a spin-12\frac{1}{2}21​ particle. We show that the Hilbert space H\mathcal{H}H of bound states for this problem is unitarily equivalent, as a representation of SU(2)×SU(2)\text{SU}(2) \times \text{SU}(2)SU(2)×SU(2), to the Hilbert space of solutions of the Weyl equation on the spacetime R×S3\mathbb{R} \times S^3R×S3. This equation describes a massless left-handed spin-12\frac{1}{2}21​ particle. We then form the fermionic Fock space on H\mathcal{H}H and show this is unitarily equivalent to the Hilbert space of a massless left-handed spin-12\frac{1}{2}21​ free quantum field on R×S3\mathbb{R} \times S^3R×S3, again as representations of SU(2)×SU(2)\text{SU}(2) \times \text{SU}(2)SU(2)×SU(2). By modifying the Hamiltonian of this free field theory, we obtain the well-known 'Madelung rules'. These give a reasonable approximation to the observed filling of subshells as we consider elements with more and more electrons, and match the rough overall structure of the periodic table.

John Baez (Jul 20 2025 at 09:01):

  • The Kepler problem (part 4).

Noether's theorem says conserved quantities come from symmetries - and in the Kepler problem, conservation of the angular momentum and the eccentricity vector come from symmetry under rotations in 4-dimensional space. Here I explain how this works. I show how the Kepler problem can be rephrased in terms of a free particle moving around on a sphere in 4-dimensional space. Then I rephrase the Kepler problem in terms of a massless, relativistic free particle moving at the speed of light on a sphere in 4-dimensional space. This sets the stage for even more shocking developments to come.

John Baez (Jul 24 2025 at 08:14):

  • The Kepler problem (part 5).

I've been talking about how a classical particle in an inverse square force law, like the Earth orbiting the Sun, can be reinterpreted as a particle moving at the speed of light on a sphere in 4-dimensional space (that is, S3S^3S3).

That's already cool. But the same story also works in quantum mechanics, like for an electron orbiting a proton in a hydrogen atom!

There are various ways to show this is true. In my blog article I take a brutally efficient approach. A quantum particle moving on S3S^3S3 is described by a vector in the Hilbert space L2(S3)L^2(S^3)L2(S3). I describe operators on this Hilbert space arising from rotational symmetries: these give a representation of so(4)\mathfrak{so}(4)so(4) on L2(S3)L^2(S^3)L2(S3). Combining these with standard facts about the hydrogen atom, we can set up a correspondence between vectors in L2(S3)L^2(S^3)L2(S3) and bound states of a hydrogen atom — that is, states where the electron orbits the proton, instead of flying off.

Here’s how it works. Chemists use a basis of bound states of the hydrogen atom called ∣n,ℓ,m⟩|n,\ell,m\rangle∣n,ℓ,m⟩ where

• n tells us the energy of the state,
• ℓ tells us its total angular momentum, and
• m tells us its angular momentum around the z axis.

(For now we’re ignoring the electron’s spin: I'll bring that in later.) In my blog article I show that L2(S3)L^2(S^3)L2(S3) also have a nice basis of states called ∣n,ℓ,m⟩|n,\ell,m\rangle∣n,ℓ,m⟩. This will let us match up hydrogen atom bound states and vectors in L2(S3)L^2(S^3)L2(S3).

So the most basic element has a nice description in terms of functions on a sphere in 4d space!

John Baez (Jul 24 2025 at 20:53):

Kevin Coloumbier has come out with a paper claiming to prove some conjectures that Todd Trimble, Joe Moeller and I made in our paper 2-Rig extensions and the splitting principle! I explain these conjectures here:

  • 2-Rig conjectures proved?, The n-Category Cafe.

and point to some related conjectures left to prove. I'm really glad categorified rig theory is getting attention from someone with expertise in representation theory.

Mike Shulman (Jul 24 2025 at 21:13):

It's curious to me to see something freely generated by something defined (partially) by a negative condition (an object has bosonic dimension nnn if it is not a fermionic line object and ...). It's like asking for a group that's freely generated by two elements that don't commute, or by one element whose order is not 17.

In general I wouldn't expect such things to exist. In my two examples such a group does exist, but only because the group freely generated without the negative condition happens to satisfy the negative condition: in the free group on two elements the generators don't commute, and in the free group on one element the generator doesn't have order 17. So I wonder whether the free 2-rig on an object of bosonic dimension nnn also has a stronger universal property in which the negative part of the condition is omitted?

Mike Shulman (Jul 24 2025 at 21:14):

The fact that Coloumbier needed to add the negative condition suggests that you can't just omit it from the definition, but maybe there is a different definition than your original one that also generalizes his version and doesn't include a negation?

John Baez (Jul 25 2025 at 05:46):

I think you must be right. I'll copy your remark to the n-Cafe, if that's okay, because that's where I'm talking about this!

But first what I did is copy the blog article back to the real n-Cafe, because I think it's working well again.

Mike Shulman (Jul 25 2025 at 06:35):

Ok, great. I only made the comment here because I wasn't sure what the long-term solution would be.

David Michael Roberts (Jul 25 2025 at 07:07):

BTW I'm still getting a spam comment on the counting trees post, even though it doesn't appear on the front page under 'recent comments'

John Baez (Jul 25 2025 at 07:22):
