John Baez (Jul 25 2025 at 07:26):

@David Michael Roberts - I'll try to delete that spam. For a while there was a bug where deleting a comment did not delete comments on that comment, yet made them invisible on the back end, making them impossible to delete.. . for anyone except Jacques! I may need to call him in.

John Baez (Jul 25 2025 at 07:35):

Okay - it was invisible on the back end, but I was able to delete it another way.

John Baez (Jul 29 2025 at 12:58):

  • The Kepler problem (part 6).

A planet orbiting the Sun has the same symmetries as a massless particle moving at the speed of light around a sphere in 4-dimensional space! Their energies are different, but related. If the massless particle has energy A, the energy of the planet is

E = -1/8A²

(in some convenient units).

The same thing works in quantum mechanics, where now we have a hydrogen atom instead of a planet. But there's a slight twist! Now, if the massless particle has energy A, the energy of the hydrogen atom is

E = -1/8(A² + ¼)

(in some convenient units where Planck's constant is 1).

Where does the extra ¼ come from? It's called the Duflo isomorphism, and it turns out this ¼ is related to the 1/24 in string theory - the one that makes bosonic string theory work best in 26 dimensions!

John Baez (Jul 29 2025 at 13:05):

  • The Kepler problem (part 7).

So far my series has been review of known facts, but now comes some new stuff from my paper. It starts out with a simple idea: let's include the electron's spin in our description of the hydrogen atom! Now the space of electron bound states becomes the space of C2\mathbb{C}^2C2-valued wavefunctions on the 3-sphere. The group SU(2)\text{SU}(2)SU(2) has 3 commuting representations on this Hilbert space: via left translations on S3≅SU(2)S^3 \cong \text{SU}(2)S3≅SU(2), via right translations, and via its action on C2\mathbb{C}^2C2. These 3 commuting representations spawn 23=82^3 = 823=8 different representations of SU(2)\text{SU}(2)SU(2) on the same Hilbert space - and while not all of them are physically important, a bunch do show up in the usual textbook treatment of hydrogen, so I explain those.

John Baez (Aug 01 2025 at 08:26):

  • The Kepler problem (part 8).

Now comes the really new stuff. I want to explain how the hydrogen atom with a spin-½ electron is equivalent to a massless spin-½ particle in the ‘Einstein universe’. This is the universe Einstein believed in before Hubble said the universe was expanding! It has a 3-sphere S3S^3S3 for space, and this sphere stays the same size as time passes.

Today I’ll just lay the groundwork. To study relativistic spin-½ quantum particles, we need to understand the Dirac operator. So, I describe the first the Laplacian and then the Dirac operator on S3S^3S3, express the Hamiltonian of the hydrogen atom in terms of the Dirac operator, and use the work of Kronheimer to study the eigenspaces of the Dirac operator.

John Baez (Aug 01 2025 at 17:48):

News flash: they found the oldest rocks in the world!

John Baez (Aug 03 2025 at 16:21):

I finally finished my paper Second quantization for the Kepler problem. I could not resist adding a short section on a purely quaternionic description of bound states of the hydrogen atom! Roughly speaking, they correspond to functions ψ:H−{0}→H\psi : \mathbb{H} - \{0\} \to \mathbb{H}ψ:H−{0}→H which are analytic in the quaternion sense - that is, they obey the quaternionic version of the Cauchy-Riemann equation. Having come very close to showing this, I could not resist going all the way.

I now want to submit this somewhere. I'd normally try Journal of Mathematical Physics (since the more prestigious Communications in Mathematical Physics is owned by the evil Springer), but they're not diamond open access, so I thought it might be nice to find the best diamond open access mathematical physics journal and submit there. So far the best one seems to be SIGMA: Symmetry, Integrability and Geometry. It's perfectly suited to this paper, since the hydrogen atom is a completely integrable, very symmetrical system and I'm using geometry. And it's part of the Free Journal Network.

Ruby Khondaker (she/her) (Aug 03 2025 at 17:21):

Ooh, so did you put it up on your personal website in-real-time as you were working through the problem?

John Baez (Aug 03 2025 at 20:01):

Yes. This version will show up on the arXiv on Tuesday.

I thought I'd be content to submit the previous version for publication, but I was so close to really nailing this quaternionic description of the hydrogen atom it seemed a shame not to put in the extra work to finish it off. And I'm glad I did!

Ruby Khondaker (she/her) (Aug 03 2025 at 20:02):

I'm currently writing my own expository article on Category Theory, so I think I'll also end up putting that on my personal website! (Probably won't be good enough for arxiv though :sweat_smile: )

John Baez (Aug 03 2025 at 22:10):

At least announce it here!

Ruby Khondaker (she/her) (Aug 03 2025 at 22:27):

Will do once I’ve got it done - trying to follow your advice about not having open secrets :)

John Baez (Aug 04 2025 at 07:56):

Great!

John Baez (Aug 04 2025 at 12:00):

  • The Kepler problem (part 9).

In 1843 Hamilton invented the quaternions: a 4-dimensional division algebra ℍ over the real numbers. Quaternions have a 3d 'vector' part and a 1d 'scalar' part, but they were invented before vectors. So, throughout the 1800s, scientists used them a lot. Maxwell wrote a version of his equations using quaternions! And for a while, quaternions were the only graduate-level mathematics course in the US.

In complex analysis we single out certain specially nice functions from the complex numbers to the complex numbers called 'analytic' functions. But you can also define analytic functions from ℍ to ℍ. Not many people think about these - but some good mathematical physicists have.

Here's something I discovered, which I explain in this article:

If we take into account the fact that electrons have spin ½, bound states of the hydrogen atom - states where the electron doesn't fly off - correspond to analytic functions from ℍ - {0} to ℍ.

John Baez (Aug 10 2025 at 08:28):

Now back to the main track:

  • The Kepler Problem (part 10).

The poet Blake wrote that you can see a world in a grain of sand. But even better, you can see a universe in an atom! Here we'll see that bound states of hydrogen atom correspond to states of a massless chiral spin-½ particle in the Einstein universe R×S3\mathbb{R} \times S^3R×S3. The rotational symmetries of the Einstein universe correspond to symmetries of the hydrogen atom. The energy eigenstates of the massless spin-½ particle correspond in a one-to-one way to energy eigenstates of the hydrogen atom.

We need to use a chiral spin-½ particle, i.e. one with handedness, and I arbitrarily choose to use a left-handed one. These obey a version of the Dirac equation called the Weyl equation (yes, Hermann Weyl!), so I explain that.

The tricky part was noticed already by Dirac in a related context: the Weyl equation has solutions of arbitraily large negative energy! I carry out the accept way to solve this problem: to modify the complex structure on the Hilbert space of solutions, multiplying it by −1-1−1 on the negative-frequency solutions of the Weyl equation. This is an updated version of Dirac’s original idea of treating antiparticles as ‘holes in the sea of negative-energy particles,’ or the later and still popular idea of switching annihilation and creation operators for negative-frequency solutions.

John Baez (Aug 10 2025 at 08:46):

  • The Kepler problem (part 11).

In Part 10 we saw that, loosely speaking, the theory of a hydrogen atom is equivalent to the theory of a massless left-handed spin-½ particle in the Einstein universe - a static universe where space is a 3-sphere. Today we'll 'second quantize’ both of these equivalent theories and get new theories that again are equivalent.

'Second quantization' is a funny term. It's a completely systematic way to get a new quantum theory from an old one. When you second quantize the theory of some particle, you get a theory that describes noninteracting collections of that kind of particle. So, when we second quantize the theory of a hydrogen atom, we get a theory that describes collections of electrons orbiting the nucleus - but in a simplified way, where the electrons do not interact. When we second quantize the theory of a massless left-handed spin-½ particle in the Einstein universe, we get a theory of noninteracting collections of such particles. This is called a 'free quantum field theory': it's the easiest sort of quantum field theory to understand.

So, there's a relationship between multi-electron atoms and a free quantum field theory on the Einstein universe!

John Baez (Aug 12 2025 at 17:21):

Kevin Coulembier now claims to have proved most of the conjectures Todd Trimble and I posed regarding categories of group representations at the end of Tannaka reconstructoin and the monoid of matrices. He has a new version of his paper on this:

  • Kevin Coulembier, Invertible exterior powers.

When I discussed an earlier version of his paper here, @Mike Shulman raised an objection. I'm pleased to say that now Coulembier has dealt with that objection. I still haven't checked his arguments (which use a lot of results by Deligne, etc.) but I'm optimistic.

I still hope there's an easier more 'tautologous' way to prove our conjectures... but I don't know!

Mike Shulman (Aug 12 2025 at 18:00):

FWIW, I didn't view my comment as an "objection" in the sense that I thought it made the results wrong. I was just observing that in general there's no abstract reason to expect free objects with a negative property to exist, so that when they do exist (as they do, apparently, in this case), it's generally because the negative condition is automatic or can be rephrased positively -- which is what Coulembier has now done.

John Baez (Aug 12 2025 at 19:57):

Right. I didn't say it made the results wrong.

John Baez (Aug 14 2025 at 17:45):

I blogged about this week's big meeting of UK-funded category theorists and software engineers working on "safeguarded AI":

  • Safeguarded AI meeting.

This is a big deal regardless of whether it accomplishes anything, because I don't know of any other project that tried to get lots of category theorists working to solve a real-world problem!

Joe Moeller (Aug 18 2025 at 18:41):

I've used Lulu before and found it easy to work with. I only used it to get myself a physical copy of a book I couldn't find physical copies of otherwise, which is certainly not the intended use.

Mike Shulman (Aug 18 2025 at 19:52):

The HoTT Book is self-published on Lulu. As far as I know we've never had any problems.

John Baez (Aug 18 2025 at 21:03):

Thanks!

John Baez (Aug 21 2025 at 08:56):

I'm visiting Joachim Kock this week. He's been thinking a lot about Loeb's sets with a negative number of elements and other attempts to generalize the concept of cardinality.

Loeb's main focus is generalizing the binomial coefficients (nk)\binom{n}{k}(kn​) to all n,k∈Z.n, k \in \mathbb{Z}.n,k∈Z. There's a unique way to do it by analytic continuation, and the result is always an integer, but the combinatorial interpretation of this integer seems to break up into 5 different cases, and it's hard to get a clear unified understanding of all cases. Loeb has an approach, but I find it mysterious.

Joachim told me that Maxime Ramzy has some big results on homotopy cardinality, which are not yet published. Remember that homotopy cardinality assigns a nonnegative rational number to any tame homotopy type - a homotopy type with finitely many connected components, each of which has only finitely many nonzero homotopy groups, all of which are finite groups. The formula for homotopy cardinality is here. Ramzy has uniquely characterized homotopy cardinality given a few axioms including

  1. invariance under weak homotopy equivalence,
  2. additivity under finite coproducts and
  3. multiplicativity under fibrations (if F→E→BF \to E \to BF→E→B is a fibration with connected base BBB and fiber FFF, then the cardinality of EEE is the cardinality of BBB times the cardinality of FFF).

These may be the only axioms needed - I forget. But the cool part is that these axioms (whatever they are) supposedly characterize homotopy cardinality, not only among invariants valued in nonnegative rationals, but among invariants valued in commutative rigs, or even (∞,1)(\infty,1)(∞,1)-versions of commutative rigs. I would really like to see these results.

I have long been interested in unifying homotopy cardinality (which takes nonegative fractional values) and Euler characteristic (which can take negative integral values), despite a no-go theorem about this. Joachim told me that Mathieu Anel has written a paper constructing a category for which a concept of cardinality can be defined that unifies these two concepts.

I don't think it can be this paper:

  • Mathieu Anel, The category of pi-finite spaces.

since that category is a perfect home for homotopy cardinality, but not Euler characteristic.

David Corfield (Aug 21 2025 at 09:12):

Did you ever take a look at

  • Andrés Ortiz-Muñoz, Homotopical Entropy?

Seemed right up your street with entropy and homotopy cardinality combined.

John Baez (Aug 21 2025 at 09:24):

Hmm, that could be interesting! Too bad there are no examples (except ordinary Shannon entropy).

John Baez (Aug 21 2025 at 10:40):

But as usual, one can complain about lack of examples or take advantage of the opportunity to be the first to invent an interesting example!

David Michael Roberts (Aug 21 2025 at 12:04):

Ramzi, not Ramzy :upside_down:

Chris Grossack (she/they) (Aug 21 2025 at 15:29):

Have you seen this (fairly) recent paper of Yanovski which uses Morava K Theory to unify homotopy cardinality and euler characteristic? *Homotopy Cardinality via Extrapolation of Morava-Euler Characteristics*

John Baez (Aug 21 2025 at 16:11):

Yes, that's the second of two papers I know that present a no-go theorem saying how naive attempts to unify homotopy cardinality and Euler characteristic must fail, and then tries to work around it. The first is

  • John D. Berman, Euler characteristic and homotopy cardinality.

Later Yanofski emailed me saying:

Regarding Berman's paper, I was informed that it has a mistake by himself, but he did not elaborate; that's why it was never published (and removed from his homepage). I have also been told by someone else, who checked it carefully, that it has several very essential problems, and it is far from clear that the main statement should even be true. 

The negative result, that there is no integral (i.e. not p-local) additive extension of the homotopy cardinality is easy. The smash product of the classifying spaces BCpBC_pBCp​ and BCqBC_qBCq​ is contractible but additivity forces the value 1/p +1/q +1/pq1/p + 1/q + 1/pq1/p +1/q +1/pq, which is of course different from 111. I have a vague intuition that there should be an integral version not for spaces but for some variant of "orbispaces" with finite abelian stabilizers. This is very much related to Lurie's work on tempered cohomology (elliptic cohomology III).
